actions: Induces sweating to release the exterior
Disseminates Lung qi and calms wheezing
indications: Exterior excess wind-cold pattern: aversion to cold, fever, headache, body aches, absence of sweating, wheezing.
mnemonic: Ma Gui Xing Gan — 'Ma's GUI (computer interface) is APRICOT-flavored and SWEET': Ma Huang opens the exterior, Gui Zhi warms the channels, Xing Ren descends Lung qi, Gan Cao harmonizes. No sweat + tight pulse = excess cold locked tight.
board_pearl: Ma Huang Tang is for wind-cold EXCESS with NO SWEATING — the key differentiator from Gui Zhi Tang (which treats deficiency with sweating).
formula_math: Ma Huang Tang − Ma Huang + Shao Yao = Gui Zhi Tang (swap from opening pores to harmonizing Ying/Wei). Ma Huang Tang + Shi Gao + more Xing Ren − Gui Zhi = Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang (interior heat).

key_modifications.modification: Add Qiang Huo and Du Huo
key_modifications.for: Pronounced body aches and pain

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Zhu
key_modifications.for: Exterior cold with dampness (Ma Huang Jia Zhu Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Xi Xin and Ban Xia
key_modifications.for: Copious thin white sputum from cold-fluid in the Lungs

comparison_formulas: Gui Zhi Tang: wind-cold DEFICIENCY with sweating vs. Ma Huang Tang for EXCESS without sweating
Da Qing Long Tang: wind-cold exterior excess with interior heat — adds Shi Gao to Ma Huang Tang base
Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang: interior heat with wheezing — replaces Gui Zhi with Shi Gao
clinical_pearls: The absence of sweating is the cardinal indicator for choosing Ma Huang Tang over Gui Zhi Tang.
Floating-tight pulse reflects the struggle between exterior pathogen (floating) and cold constriction (tight).
Ma Huang and Gui Zhi together strongly open the exterior — this is the most diaphoretic of the release exterior formulas.
contraindications: Patients with qi or blood deficiency
Caution in patients with hypertension or cardiac conditions due to Ma Huang

actions: Releases the exterior and expels wind-cold
Harmonizes the Ying (nutritive) and Wei (defensive) qi
indications: Wind-cold exterior deficiency pattern: fever, headache, aversion to wind, spontaneous sweating, nasal congestion, dry heaves.
pulse: Floating and moderate (huan) or floating and weak
mnemonic: GUI SHAOs at GINGER DATE parties with LICORICE dessert: Gui Zhi + Bai Shao harmonize Ying/Wei like a perfect couple. Sweating already present = deficiency, so harmonize don't blast open.
board_pearl: Gui Zhi Tang treats wind-cold with SPONTANEOUS SWEATING (exterior deficiency) by harmonizing Ying and Wei qi — the equal doses of Gui Zhi and Bai Shao are the hallmark pairing.
formula_math: Gui Zhi Tang + Ma Huang − Bai Shao = Ma Huang Tang. Gui Zhi Tang + Ge Gen = Ge Gen Tang (stiff neck). Gui Zhi Tang + Long Gu, Mu Li = Gui Zhi Jia Long Gu Mu Li Tang (seminal emission).

key_modifications.modification: Add Ge Gen (Radix Puerariae)
key_modifications.for: Stiff neck from wind-cold (Ge Gen Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Hou Po and Xing Ren
key_modifications.for: Exterior wind-cold with wheezing and chest fullness

key_modifications.modification: Double Bai Shao, add Yi Tang (maltose)
key_modifications.for: Abdominal pain with exterior pattern (Xiao Jian Zhong Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Fu Zi (Radix Aconiti Lateralis Praeparata)
key_modifications.for: Exterior wind-cold with yang deficiency (Gui Zhi Jia Fu Zi Tang)

comparison_formulas: Ma Huang Tang: wind-cold EXCESS with no sweating — stronger diaphoresis
Xiao Jian Zhong Tang: Gui Zhi Tang with doubled Bai Shao + Yi Tang shifts focus to interior Middle Jiao deficiency cold
Ge Gen Tang: Gui Zhi Tang + Ge Gen + Ma Huang for stiff neck/upper back from wind-cold
clinical_pearls: After taking the decoction, patient should eat a bowl of hot rice porridge and cover up to promote mild sweating — the formula assists rather than forces diaphoresis.
The Gui Zhi-Bai Shao pair is foundational: Gui Zhi releases the exterior (Wei qi), Bai Shao nourishes the interior (Ying qi) — their equal pairing harmonizes these two layers.
This is the most commonly modified formula in the Shang Han Lun, forming the basis for dozens of derivative formulas.
contraindications: Exterior excess (no sweating) — use Ma Huang Tang instead
Patients with vomiting of blood or epistaxis from heat

actions: Releases the exterior and disperses cold
Transforms thin mucus (Yin) and warms the Lungs
Directs rebellious qi downward and arrests wheezing
indications: Exterior wind-cold with interior congested thin mucus (tan yin): aversion to cold, fever, no sweating, wheezing, cough with copious thin white watery sputum, difficulty breathing when lying down, a sensation of heaviness in the body, edema of the extremities.
pulse: Floating and tight, or floating and slippery
mnemonic: The SMALL BLUE DRAGON has 8 legs: Ma-Gui (exterior pair), Gan-Xi (warming pair), Wu-Bai (astringent pair), Ban-Gan (harmonize pair). Cold outside + watery phlegm inside = the Dragon dries the flood.
board_pearl: Xiao Qing Long Tang is THE formula for wind-cold exterior with COPIOUS THIN WHITE WATERY SPUTUM (tan yin/cold fluid in the Lungs) — the combination of exterior-releasing and interior-warming herbs addresses both layers simultaneously.
formula_math: Ma Huang Tang base (Ma Huang + Gui Zhi + Gan Cao) + warming/drying herbs (Gan Jiang, Xi Xin, Ban Xia) + astringent/restraining herbs (Wu Wei Zi, Bai Shao) − Xing Ren.

key_modifications.modification: Add Shi Gao
key_modifications.for: Irritability with interior heat signs developing (Xiao Qing Long Jia Shi Gao Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Remove Ma Huang, add Dang Gui and Huang Qi
key_modifications.for: Weak constitution with same presentation

key_modifications.modification: Add Su Zi and Lai Fu Zi
key_modifications.for: More pronounced wheezing and difficulty breathing

comparison_formulas: Ma Huang Tang: exterior wind-cold excess but WITHOUT interior thin mucus congestion
Da Qing Long Tang: exterior cold with interior HEAT (irritability, thirst) vs. Xiao Qing Long's interior COLD fluids
Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang: interior thin mucus without exterior pattern — purely interior formula
Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang: chronic phlegm-wheezing from Kidney not grasping qi — no exterior pattern
clinical_pearls: The pairing of Xi Xin + Wu Wei Zi is critical: Xi Xin disperses and warms while Wu Wei Zi astringes and contains — they prevent each other's excess.
The copious, thin, white, watery sputum is the hallmark distinguishing it from formulas for thick yellow phlegm (heat) or dry cough (yin deficiency).
This formula is the classical basis for treating allergic rhinitis and bronchial asthma triggered by cold.
contraindications: Cough from Lung yin deficiency (dry cough, scanty sputum)
Patients with significant qi or yin deficiency
Caution with long-term use due to the many warm, drying herbs

actions: Strongly induces sweating to release the exterior
indications: Exterior wind-cold excess with interior heat: fever, aversion to cold, no sweating, irritability, body aches, a sensation of heaviness, general discomfort.
tongue: Thin white or slightly yellow coating
mnemonic: The BIG BLUE DRAGON breathes GYPSUM FIRE: Ma Huang Tang (Ma-Gui-Xing-Gan) + Shi Gao for interior heat + Jiang/Zao to protect the Stomach. Cold exterior locked tight, but IRRITABILITY betrays fire within.
board_pearl: Da Qing Long Tang = Ma Huang Tang + Shi Gao (+ Sheng Jiang, Da Zao) — the key distinguishing symptom is IRRITABILITY from interior heat trapped by the unsweated exterior cold.
formula_math: Ma Huang Tang + Shi Gao + Sheng Jiang + Da Zao = Da Qing Long Tang. Compare: Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang focuses on interior heat with less exterior (drops Gui Zhi entirely).

key_modifications.modification: Reduce Ma Huang dosage
key_modifications.for: Weaker patients to prevent excessive sweating

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: More pronounced interior heat with bitter taste

key_modifications.modification: Add Ban Xia
key_modifications.for: Nausea or vomiting accompanying the exterior pattern

comparison_formulas: Xiao Qing Long Tang: exterior cold with interior COLD thin mucus vs. Da Qing Long's interior HEAT (irritability)
Ma Huang Tang: exterior cold excess without interior heat (no irritability)
Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang: interior heat predominates with wheezing — no Gui Zhi, less exterior focus
clinical_pearls: IRRITABILITY (fan zao) is the essential symptom differentiating Da Qing Long Tang from Ma Huang Tang — it signals interior heat.
The high dose of Ma Huang (12g) combined with Shi Gao allows strong exterior release while clearing interior heat — the cold mineral prevents the warm herbs from stoking internal fire.
Zhang Zhongjing warned that if the patient has already sweated, this formula is contraindicated — overcooking the exterior can collapse yang and cause adverse reactions.
contraindications: Patients who are already sweating (risk of yang collapse)
Caution in hypertension or heart conditions due to high-dose Ma Huang

actions: Induces sweating to release the exterior
indications: External contraction of wind-cold-dampness with interior heat: severe headache, generalized body aches and pain, aversion to cold, no sweating, slight bitter taste in the mouth, mild thirst.
mnemonic: NINE warriors QIANG-charge into battle: QIANG Huo leads FANG-wind defense and CANG-grey troops (exterior team), while XI-XIN, CHUAN-XIONG, BAI-ZHI fight pain (headache team), and SHENG DI + HUANG QIN cool the rear (prevent interior heat), GANCAo supplies rations.
board_pearl: Jiu Wei Qiang Huo Tang uniquely adds Huang Qin and Sheng Di to a wind-cold-damp releasing formula — this protects against interior heat generated by the many warm acrid herbs, embodying the 'fen jing lun zhi' (treatment by channel differentiation) principle.
formula_math: Conceptually: wind-cold-damp exterior herbs (Qiang Huo, Fang Feng, Cang Zhu, Xi Xin, Chuan Xiong, Bai Zhi) + cooling balance (Huang Qin, Sheng Di) + harmonizer (Gan Cao). A later-dynasty alternative to Shang Han Lun formulas.

key_modifications.modification: Remove Sheng Di and Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: Pure wind-cold-dampness without any interior heat signs

key_modifications.modification: Add Ge Gen and Chai Hu
key_modifications.for: Headache and neck stiffness involving Yangming and Shaoyang channels

key_modifications.modification: Increase Cang Zhu and add Yi Yi Ren
key_modifications.for: Pronounced dampness with heavy body and limb edema

comparison_formulas: Ma Huang Tang: pure wind-cold excess without dampness component
Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang: focuses on wind-dampness in the exterior causing joint pain — less interior heat concern
Chai Ge Jie Ji Tang: exterior wind-cold transitioning to interior heat at a later stage with more interior heat signs
clinical_pearls: Zhang Yuansu designed this formula with the principle that each herb targets a specific channel: Qiang Huo (Taiyang), Bai Zhi (Yangming), Chuan Xiong (Jueyin/Shaoyang), Xi Xin (Shaoyin), etc.
The inclusion of Huang Qin and Sheng Di Huang is a safeguard — they prevent the many warm, drying, acrid herbs from damaging yin or generating internal heat.
This formula is particularly useful for the body aches and heavy sensation of wind-cold-dampness, such as seen in early-stage flu with prominent myalgias in damp weather.

indications: Early-stage wind-heat or warm-febrile disease (wei level): fever, slight or no aversion to cold, headache, thirst, sore throat, cough.
tongue: Red tip, thin white or thin yellow coating
mnemonic: YIN (silver) QIAO (forsythia) is GOLDEN: 'Gold-Silver flowers with Forsythia, Mint and Burdock clear the throat-ia; Jie Geng opens, Dou Chi releases, Reed root and Bamboo cool heat's creases, Jing Jie pushes out, Gan Cao eases.' Sore throat + fever = wind-heat #1.
board_pearl: Yin Qiao San is THE first-line formula for early-stage wind-heat or warm-febrile disease (wei level) — fever, sore throat, floating-rapid pulse. It is to wind-heat what Ma Huang Tang is to wind-cold.
formula_math: Yin Qiao San vs. Sang Ju Yin: Yin Qiao San is stronger for heat-toxin and sore throat; Sang Ju Yin is milder and focuses on cough. Yin Qiao San − (Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Niu Bang Zi, Dan Zhu Ye, Lu Gen) + (Sang Ye, Ju Hua, Xing Ren) ≈ Sang Ju Yin.

key_modifications.modification: Add Da Qing Ye and Ban Lan Gen
key_modifications.for: Pronounced sore throat with swelling and heat-toxin

key_modifications.modification: Add Shi Gao and Zhi Mu
key_modifications.for: High fever indicating heat penetrating to the qi level

key_modifications.modification: Add Xuan Shen and Ma Bo
key_modifications.for: Severe swollen painful throat (approaching lan hou sha)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: Pronounced upper respiratory heat with yellow nasal discharge

comparison_formulas: Sang Ju Yin: milder wind-heat formula emphasizing COUGH over sore throat — lighter, more Lung-focused
Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang: interior Lung heat with wheezing — deeper heat level than Yin Qiao San
Bai Hu Tang: qi-level heat (four bigs) — Yin Qiao San is wei-level, if heat deepens you shift to Bai Hu Tang
clinical_pearls: Wu Jutong specified this formula should be taken frequently in small doses throughout the day, not boiled long — light decoction preserves the aromatic, exterior-releasing properties.
Bo He (mint) is added near the end of decoction (hou xia) to preserve its volatile oils.

Jing Jie Sui and Dan Dou Chi are technically warm/acrid herbs used here to gently vent the exterior — they work with rather than against the cool formula because their role is directional (outward-pushing), not warming.
contraindications: Later-stage warm-febrile disease where heat has entered the qi, ying, or blood levels
Early rashes from warm-febrile disease requiring blood-level cooling

actions: Disperses wind-heat with light, cool herbs
Stops cough by ventilating and moistening the Lungs
indications: Early-stage wind-heat with predominantly Lung symptoms: cough (may be slight), slight fever, slight thirst. This is a milder wind-heat presentation than Yin Qiao San patterns.
tongue: Thin white coating, tip may be slightly red
mnemonic: SANG (mulberry) leaves on a JU (chrysanthemum) bush — a LIGHT garden remedy: the lightest wind-heat formula, focused on COUGH. 'When the breeze is warm but the cough is the chief, pick Mulberry and Mum for gentle relief.'
board_pearl: Sang Ju Yin is the MILDER wind-heat formula for when COUGH predominates over sore throat — compare to Yin Qiao San which is stronger and targets sore throat and heat-toxin.
formula_math: Sang Ju Yin + (Jin Yin Hua, Niu Bang Zi, Dan Dou Chi, Jing Jie Sui, Dan Zhu Ye) − (Sang Ye, Ju Hua, Xing Ren) + increased Lu Gen and Lian Qiao doses ≈ Yin Qiao San.

key_modifications.modification: Add Shi Gao and Zhi Mu
key_modifications.for: Cough with high fever indicating qi-level heat

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin and Zhi Zi
key_modifications.for: More pronounced heat signs with yellow sputum

key_modifications.modification: Add Bei Mu (Chuan Bei Mu) and Gua Lou Pi
key_modifications.for: Persistent cough with thicker sputum that is difficult to expectorate

comparison_formulas: Yin Qiao San: stronger wind-heat formula emphasizing SORE THROAT and heat-toxin over cough
Xing Su San: wind-cool-dryness with cough — acrid-warm and moistening vs. Sang Ju Yin's acrid-cool approach
Sang Xing Tang: warm-dryness injuring the Lungs — focuses on dryness rather than wind-heat
clinical_pearls: Wu Jutong considered this the lightest formula in the 'upper burner' chapter of the Wen Bing Tiao Bian — like Yin Qiao San it should be lightly decocted.
Sang Ye and Ju Hua both enter the Lung and Liver channels — they disperse wind-heat from the head and eyes while ventilating the Lungs.
The inclusion of Xing Ren (which descends Lung qi) differentiates this from Yin Qiao San and makes it specifically suited for cough as the dominant complaint.
contraindications: Cough due to Lung dryness or yin deficiency
Severe exterior heat-toxin (use Yin Qiao San or stronger formulas instead)

english_name: Ephedra, Apricot Kernel, Gypsum, and Licorice Decoction
actions: Facilitates the flow of Lung qi
indications: Heat lodged in the Lungs: fever, cough, wheezing, labored breathing, nasal flaring, thirst, sweating or no sweating. Can present with or without residual exterior signs.
mnemonic: Ma-Xing-Shi-Gan: 'MASsive STONE (Shi Gao at 4x dose) crushes lung heat while APRICOT (Xing Ren) settles wheezing and LICORICE sweetens the deal.' The Shi Gao-to-Ma Huang ratio (4:1) flips Ma Huang from diaphoretic to bronchodilator.
board_pearl: In Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang, the large dose of Shi Gao (4x the Ma Huang dose) redirects Ma Huang from inducing sweating to ventilating the Lungs and stopping wheezing — sweating may or may not be present, which distinguishes it from exterior-focused formulas.
formula_math: Ma Huang Tang − Gui Zhi + Shi Gao = Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang. The removal of Gui Zhi (warming exterior opener) and addition of Shi Gao (cold, clears interior heat) transforms the formula's strategy from releasing the exterior to clearing Lung heat.

key_modifications.modification: Add Sang Bai Pi and Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: More severe Lung heat with yellow thick sputum

key_modifications.modification: Add Yu Xing Cao
key_modifications.for: Lung abscess or Lung heat with purulent sputum

key_modifications.modification: Add Ting Li Zi
key_modifications.for: Severe wheezing with phlegm-heat congesting the Lungs

comparison_formulas: Ma Huang Tang: both contain Ma Huang + Xing Ren, but Ma Huang Tang warms (Gui Zhi) while Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang clears heat (Shi Gao)
Da Qing Long Tang: exterior cold excess WITH interior heat — still has Gui Zhi for exterior release; Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang focuses on interior Lung heat
Yue Bi Tang: edema from wind-water with heat — also uses Ma Huang + Shi Gao but for fluid regulation, not Lung heat
Bai Hu Tang: qi-level heat (four bigs) without Lung qi obstruction — no wheezing focus
clinical_pearls: This formula treats Lung heat regardless of whether the patient is sweating — the presence or absence of sweating does NOT determine its use, unlike Ma Huang Tang.
When combined with Shi Gao at a 4:1 ratio, Ma Huang shifts from a diaphoretic to a Lung-ventilating bronchodilator — this pharmacological shift is a high-yield exam concept.
Commonly used in modern practice for pneumonia, bronchitis, and asthma with heat signs — one of the most frequently prescribed classical formulas in Chinese hospitals.
contraindications: Cough or wheezing from deficiency (Lung qi or Kidney yang deficiency)

english_name: Bupleurum and Kudzu Decoction to Release the Muscle Layer
actions: Releases the exterior from the muscle layer
indications: Exterior wind-cold that is beginning to transform into interior heat (between Taiyang and Yangming stages): increasing fever, decreasing but still present chills, headache, stiffness of the extremities, orbital and eye pain, dry nasal passages, irritability, insomnia.
mnemonic: CHAI-GE unlocks the MUSCLE LAYER: Chai Hu pivots Shaoyang + Ge Gen releases Yangming muscles. Think 'Chai-Ge with Qiang-Bai for the exterior, Qin-Gao for the heat interior, Shao-Jie-Jiang-Zao-Cao for the supporting floor.' Cold changing to heat = transitional formula.
board_pearl: Chai Ge Jie Ji Tang treats the TRANSITIONAL stage where wind-cold is converting to interior heat — the patient still has chills (exterior not fully resolved) but has increasing fever and early interior heat signs like orbital pain, irritability, and dry nose.
formula_math: Combines exterior-releasing herbs from Taiyang formulas (Qiang Huo, Bai Zhi, Ge Gen) + Shaoyang pivot herb (Chai Hu) + interior heat-clearing (Huang Qin, Shi Gao) + harmonizing (Bai Shao, Jie Geng, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao, Gan Cao). A Taiyang-Yangming bridging formula.

key_modifications.modification: Increase Shi Gao and add Zhi Mu
key_modifications.for: Higher fever with more pronounced interior heat (closer to Yangming stage)

key_modifications.modification: Add Chuan Xiong
key_modifications.for: More severe headache

key_modifications.modification: Remove Qiang Huo and Bai Zhi, increase Chai Hu
key_modifications.for: Pattern shifting more toward Shaoyang with alternating chills and fever

comparison_formulas: Xiao Chai Hu Tang: Shaoyang stage (alternating chills/fever, bitter taste, hypochondriac distension) — Chai Ge Jie Ji Tang still has a stronger exterior component
Jiu Wei Qiang Huo Tang: exterior wind-cold-dampness with mild interior heat — earlier stage, more exterior-dominant
Ge Gen Tang: pure exterior wind-cold with stiff neck — no interior heat component
Da Qing Long Tang: exterior cold with interior heat but at an earlier stage with no sweating — stronger exterior-releasing action
clinical_pearls: This formula was designed by Tao Hua for the specific transitional moment when a cold is 'turning' — the patient still feels cold but is becoming more feverish, and early heat signs like eye pain and irritability are emerging.
Ge Gen both releases the muscle layer and generates fluids — it addresses the stiffness of the neck/back while counteracting the drying effect of the heat.

The combination of Chai Hu + Ge Gen is the signature: Chai Hu works the Shaoyang pivot while Ge Gen releases the Yangming muscle layer — together they address the exact anatomical and channel location of the transitional pathology.
contraindications: Pure interior heat (qi-level or deeper) without exterior signs
Early-stage exterior wind-cold without any heat transformation

indications: Yangming channel heat or qi-level heat with the four bigs: great fever, great sweating, great thirst with desire for cold drinks, and great (surging/flooding) pulse.
pulse: Surging, big (hong da), forceful, or slippery and rapid
mnemonic: The White TIGER ROAMS with FOUR BIGS — Stone (Shi Gao) cold knowledge (Zhi Mu) with sweet (Gan Cao) rice (Geng Mi) to tame yangming fire.
board_pearl: Bai Hu Tang is THE formula for qi-level heat with the four bigs (da re, da han, da ke, da mai).
formula_math: Bai Hu Tang + Ren Shen = Bai Hu Jia Ren Shen Tang (for qi-level heat with qi/fluid damage); Bai Hu Tang + Gui Zhi = Bai Hu Jia Gui Zhi Tang (for heat bi syndrome)

key_modifications.modification: Add Ren Shen 9g
key_modifications.for: Concurrent qi and fluid damage with fatigue

key_modifications.modification: Add Cang Zhu 6-9g
key_modifications.for: Damp-heat with body aches and heaviness (Bai Hu Jia Cang Zhu Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Gui Zhi 9g
key_modifications.for: Heat bi with joint pain, swelling, and fever

comparison_formulas: Zhu Ye Shi Gao Tang: post-febrile qi and yin damage (milder heat, more deficiency)
Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang: lung heat with cough and wheezing (heat constrained in lungs)
Qing Ying Tang: heat has entered the nutritive/ying level (deeper than qi level)
clinical_pearls: The four bigs distinguish yangming channel heat from yangming organ (fu) heat which presents with constipation and abdominal fullness.
Although there is great sweating, do NOT use astringent herbs — the sweating is caused by heat forcing fluids outward; clear the heat and sweating resolves.
Contraindicated for true cold with false heat presentations — always confirm the pulse is truly forceful, not feeble on pressure.
contraindications: True cold with false heat (yang deficiency with floating yang)
Fever without sweating (exterior pattern not yet resolved)
Blood deficiency heat (pulse thin and rapid, not flooding)

indications: Lingering qi-level heat in the aftermath of a febrile disease with damage to qi and fluids. Low-grade fever, irritability, thirst, stifling sensation in the chest, nausea or vomiting, reduced appetite.
mnemonic: After the TIGER (Bai Hu) battle, BAMBOO LEAVES (Zhu Ye) and STONE (Shi Gao) remain — add a GINSENG-OPHIOPOGON rescue squad with RICE and PINELLIA to settle the depleted stomach.
board_pearl: Zhu Ye Shi Gao Tang treats the aftermath of febrile disease — residual heat with qi and yin damage — unlike Bai Hu Tang which addresses the acute stage.
formula_math: Bai Hu Tang (remove Zhi Mu) + Ren Shen + Mai Men Dong + Dan Zhu Ye + Ban Xia = Zhu Ye Shi Gao Tang

key_modifications.modification: Increase Mai Men Dong and Ren Shen
key_modifications.for: Severe fluid and qi damage

key_modifications.modification: Remove Ban Xia, add Tian Hua Fen
key_modifications.for: Severe thirst without nausea (more fluid damage)

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhu Ru 9g
key_modifications.for: Prominent nausea and vomiting from stomach heat

comparison_formulas: Bai Hu Tang: acute qi-level heat with four bigs, no deficiency of qi or yin
Bai Hu Jia Ren Shen Tang: qi-level heat still vigorous but with concurrent qi/fluid damage
Sha Shen Mai Dong Tang: pure yin deficiency of lung and stomach, no residual heat pathogen
clinical_pearls: Ban Xia is warm and drying — seemingly contradictory — but it directs rebellious stomach qi downward to stop vomiting and prevents the cloying yin tonics from causing stagnation.
Key differentiator from Bai Hu Tang: the heat here is residual (low-grade) not blazing, and the patient shows clear signs of qi and yin depletion.
Think of this formula for patients recovering from high fevers or infections who still feel unwell with mild fever, thirst, poor appetite, and fatigue.
contraindications: Vigorous heat without qi or yin damage (use Bai Hu Tang instead)
Early-stage exterior febrile disease where the pathogen has not been resolved

composition.herb: Xi Jiao (Rhinocerotis Cornu) [substituted with Shui Niu Jiao (Bubali Cornu)]

indications: Heat entering the blood level causing: hematemesis, epistaxis, bloody stool, bloody urine (any bleeding with heat); or heat toxin with maculae (dark purple skin eruptions); or heat disturbing the Shen causing manic behavior, delirium, impaired consciousness.
mnemonic: RHINO HORNS (Xi Jiao) DIG (Di Huang) RED PEONY (Chi Shao) TREE BARK (Mu Dan Pi) — four herbs cool blood-level heat that makes you bleed and go crazy.
board_pearl: Xi Jiao Di Huang Tang is THE blood-level heat formula: crimson tongue + bleeding + possible manic delirium; it simultaneously cools blood AND moves blood to prevent stasis from congealed heat.
formula_math: Xi Jiao Di Huang Tang is the blood-level counterpart of Qing Ying Tang (nutritive level); Qing Ying Tang + (remove Huang Lian, Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Dan Zhu Ye, Mai Dong, Xuan Shen) → Xi Jiao Di Huang Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Da Huang 9g, Huang Qin 9g
key_modifications.for: Blood-level heat with concurrent yangming constipation

key_modifications.modification: Add San Qi 3g, Ce Bai Ye 9g, He Ye 9g
key_modifications.for: Severe bleeding (hematemesis, epistaxis)

key_modifications.modification: Add Zi Cao 9g, Da Qing Ye 15g
key_modifications.for: Pronounced maculae or purpura from heat toxin

comparison_formulas: Qing Ying Tang: heat in the nutritive (ying) level — earlier/lighter stage, tongue scarlet but less bleeding
Huang Lian Jie Du Tang: fire toxin at qi level affecting all three burners, no bleeding
Shi Hui San: purely for bleeding from heat above, no manic symptoms
clinical_pearls: The deep crimson (jiang se) tongue is the hallmark — darker than the scarlet tongue of ying-level heat.
Both Chi Shao and Mu Dan Pi cool blood AND invigorate blood. This prevents heat from congealing the blood into stasis, which would create a vicious cycle of heat + stasis generating more heat.
Xi Jiao (rhinoceros horn) is banned; Shui Niu Jiao (water buffalo horn) is the standard substitute but used at 10x the dosage (30g vs. 3g).
This formula treats the syndrome 're ru xue fen' — heat entering the blood division — the deepest of the four levels in Wen Bing theory.
contraindications: Spleen yang deficiency with bleeding (bleeding from failure to control blood, not heat)
Yin deficiency without true heat toxin in the blood level
Pregnancy (blood-moving herbs Chi Shao and Mu Dan Pi)

composition.herb: Xi Jiao (Rhinocerotis Cornu) [substituted with Shui Niu Jiao (Bubali Cornu)]

actions: Vents heat from the nutritive level to the qi level
indications: Heat entering the nutritive (ying) level: fever worse at night, severe irritability and restlessness, possible delirium, thirst with no strong desire to drink, faint erythema or maculae beginning to appear.
mnemonic: CLEAR the YING camp with a 9-herb army: RHINO and RAW EARTH (Sheng Di) lead, SCROPHULARIA-OPHIOPOGON nourish, COPTIS drains the heart, HONEYSUCKLE-FORSYTHIA vent heat outward, BAMBOO LEAVES guide, and RED SAGE (Dan Shen) keeps blood moving.
board_pearl: Qing Ying Tang treats nutritive (ying) level heat with the strategy of 'tou re zhuan qi' — venting heat from the ying level back out to the qi level (via Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Dan Zhu Ye).
formula_math: Contains Xi Jiao Di Huang Tang herbs (Xi Jiao, Sheng Di) + qi-level venting herbs (Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Dan Zhu Ye) + yin-nourishing (Xuan Shen, Mai Dong) + heart-clearing (Huang Lian) + blood-moving (Dan Shen)

key_modifications.modification: Add Chi Shao 9g, Mu Dan Pi 9g
key_modifications.for: Heat progressing into the blood level with bleeding or pronounced maculae (essentially combining with Xi Jiao Di Huang Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Increase Huang Lian, add Lian Zi Xin
key_modifications.for: Severe delirium and loss of consciousness from heat clouding the pericardium

key_modifications.modification: Add Zi Cao 9g, Da Qing Ye 15g
key_modifications.for: Pronounced maculae and skin eruptions

comparison_formulas: Xi Jiao Di Huang Tang: blood-level heat (deeper) — active bleeding, crimson tongue, possible mania
Bai Hu Tang: qi-level heat (shallower) — four bigs, no nighttime worsening
An Gong Niu Huang Wan: heat clouding the pericardium with coma — more severe consciousness disturbance
clinical_pearls: The key clinical distinction: fever worse at night indicates heat has sunk to the ying level (yin = night).
Thirst with no desire to drink (or only sipping) is characteristic of ying-level heat — unlike qi-level heat which has great thirst with desire for cold drinks.

The strategy of 'tou re zhuan qi' (venting heat back to the qi level) is unique to this formula — the light, ascending herbs (Jin Yin Hua, Lian Qiao, Dan Zhu Ye) give the pathogenic heat an exit route.
Dan Shen is included to prevent blood stasis — when heat enters the ying level, it can congeal the blood.
contraindications: Damp-heat patterns (the yin-nourishing herbs will worsen dampness)
Early-stage exterior wind-heat that has not yet entered the ying level

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat and Resolve Toxin
indications: Fire toxin in all three burners: high fever, irritability, restlessness, dry mouth and throat, insomnia, dark scanty urine. Can also present with sores, carbuncles, nosebleed, or skin eruptions from fire toxin.
mnemonic: THREE YELLOWS (Huang Lian, Huang Qin, Huang Bai) + GARDENIA drain fire from ALL THREE BURNERS top to bottom — Qin for upper, Lian for middle, Bai for lower, Zhi Zi clears heat via urination.
board_pearl: Huang Lian Jie Du Tang is the representative formula for draining fire toxin from all three burners simultaneously — three Huangs for three burners plus Zhi Zi to drain heat downward via urine.
formula_math: Huang Lian Jie Du Tang + Si Wu Tang = Wen Qing Yin (fire toxin with blood deficiency); Huang Lian Jie Du Tang is the 'bitter-cold clearing' counterpart to the 'pungent-cool releasing' approach of Yin Qiao San

key_modifications.modification: Add Da Huang 6g
key_modifications.for: Fire toxin with constipation

key_modifications.modification: Add Sheng Di Huang 15g, Mu Dan Pi 9g
key_modifications.for: Fire toxin with bleeding (hematemesis, epistaxis)

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Si Wu Tang (= Wen Qing Yin)
key_modifications.for: Fire toxin with concurrent blood deficiency

comparison_formulas: Long Dan Xie Gan Tang: drains fire specifically from the liver and gallbladder, not all three burners
Qing Wen Bai Du Yin: more severe fire toxin at qi and blood levels simultaneously, bigger formula
Xie Xin Tang (Da Huang Huang Lian Xie Xin Tang): drains heart and stomach fire with Da Huang, no Huang Bai or Zhi Zi
clinical_pearls: This is the archetypal 'bitter and cold' formula — pure draining with no supplementing. Use only for excess fire patterns.
Huang Lian drains heart and middle jiao fire, Huang Qin drains lung and upper jiao fire, Huang Bai drains kidney and lower jiao fire — hence 'three Huangs for three burners.'
Zhi Zi is the only herb that is not in the Huang (yellow) family — it clears heat from all three burners and directs it downward and out through the urine.
Long-term use or use in deficiency patients will damage the spleen and stomach yang.
contraindications: Yin deficiency with empty fire (no true excess fire)
Patients with cold constitutions or loose stools from spleen qi deficiency

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat and Resolve Toxin

composition.herb: Zi Bei Tian Kui (Begoniae Fimbristipulatae Herba)

indications: Fire toxin manifesting as boils, carbuncles, furuncles, and other localized hot sores that are red, swollen, hot, and painful. Fever and chills may accompany.
mnemonic: FIVE FLOWERS for FIVE POISONS: HONEYSUCKLE (Jin Yin Hua) leads WILD CHRYSANTHEMUM, DANDELION (Pu Gong Ying), VIOLET (Zi Hua Di Ding), and BEGONIA (Zi Bei Tian Kui) to detox hot sores.
board_pearl: Wu Wei Xiao Du Yin is THE formula for hot, red, swollen, painful boils and carbuncles caused by fire toxin — five heat-clearing, toxin-resolving herbs, most of which are flowers.

key_modifications.modification: Add Lian Qiao 9g, Chi Shao 9g
key_modifications.for: Severe redness and swelling with more pronounced heat toxin

key_modifications.modification: Add Zao Jiao Ci 6g, Chuan Shan Jia 6g
key_modifications.for: Abscess that has not yet burst to promote drainage

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi 15g, Dang Gui 9g
key_modifications.for: Sores in patients with underlying qi and blood deficiency (chronic non-healing)

comparison_formulas: Xian Fang Huo Ming Yin: more complex formula for sores that also addresses qi stagnation and blood stasis — more for deep abscesses
Huang Lian Jie Du Tang: systemic fire toxin in all three burners, not specifically targeting sores
Pu Ji Xiao Du Yin: fire toxin specifically in the head and face (big head plague)
clinical_pearls: Best used in the early stages of sores and carbuncles before pus has formed.
The formula is composed almost entirely of heat-clearing, toxin-resolving herbs with no supplementing herbs — appropriate only for excess patterns.
Jin Yin Hua as Jun at double dose underscores that it is the strongest toxin-resolving herb in the formula.
Classically taken with wine to enhance circulation and direct the herbs to the affected area.
contraindications: Yin-type sores (pale, flat, non-painful, chronic) from yang deficiency — use Yang He Tang instead
Sores that have already ulcerated with deficiency pattern

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat and Resolve Toxin

composition.herb: Dang Gui Wei (Angelicae sinensis Radicis Extremitas)

indications: Sores, carbuncles, boils in the early stage — red, swollen, hot, and painful — that have not yet suppurated. The sore is hard and deep-rooted.
tongue: Red with yellow or thin yellow coating
mnemonic: SUBLIME LIFE formula: HONEYSUCKLE clears toxin, FRANKINCENSE & MYRRH (Ru Xiang, Mo Yao) move blood, PANGOLIN SCALES & THORNS (Chuan Shan Jia, Zao Jiao Ci) penetrate and drain, DANG GUI WEI nourishes blood at the tail end — 13 herbs for deep abscesses.
board_pearl: Xian Fang Huo Ming Yin is the most comprehensive sore formula — it clears toxin, moves blood, breaks through accumulation, AND disperses swelling, making it ideal for deep-rooted abscesses in their early stage.
formula_math: More complex than Wu Wei Xiao Du Yin — adds blood-moving (Ru Xiang, Mo Yao, Dang Gui Wei, Chi Shao), penetrating (Chuan Shan Jia, Zao Jiao Ci), and dispersing (Fang Feng, Bai Zhi) strategies

key_modifications.modification: Remove Chuan Shan Jia (endangered species), substitute with Wang Bu Liu Xing 6g
key_modifications.for: Modern clinical practice (pangolin conservation)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi 15g
key_modifications.for: Sores with underlying qi deficiency to help generate flesh

key_modifications.modification: Add Lian Qiao 9g, Pu Gong Ying 15g
key_modifications.for: More severe heat toxin

comparison_formulas: Wu Wei Xiao Du Yin: simpler formula for superficial boils, purely clears heat toxin without blood-moving or penetrating herbs
Tuo Li Xiao Du Yin: for sores that have already suppurated with deficiency, more supplementing in nature
Nei Tuo Sheng Ji San: for later-stage sores with pus and deficiency
clinical_pearls: Best used in the EARLY stage before pus has formed — once pus has formed and drained, switch to supplementing formulas.
Dang Gui Wei (the tail of Dang Gui) is specifically chosen over whole Dang Gui because the tail is best at invigorating blood rather than tonifying.
Chuan Shan Jia (pangolin scales) and Zao Jiao Ci (gleditsia thorns) are the key 'penetrating' herbs that break through stagnation and drain pus — they can be compared to surgical lancing.
Classically taken with wine to enhance blood circulation.
contraindications: Yin-type sores (cold, pale, non-inflamed) from yang or qi deficiency
After the sore has already ruptured and drained (switch to tonifying approach)

english_name: Gentiana Decoction to Drain the Liver
category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat from Organs
actions: Drains excess fire from the liver and gallbladder
Clears and drains damp-heat from the lower jiao
indications: Liver/gallbladder excess fire flaring upward: headache, red eyes, bitter taste, irritability, hearing loss, swelling in ears. OR liver/gallbladder damp-heat pouring downward: foul-smelling leukorrhea, genital swelling/itching/pain, turbid or painful urination, damp-heat jaundice.
mnemonic: The DRAGON (Long Dan) drains the LIVER with a two-pronged attack: SCUTELLARIA and GARDENIA clear fire above, while THREE DRAINERS (Ze Xie, Mu Tong, Che Qian Zi) flush damp-heat below — SHENG DI and DANG GUI prevent the bitter-cold from damaging yin and blood.
board_pearl: Long Dan Xie Gan Tang treats BOTH liver fire flaring UP (red eyes, headache, tinnitus) AND liver damp-heat pouring DOWN (genital itching, leukorrhea, urinary issues) — the only formula that addresses both directions of liver pathology.
formula_math: Conceptually: Long Dan Cao (drain liver fire) + Huang Qin/Zhi Zi (clear heat) + Ze Xie/Mu Tong/Che Qian Zi (drain dampness) + Sheng Di/Dang Gui (protect yin/blood) + Chai Hu (guide to liver) + Gan Cao (harmonize)

key_modifications.modification: Add Xia Ku Cao 12g, Ju Hua 9g
key_modifications.for: Liver fire causing severe red eyes and headache

key_modifications.modification: Add Yi Yi Ren 15g, Huang Bai 9g
key_modifications.for: Prominent lower jiao damp-heat with genital itching or discharge

key_modifications.modification: Add Long Gu 15g, Mu Li 15g
key_modifications.for: Liver fire causing insomnia and severe irritability

key_modifications.modification: Replace Mu Tong with Deng Xin Cao 3g
key_modifications.for: Concern for renal toxicity (Mu Tong aristolochic acid issue)

comparison_formulas: Zuo Jin Wan: liver fire invading the stomach specifically — acid reflux, vomiting — just 2 herbs
Huang Lian Jie Du Tang: fire toxin in all three burners, not specifically liver-targeted
Dan Zhi Xiao Yao San: liver constraint transforming to heat with underlying blood deficiency — milder, more for constraint than excess fire
clinical_pearls: This formula is both 'draining' (xie) and 'clearing' (qing) — it attacks from two angles: bitter-cold herbs drain fire, while bland-draining herbs leach out damp-heat through urination.
Sheng Di and Dang Gui are the protective pair — they prevent the intensely bitter-cold and draining herbs from damaging yin and blood. Without them, the formula would be too harsh.
Chai Hu serves as the envoy (Shi) to guide the formula to the liver channel — it does NOT serve its usual function of releasing the exterior here.
Modern substitution: Mu Tong (Aristolochia) is replaced with Chuan Mu Tong or Deng Xin Cao due to nephrotoxicity concerns from aristolochic acid.
contraindications: Spleen or stomach deficiency (the bitter-cold herbs will further damage yang)
Liver yin deficiency with rising yang (deficient fire, not excess fire)
Long-term use (will damage spleen and stomach)

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat from Organs
indications: Liver fire invading the stomach: acid regurgitation, vomiting of sour or bitter fluid, hypochondriac pain, epigastric fullness and distention, belching.
mnemonic: LEFT METAL controls WOOD: 6 parts COPTIS (bitter-cold, clears liver fire) with just 1 part EVODIA (acrid-hot, directs qi down) — the tiny hot rebel prevents the cold general from freezing the stomach.
board_pearl: Zuo Jin Wan uses the metal-controls-wood (jin ke mu) principle: Huang Lian (metal = lung, bitter) restrains liver fire, and the 6:1 ratio of cold-to-hot is the key testable fact.
formula_math: Reverse the 6:1 ratio (Wu Zhu Yu 6: Huang Lian 1) = Fan Zuo Jin Wan (for stomach cold with vomiting, exactly opposite indication)

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Shao 9g, Chai Hu 6g
key_modifications.for: Pronounced hypochondriac pain from liver qi stagnation with heat

key_modifications.modification: Add Ban Xia 9g, Chen Pi 6g
key_modifications.for: Severe nausea and vomiting with phlegm

key_modifications.modification: Add Hai Piao Xiao 12g, Wa Leng Zi 15g
key_modifications.for: Severe acid reflux with epigastric burning (modern use for gastric acid)

comparison_formulas: Long Dan Xie Gan Tang: broader liver fire formula for fire flaring up and damp-heat pouring down — Zuo Jin Wan specifically targets liver fire invading stomach
Fan Zuo Jin Wan: reversed ratio for stomach cold vomiting — opposite pathology
Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi Tang: stomach disharmony from qi deficiency with dampness, not fire
clinical_pearls: The name 'Left Metal' refers to the five-phase principle: metal (lung, associated with the left in some systems) controls wood (liver). Huang Lian enters the heart but its bitter flavor belongs to the fire phase — it uses fire to drain fire from the liver.
The 6:1 ratio is critical: enough Huang Lian to powerfully clear fire, with just enough Wu Zhu Yu to prevent Huang Lian from damaging stomach yang, direct qi downward, and stop vomiting.
Wu Zhu Yu is hot and enters the liver — it seems contradictory but serves as a 'guiding envoy' to the liver while preventing the cold Huang Lian from congealing stomach qi.
The smallest formula in the Clear Heat category — only 2 herbs — yet highly effective for its specific pattern.
contraindications: Vomiting from stomach cold or deficiency (use Li Zhong Wan instead)
Acid reflux from spleen/stomach yang deficiency
Yin deficiency with empty fire rising

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat from Organs
indications: Smoldering lung heat (lurking fire in the lungs): cough with wheezing, skin that feels warm to the touch (especially in the afternoon), mild fever in the afternoon, possible sweating.
mnemonic: DRAIN the WHITE (lung): LYCIUM BARK (Di Gu Pi) and MULBERRY BARK (Sang Bai Pi) — two BARKS strip heat from the lung metal, while RICE and LICORICE protect the stomach from the cold drainage.
board_pearl: Xie Bai San drains smoldering/lurking fire from the lungs — characterized by skin warm to the touch and afternoon fever — unlike Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang which treats acute, vigorous lung heat with dyspnea.

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin 9g, Zhi Mu 9g
key_modifications.for: More severe lung heat with pronounced cough and thick yellow sputum

key_modifications.modification: Add Mai Men Dong 12g, Bei Sha Shen 12g
key_modifications.for: Lung yin damage with dry cough

key_modifications.modification: Add Qian Hu 9g, Xing Ren 9g
key_modifications.for: Cough with more prominent wheezing and chest oppression

comparison_formulas: Ma Xing Shi Gan Tang: acute vigorous lung heat with dyspnea and nasal flaring — excess and acute, not smoldering
Qing Hao Bie Jia Tang: steaming bone disorder with yin deficiency — heat from deficiency, not lurking fire in an organ
Bai He Gu Jin Tang: lung yin deficiency with dry cough and blood-streaked sputum — more yin deficiency, less heat
clinical_pearls: 'Drain the White' refers to the color of the lungs (metal = white) — this formula specifically targets the lung organ.

Both Jun herbs are tree barks (Pi = bark): Di Gu Pi (lycium root bark) cools deficiency heat and Sang Bai Pi (mulberry root bark) drains lung heat and calms wheezing — together they address both the root and the manifestation.
The heat here is 'lurking' or 'smoldering' (fu huo) — not as vigorous as yangming-level heat but persistent and damaging to lung yin over time.
Geng Mi and Zhi Gan Cao protect the middle jiao, recognizing that prolonged use of cooling herbs can damage the spleen and stomach.
contraindications: Wind-cold cough with thin white sputum (exterior cold, not internal heat)
Lung qi deficiency cough (needs supplementing, not draining)
Cough from liver fire invading the lungs (use Xie Qing Wan or modified Long Dan Xie Gan Tang)

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat from Organs
indications: Stomach fire: toothache (especially severe, may feel as though the teeth are loose), facial swelling, bleeding gums, fever, foul breath, preference for cold drinks, general sensitivity of teeth to heat.
mnemonic: CLEAR the STOMACH fire causing TOOTHACHE: COPTIS (Huang Lian) directly drains stomach fire, CIMICIFUGA (Sheng Ma) guides it upward to the gums, MU DAN PI cools blood, SHENG DI and DANG GUI protect yin and blood from fire damage.
board_pearl: Qing Wei San is THE formula for stomach fire toothache — Sheng Ma is the key guiding herb that directs the formula to the yangming channel (stomach/teeth/gums).

key_modifications.modification: Add Shi Gao 15-30g
key_modifications.for: Severe stomach fire with high fever and great thirst

key_modifications.modification: Add Xi Xin 1g, Bai Zhi 6g
key_modifications.for: Severe toothache with wind component

key_modifications.modification: Add Sheng Ma and Mai Men Dong 12g
key_modifications.for: Bleeding gums with yin deficiency

comparison_formulas: Yu Nu Jian: stomach fire with concomitant kidney yin deficiency — toothache plus yin deficiency signs (thirst, loose teeth from deficiency)
Huang Lian Jie Du Tang: fire toxin in all three burners, broader — Qing Wei San specifically targets stomach channel fire
Dao Chi San: heart fire transferring to the small intestine — mouth sores rather than toothache
clinical_pearls: Sheng Ma has a dual role here: (1) it guides the formula upward to the teeth and gums along the yangming channel, and (2) its cool, acrid nature disperses fire from the yangming level. It is NOT used here to raise yang qi.
The combination of cooling (Huang Lian) with blood-nourishing (Sheng Di, Dang Gui) and blood-cooling (Mu Dan Pi) prevents the stomach fire from further consuming yin and blood.
Huang Lian dosage is flexible (3-15g) depending on the severity of the stomach fire.
Clinically used for acute gingivitis, periodontitis, dental abscesses, and aphthous ulcers from stomach fire.
contraindications: Toothache from kidney yin deficiency with deficiency fire (use Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan)

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat from Organs
indications: Stomach heat with kidney yin deficiency: toothache with loose teeth, bleeding gums, headache, irritability, fever, thirst with desire for cold drinks, possible heat sensation in the teeth.
tongue: Red with dry or scanty coating
pulse: Rapid, large, may be floating and slippery or thin and rapid
mnemonic: The JADE WOMAN offers GYPSUM (Shi Gao) ice cream with PREPARED REHMANNIA (Shu Di) chocolate, OPHIOPOGON (Mai Dong) and ANEMARRHENA (Zhi Mu) smoothies, sending it all DOWN with ACHYRANTHES (Niu Xi) — she clears stomach fire while nourishing kidney water below.
board_pearl: Yu Nu Jian treats stomach fire with concurrent kidney yin deficiency — unlike Qing Wei San which addresses stomach fire alone; the presence of Shu Di Huang (prepared, kidney-nourishing) and Niu Xi (descending, kidney-directed) distinguishes it.
formula_math: Bai Hu Tang (Shi Gao, Zhi Mu) + kidney yin nourishing (Shu Di Huang, Mai Men Dong, Niu Xi) — combines qi-level heat clearing with kidney yin nourishment

key_modifications.modification: Replace Shu Di Huang with Sheng Di Huang 15g
key_modifications.for: More severe heat with bleeding (Sheng Di cools blood more effectively)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Bai 6g
key_modifications.for: Pronounced lower jiao deficiency fire

key_modifications.modification: Add Da Huang 6g
key_modifications.for: Concurrent constipation from stomach heat

comparison_formulas: Qing Wei San: stomach fire WITHOUT kidney yin deficiency — more blood-cooling, no yin nourishment
Bai Hu Tang: pure qi-level heat with four bigs, no yin deficiency component
Liu Wei Di Huang Wan: kidney yin deficiency without prominent stomach fire
clinical_pearls: The formula addresses fire above (stomach) and water deficiency below (kidney) simultaneously — the 'fire and water' imbalance.
Niu Xi serves as the Shi herb directing the formula downward to nourish the kidneys and prevent the fire from flaring upward.

Shu Di Huang (prepared, warm) is specifically chosen over Sheng Di Huang (raw, cold) to nourish kidney yin without adding too much cold — though in practice, many clinicians substitute Sheng Di when heat is more pronounced.
The name 'Jade Woman' evokes cooling beauty — jade is cool to the touch, symbolizing the formula's ability to cool fire while maintaining grace (nourishing yin).
contraindications: Spleen deficiency with loose stools (Shu Di is cloying and can worsen dampness)
True yin deficiency fire without excess stomach heat (use Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan)

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat from Organs
indications: Heart fire transferring to the small intestine: irritability, thirst, red face, desire for cold drinks, oral/tongue ulcers, dark scanty painful urination (lin syndrome from heart fire), possible hematuria.
mnemonic: GUIDE the RED (heart fire) OUT through urine: RAW REHMANNIA (Sheng Di) cools heart and nourishes yin, MUTONG + BAMBOO LEAVES (Dan Zhu Ye) drain fire downward through the small intestine to the bladder, LICORICE TIP (Gan Cao Shao) goes to the extremity of the urethra.
board_pearl: Dao Chi San guides heart fire down through the heart-small intestine interior-exterior relationship, treating both mouth sores (heart fire above) and painful dark urination (fire transferred below).

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Lian 3g
key_modifications.for: Severe heart fire with pronounced mouth sores and irritability

key_modifications.modification: Add Che Qian Zi 9g, Hua Shi 12g
key_modifications.for: Prominent painful urination with hematuria

key_modifications.modification: Add Mai Men Dong 12g, Xuan Shen 9g
key_modifications.for: Significant yin deficiency with dry mouth and throat

key_modifications.modification: Replace Mu Tong with Deng Xin Cao 3g
key_modifications.for: Modern practice to avoid aristolochic acid nephrotoxicity

comparison_formulas: Qing Wei San: stomach fire causing toothache and gum bleeding — different organ, different presentation
Long Dan Xie Gan Tang: liver/gallbladder fire and damp-heat — more intense and broader than Dao Chi San
Xiao Ji Yin Zi: blood lin (hematuria) from bladder heat, more focused on stopping bleeding
clinical_pearls: The heart and small intestine are interior-exteriorly related — heart fire transfers to the small intestine, which separates the pure from the turbid, causing dark, scanty, painful urination.
Gan Cao Shao (the tip of the licorice root) is specifically used — in TCM, the tip of the root reaches to the extremities of the body, including the urethra.
The red tongue TIP is the hallmark — the tongue tip corresponds to the heart in tongue diagnosis.
This is a gentle formula suitable for children with mouth sores and urinary symptoms — it drains fire without being overly harsh.
contraindications: Urinary difficulty from kidney yang deficiency
Pregnancy (Mu Tong can be toxic; the formula promotes urination and moves downward)
Yin deficiency without actual heart fire (will further drain fluids)

category: Clear Heat — Clear Heat from Organs
indications: Hot dysenteric disorder (re li): abdominal pain, tenesmus, bloody diarrhea with more blood than pus (or pure blood), burning sensation around the anus, thirst with desire for cold drinks.
mnemonic: The WHITE-HAIRED OLD MAN (Bai Tou Weng) uses COPTIS and PHELLODENDRON (two Huangs) plus ASH BARK (Qin Pi) to stop BLOODY DYSENTERY — four bitter-cold herbs for hot, bloody stool with tenesmus.
board_pearl: Bai Tou Weng Tang is THE formula for hot dysentery (re li) with more BLOOD than pus — if there is more PUS than blood, think Shao Yao Tang instead.
formula_math: Contrast with Shao Yao Tang: Shao Yao Tang treats damp-heat dysentery with more pus than blood and prominent tenesmus/abdominal pain (has Bai Shao to relax smooth muscle); Bai Tou Weng Tang treats heat-toxin dysentery with more blood than pus

key_modifications.modification: Add Chi Shao 9g, Mu Dan Pi 9g
key_modifications.for: Severe blood in stool with pronounced heat in the blood

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Shao 15g, Mu Xiang 6g
key_modifications.for: Pronounced abdominal pain and tenesmus

key_modifications.modification: Add Ge Gen 12g, Huang Qin 9g
key_modifications.for: Concurrent exterior symptoms or early-stage presentation

comparison_formulas: Shao Yao Tang: damp-heat dysentery with more pus than blood, prominent tenesmus — different pathological emphasis
Huang Lian Jie Du Tang: fire toxin in all three burners, broader than intestinal focus
Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang: heat dysentery/diarrhea at the tai yang-yang ming interface, with exterior symptoms
clinical_pearls: More blood than pus = heat toxin deeper in the blood level of the intestines; more pus than blood = damp-heat with qi stagnation.
All four herbs are bitter and cold — this is a purely draining formula with no supplementation, suitable only for robust excess patterns.
Bai Tou Weng specifically enters the large intestine and stomach channels to clear heat and cool blood — it is the lead herb for toxic dysentery in the Shang Han Lun.
Qin Pi (ash bark) is astringent in addition to being bitter-cold — it helps bind the intestines to stop diarrhea while clearing heat.
contraindications: Dysentery from deficiency cold (pale complexion, watery stool without burning, cold abdomen)
Chronic dysentery with spleen qi deficiency

english_name: Artemisia Annua and Soft-Shelled Turtle Shell Decoction
indications: Late-stage warm-febrile disease where heat has lurked in the yin level (steaming bone disorder variant): night fever that breaks with sweating at dawn but without feeling refreshed, emaciation, no other obvious symptoms. The fever comes at night and leaves in the morning.
mnemonic: TURTLE SHELL (Bie Jia) dives deep to retrieve hidden heat, ARTEMISIA (Qing Hao) vents it outward like smoke through a chimney — SHENG DI, ZHI MU, and MU DAN PI nourish the yin scorched by lurking fire.
board_pearl: Qing Hao Bie Jia Tang uses the unique Bie Jia + Qing Hao pairing: Bie Jia (heavy, sinking) enters the yin level to SEARCH OUT lurking heat, and Qing Hao (light, floating, aromatic) VENTS that heat outward — the 'search and vent' strategy.

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Wei 9g, Di Gu Pi 12g
key_modifications.for: More severe night sweating

key_modifications.modification: Add Yin Chai Hu 9g, Hu Huang Lian 6g
key_modifications.for: Pronounced steaming bone sensation

key_modifications.modification: Add Sha Shen 12g, Mai Men Dong 12g
key_modifications.for: Severe yin deficiency with dry mouth and throat

comparison_formulas: Qing Gu San: steaming bone disorder from deficiency heat — more herbs, includes herbs for all sources of deficiency heat
Da Bu Yin Wan: kidney yin deficiency fire with steaming bones, tidal fever — heavier yin supplementation
Xie Bai San: lurking heat in the LUNGS specifically, not generalized yin-level heat
clinical_pearls: The hallmark presentation: fever at night, recedes by morning with sweating, but the patient does NOT feel refreshed by the sweating — this distinguishes it from normal night sweats.

The cooking method is important: Bie Jia is cooked first (30 minutes) to extract its properties, and Qing Hao is added LAST (in the final 5 minutes or steeped) because its aromatic, venting properties are destroyed by prolonged cooking.
This is Wu Jutong's formula from Wen Bing Tiao Bian — designed for the specific late-stage warm-disease scenario where pathogenic heat has burrowed into the yin level.

The 'search and vent' (sou and tou) strategy is unique to this formula — most yin-deficiency heat formulas only nourish yin and clear heat, but this one actively retrieves and expels the lurking pathogen.
contraindications: Early-stage febrile disease where the pathogen is still at the qi or wei level
Excess heat patterns (use clearing/draining formulas instead)
Night sweating from yang deficiency (use Gui Zhi Jia Long Gu Mu Li Tang)

indications: Steaming bone disorder (gu zheng) from liver and kidney yin deficiency: tidal fever (afternoon/evening), sensation of heat radiating from the bones, irritability, emaciation, night sweats, fatigue, red lips and cheeks.
mnemonic: COOL the BONES with 8 herbs: Silver BUPLEURUM (Yin Chai Hu) and PICRORHIZA (Hu Huang Lian) are the two 'fakes' (silver Chai Hu is NOT Chai Hu, Hu Huang Lian is NOT Huang Lian) — joined by ANEMARRHENA, LYCIUM BARK, ARTEMISIA, GENTIANA, TURTLE SHELL, and LICORICE.
board_pearl: Qing Gu San treats steaming bone disorder (gu zheng) — Yin Chai Hu (NOT regular Chai Hu) is the key Jun herb that specifically clears deficiency heat without releasing the exterior; Hu Huang Lian (NOT regular Huang Lian) clears deficiency heat without draining excess fire.
formula_math: Contains Qing Hao and Bie Jia (from Qing Hao Bie Jia Tang) but adds more deficiency-heat specialists (Yin Chai Hu, Hu Huang Lian, Di Gu Pi, Qin Jiao) for a broader approach to steaming bone disorder

key_modifications.modification: Add Sheng Di 15g, Shu Di 15g
key_modifications.for: Significant yin deficiency with dry mouth and emaciation

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Wei 9g, Mu Dan Pi 9g
key_modifications.for: Night sweats with more pronounced blood-level heat

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi 12g, Dang Gui 9g
key_modifications.for: Concurrent qi and blood deficiency with fatigue

comparison_formulas: Qing Hao Bie Jia Tang: lurking heat in yin level from warm-febrile disease — more pathogen-focused ('search and vent'), Qing Gu San is more deficiency-focused
Da Bu Yin Wan: kidney yin deficiency fire with stronger yin supplementation (Shu Di, Gui Ban) — less heat-clearing, more nourishing
Dang Gui Liu Huang Tang: yin deficiency fire with severe night sweats as the chief complaint (uses Huang Qi to stabilize exterior)
clinical_pearls: The two 'impostor' herbs are the key board-testable fact: Yin Chai Hu is NOT Chai Hu (Bupleurum) — they come from different plants with different actions. Yin Chai Hu clears deficiency heat; Chai Hu releases the exterior and raises yang.
Similarly, Hu Huang Lian (Picrorhiza) is NOT Huang Lian (Coptis) — Hu Huang Lian clears deficiency heat and childhood nutritional impairment heat; Huang Lian drains excess fire.
Steaming bone disorder (gu zheng) is the most severe form of yin deficiency heat — the patient feels as though the heat is radiating outward from the bones themselves.
All herbs are used in equal doses (5g each) except Gan Cao (3g) — the formula is balanced and gentle, reflecting its use in chronic deficiency conditions.
contraindications: Exterior patterns with fever (use formulas that release the exterior)
Excess heat patterns in the qi or blood level
Yang deficiency with cold limbs and cold sensation
Spleen yang deficiency with loose stools

actions: Drains excessive fullness from the Intestines
indications: Yangming organ-stage pattern with severe constipation, focal distention and fullness in the chest and abdomen, abdominal pain that worsens with pressure, tidal fever, profuse sweating from the palms and soles, delirious speech, or in severe cases, unconsciousness.
tongue: Red with dry yellow or black, thick, dry coating
pulse: Deep, excess, and forceful (chen shi you li)
mnemonic: The MAJOR general (Da Huang) orders his troops with THICK armor (Hou Po) and STONE shields (Zhi Shi), using SALTY catapults (Mang Xiao) to purge the fortress of Yangming fu heat.
board_pearl: Da Cheng Qi Tang is the strongest purgative — uses ALL four herbs (Da Huang, Mang Xiao, Zhi Shi, Hou Po) for Yangming fu organ excess with 'four bigs' (da re, da han, da ke, da mai).
formula_math: Da Cheng Qi Tang = Xiao Cheng Qi Tang + Mang Xiao (adds softening/moistening) = Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang + Zhi Shi + Hou Po (adds qi-moving)

key_modifications.modification: Add Ren Shen, Mai Men Dong
key_modifications.for: Qi and yin deficiency with constipation (Xin Jia Huang Long Tang concept)

key_modifications.modification: Add Tao Ren, Chi Shao
key_modifications.for: Blood stasis with heat accumulation

key_modifications.modification: Increase Hou Po dose
key_modifications.for: Severe abdominal distention

clinical_pearls: Distinguished from Xiao Cheng Qi Tang by the presence of Mang Xiao which softens hardness — use when stool is dry and hard.
The four presentations guiding use: constipation (bi), fullness (man), dryness (zao), and firmness (shi).
Must discontinue once desired effect achieved to prevent damage to zheng qi.
contraindications: Elderly or debilitated patients without modification

indications: Yangming organ-stage pattern with tidal fever, constipation, abdominal distention and fullness, but the stool is not yet dry and hard. Also for early-stage dysenteric disorders with tidal fever.
mnemonic: MINOR general skips the salt (no Mang Xiao) — Da Huang leads a SMALLER platoon of Zhi Shi and Hou Po when the enemy (stool) is not yet hardened into stone.
board_pearl: Xiao Cheng Qi Tang = Da Cheng Qi Tang minus Mang Xiao — used when there is fullness and distention (man) but stool is NOT yet dry and hard (no zao or shi).
formula_math: Xiao Cheng Qi Tang = Da Cheng Qi Tang - Mang Xiao

key_modifications.modification: Add Mang Xiao
key_modifications.for: If stool becomes dry and hard (becomes Da Cheng Qi Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Reduce Da Huang dose
key_modifications.for: Milder constipation with more distention

clinical_pearls: Key distinction: fullness (man) is the primary presentation, not dryness (zao) — stool has not yet hardened.
Uses LESS Hou Po than Da Cheng Qi Tang — Hou Po dose is smaller because distention is less severe.
Can be used as a diagnostic test: if patient passes flatus after taking it, no need for Da Cheng Qi Tang.

english_name: Regulate the Stomach and Order the Qi Decoction
indications: Yangming excess heat with constipation, but without significant distention or fullness. Symptoms include irritability, thirst, abdominal discomfort, fever, and delirious speech in mild cases. Also for Yangming channel-stage symptoms like steaming bone or tidal fever with constipation.
mnemonic: TIAO WEI (regulate stomach) is the GENTLE purge — Gan Cao is the peacekeeper who tames Da Huang and Mang Xiao, no Qi-movers needed because there's no distention.
board_pearl: Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang = Da Huang + Mang Xiao + Gan Cao — the GENTLEST Cheng Qi, used when there is heat and dryness but NO distention or fullness (no Zhi Shi/Hou Po needed).
formula_math: Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang = Da Cheng Qi Tang - Zhi Shi - Hou Po + Zhi Gan Cao

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Shi, Hou Po
key_modifications.for: If distention develops (becomes Da Cheng Qi Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Reduce Da Huang dose
key_modifications.for: Milder heat accumulation

clinical_pearls: The mildest of the three Cheng Qi formulas — focuses on dryness (zao) without fullness (man).
Gan Cao moderates the harsh purging action and protects the Stomach.
Classic formula for early Yangming heat when constipation is present but qi stagnation/distention is not.

indications: Constipation due to blood deficiency and dryness of the Intestines, particularly in the elderly, postpartum women, or those with chronic illness. Dry, hard stool that is difficult to pass.
mnemonic: RUN CHANG (moisten intestines) — old HEMP farmer (Huo Ma Ren) and PEACH grower (Tao Ren) need BLOOD (Dang Gui) and JUICE (Sheng Di) to keep their dry land flowing, with a gentle PUSH (Zhi Ke) downstream.
board_pearl: Run Chang Wan moistens the Intestines for constipation due to blood deficiency and dryness — key for elderly or postpartum patients with dry stools and a thin, choppy pulse.

key_modifications.modification: Add Rou Cong Rong, He Shou Wu
key_modifications.for: Kidney essence deficiency contributing to dryness

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi
key_modifications.for: Qi deficiency failing to move stool

key_modifications.modification: Add Xuan Shen, Mai Men Dong
key_modifications.for: More severe yin deficiency with dryness

clinical_pearls: This is a MOISTENING laxative, not a purging formula — appropriate for deficiency-type constipation.
Distinguished from Ma Zi Ren Wan by the absence of purgative Da Huang and the presence of blood-nourishing Dang Gui and Sheng Di.
Particularly suited for postpartum blood deficiency constipation.

indications: Spleen-constrained pulse pattern (pi yue) from the Shanghan Lun: Stomach heat with Spleen yin deficiency leading to dry, hard stools that are difficult to pass, frequent urination, habitual constipation.
pulse: Floating, choppy, or superficially tight and deep and moderate (fu se)
mnemonic: HEMP SEED has a secret PILL inside: it's a MINI Cheng Qi (Da Huang, Zhi Shi, Hou Po) wrapped in MOISTENING seeds (Ma Ren, Xing Ren) with HONEY, and Bai Shao to protect yin — for the 'spleen-constrained' patient who pees a lot but can't poop.
board_pearl: Ma Zi Ren Wan = Xiao Cheng Qi Tang (Da Huang, Zhi Shi, Hou Po) + moistening herbs (Huo Ma Ren, Xing Ren, Bai Shao, Honey) for 'pi yue' (spleen-constrained) pattern: frequent urination + constipation.
formula_math: Ma Zi Ren Wan = Xiao Cheng Qi Tang + Huo Ma Ren + Xing Ren + Bai Shao + Feng Mi

key_modifications.modification: Remove Da Huang, increase Huo Ma Ren
key_modifications.for: Elderly or weak patients who cannot tolerate purgation

key_modifications.modification: Add Dang Gui, Sheng Di
key_modifications.for: More pronounced blood deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Yu Li Ren
key_modifications.for: More severe constipation

clinical_pearls: Pi yue means the Spleen is constrained — Stomach excess heat dries fluids while Spleen fails to transport moisture to the Intestines.
The hallmark is frequent urination WITH constipation — fluids go to the Bladder instead of the Intestines.
Contains Xiao Cheng Qi Tang inside it, but the moistening herbs make it much gentler.

actions: Attacks and expels congested fluid (suspended thin mucus/xuan yin)
indications: Severe fluid accumulation in the chest and hypochondria (xuan yin/suspended thin mucus), with cough, fullness and distention of the chest and hypochondria, pain when coughing, shortness of breath, or generalized edema with ascites. The condition is excess in nature.
mnemonic: TEN DATES (Shi Zao) buffer the THREE TOXIC GENERALS (Yuan Hua, Gan Sui, Da Ji) who violently expel SUSPENDED FLUID from the chest — remember 'Flowers (Yuan Hua) in the GAN-den (Gan Sui) attract GEESE (Da Ji)' protected by 10 date bodyguards.
board_pearl: Shi Zao Tang uses three TOXIC drastics (Yuan Hua, Gan Sui, Da Ji) buffered by 10 Da Zao to violently purge FLUID/WATER accumulation from the chest and flanks — never for deficiency.

key_modifications.modification: Adjust dosage of three toxic herbs downward
key_modifications.for: Weaker patients, but still only for excess conditions

key_modifications.modification: Can rotate with tonifying formulas
key_modifications.for: Patients who need repeated treatments but require recovery between doses

comparison_formulas: Ting Li Da Zao Xie Fei Tang
clinical_pearls: All three drastic herbs are ground into powder and taken with the Da Zao decoction on an EMPTY stomach in the morning.
Da Zao protects the Stomach and Spleen from the harsh, toxic herbs — the formula name emphasizes this protective role.
Must STOP the formula once the desired drainage is achieved. Not for long-term use.
The three toxic herbs are incompatible (xiang fan) with Gan Cao — never combine.
contraindications: Do NOT combine with Gan Cao (incompatible with Gan Sui and Yuan Hua per 18 incompatibles)

actions: Warms the interior and disperses cold
indications: Cold accumulation in the interior with constipation. Abdominal pain, constipation, hypochondriac pain, fever, cold body and extremities. The pattern is excess cold blocking the interior — an interior cold binding (li han shi) pattern.
mnemonic: COLD constipation paradox: Da Huang purges the blockage while Fu Zi WARMS the cold that caused it, and tiny Xi Xin penetrates the yin channels — it's FIRE and ICE working together to unblock.
board_pearl: Da Huang Fu Zi Tang is the formula for COLD-type constipation — Da Huang purges while Fu Zi warms; the only Drain Downward formula that treats interior COLD excess, not heat.

key_modifications.modification: Add Dang Gui, Rou Gui
key_modifications.for: Blood stasis from cold with abdominal pain

key_modifications.modification: Add Hou Po, Zhi Shi
key_modifications.for: More severe distention and fullness

clinical_pearls: Key distinction from Cheng Qi formulas: the patient has COLD signs (cold body, white coating, tight pulse) despite constipation.
Fu Zi dose is LARGER than Da Huang — warming is prioritized over purging.
Xi Xin is essential to disperse cold from the yin channels and assist Fu Zi.
Compare with Wen Pi Tang which also warms and purges but adds Ren Shen and Gan Jiang for underlying Spleen yang deficiency.

actions: Harmonizes and releases the Shaoyang stage
indications: Shaoyang syndrome (half-exterior, half-interior) with alternating chills and fever, fullness and distention in the chest and hypochondria, irritability, nausea and vomiting, poor appetite, bitter taste in the mouth, dry throat, dizziness.
tongue: Thin white coating on one side, or normal
mnemonic: SMALL CHAI HU has 7 herbs: 'CHAI Hu and HUANG Qin are the main COUPLE, BAN Xia and SHENG Jiang stop vomiting, REN Shen + GAN Cao + DA Zao support zheng qi.' Alternating chills/fever = Shaoyang = Xiao Chai Hu.
board_pearl: Xiao Chai Hu Tang is THE Shaoyang harmonizing formula — alternating chills and fever, bitter taste, wiry pulse, fullness in the hypochondria are the cardinal signs.

key_modifications.modification: Remove Ren Shen, add Gua Lou
key_modifications.for: Chest fullness and irritability (thirst)

key_modifications.modification: Remove Da Zao, Sheng Jiang; add Gui Zhi, Fu Ling
key_modifications.for: Palpitations with urinary difficulty (Chai Hu Gui Zhi Gan Jiang Tang direction)

key_modifications.modification: Remove Huang Qin, add Bai Shao, Gui Zhi
key_modifications.for: Concurrent Taiyang and Shaoyang (Chai Hu Gui Zhi Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Mang Xiao, Da Huang
key_modifications.for: Constipation with Shaoyang (moves toward Da Chai Hu Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Remove Ban Xia, Ren Shen; add Mu Dan Pi, Zhi Zi
key_modifications.for: Liver fire with irritability

clinical_pearls: The 'four cardinal symptoms' of Shaoyang: alternating chills/fever, bitter taste, dry throat, dizziness.
Chai Hu releases the Shaoyang while Huang Qin clears interior heat — this pair is the core axis.
Shanghan Lun lists numerous modifications based on which symptoms predominate.
Never use sweating, purgation, or emesis alone for Shaoyang — only harmonization.
contraindications: Yin deficiency with Liver yang rising
Excess above with deficiency below (unless modified)
Pure exterior or pure interior patterns
Vomiting of blood (tu xue) or nosebleed (nv xue) in Shanghan Lun prohibitions

indications: Concurrent Shaoyang and Yangming patterns: alternating chills and fever, fullness and burning in the epigastrium that is unrelieved by pressure, nausea and vomiting, constipation or burning diarrhea, bitter taste, irritability.
mnemonic: BIG CHAI HU is Xiao Chai Hu going to WAR — drops the tonics (no Ren Shen, no Gan Cao) and picks up WEAPONS: Da Huang purges, Zhi Shi breaks stagnation, Bai Shao calms. Shaoyang + Yangming = Da Chai Hu.
board_pearl: Da Chai Hu Tang treats concurrent SHAOYANG + YANGMING — alternating chills/fever PLUS epigastric/abdominal fullness, constipation, and a wiry forceful pulse.
formula_math: Da Chai Hu Tang = Xiao Chai Hu Tang - Ren Shen - Gan Cao + Da Huang + Zhi Shi + Bai Shao

key_modifications.modification: Remove Da Huang
key_modifications.for: If no constipation (some editions already omit it)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: More pronounced heat in the Stomach

key_modifications.modification: Add Jin Qian Cao, Yu Jin
key_modifications.for: Gallbladder stones with Shaoyang pattern

clinical_pearls: Key difference from Xiao Chai Hu Tang: removes tonifying herbs (Ren Shen, Gan Cao) and adds purging/draining herbs (Da Huang, Zhi Shi).
Some editions of the Shanghan Lun omit Da Huang — indicates the purging action is secondary to harmonization.
Modern use includes acute cholecystitis, pancreatitis, and biliary disorders with the Shaoyang-Yangming pattern.

indications: Yang-type frigid extremities (re jue/heat inversion) caused by interior heat constraining yang qi so it cannot reach the extremities. Cold fingers and toes (not entire limbs), fullness in the chest and epigastrium, abdominal pain, diarrhea with tenesmus. Also for Liver-Spleen disharmony with hypochondriac pain and epigastric distention.
mnemonic: FOUR ingredients for FOUR cold limbs — but it's a FAKE cold (yang trapped by heat)! Chai Hu FREES the stuck qi, Bai Shao SOOTHES the Liver, Zhi Shi BREAKS the stagnation, Gan Cao HARMONIZES. Cold hands ≠ cold interior here.
board_pearl: Si Ni San treats HEAT-type cold extremities (re jue) — yang qi is constrained by interior heat, NOT depleted; distinguished from Si Ni Tang (true yang collapse) by the wiry pulse and red tongue.
formula_math: Si Ni San is the BASE formula for Liver-Spleen disharmony — Xiao Yao San builds upon its concept

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: More pronounced heat

key_modifications.modification: Add Fu Ling
key_modifications.for: Dampness with loose stools

key_modifications.modification: Add Xiang Fu, Yu Jin
key_modifications.for: More severe qi stagnation with pain

clinical_pearls: CRITICAL distinction: Si Ni San (cold from constraint/heat) vs. Si Ni Tang (cold from yang collapse). Opposite mechanisms despite similar names.
The foundation formula for Liver qi stagnation affecting the Spleen — many later formulas build on this structure.
Chai Hu + Bai Shao is the classic pair: Chai Hu spreads and raises, Bai Shao nourishes and restrains.
contraindications: True yang deficiency cold extremities (use Si Ni Tang instead)
Yin deficiency with Liver yang rising

composition.herb: Wei Jiang / Sheng Jiang (Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens)

actions: Spreads Liver qi and relieves constraint
Nourishes the blood and strengthens the Spleen
indications: Liver qi stagnation with blood deficiency and Spleen weakness. Hypochondriac pain, headache, dizziness, fatigue, poor appetite, irregular menstruation, breast distention, alternating chills and fever, irritability.
mnemonic: The FREE and EASY WANDERER needs: Chai Hu to FREE the Liver, Dang Gui + Bai Shao to nourish BLOOD, Bai Zhu + Fu Ling + Gan Cao (= Si Jun Zi base) to strengthen SPLEEN, and a MINT leaf (Bo He) + GINGER for a refreshing stroll.
board_pearl: Xiao Yao San is THE formula for Liver qi stagnation with blood deficiency and Spleen weakness — the most commonly prescribed formula for Liver-Spleen disharmony, especially with menstrual irregularity.
formula_math: Xiao Yao San = Si Ni San concept (Chai Hu + Bai Shao) + Si Jun Zi Tang base (Bai Zhu, Fu Ling, Gan Cao) + Dang Gui + Bo He + Sheng Jiang

key_modifications.modification: Add Mu Dan Pi, Zhi Zi
key_modifications.for: Liver fire/heat symptoms (becomes Jia Wei Xiao Yao San)

key_modifications.modification: Add Sheng Di, Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: Blood heat with menstrual irregularity

key_modifications.modification: Add Xiang Fu, Yu Jin
key_modifications.for: More severe qi stagnation

clinical_pearls: One of the most frequently used formulas in TCM clinical practice.
Bo He is used in small quantity to assist Chai Hu in spreading Liver qi — do not omit it.
The Spleen-tonifying component (Bai Zhu, Fu Ling, Gan Cao) distinguishes this from pure qi-moving formulas.
Particularly suited for women with PMS, irregular menses, and emotional lability due to Liver-Spleen disharmony.
contraindications: Yin deficiency with significant fire (use Jia Wei Xiao Yao San instead)
Excess Liver fire without blood deficiency

actions: Spreads Liver qi and relieves constraint
Nourishes the blood and strengthens the Spleen
Clears heat and cools the blood
indications: Liver qi stagnation transforming into fire, with blood deficiency and Spleen weakness. All symptoms of Xiao Yao San PLUS irritability, red eyes, flushed cheeks, increased menstrual flow, uterine bleeding, or early periods from heat in the blood.
mnemonic: AUGMENTED Xiao Yao San = the Free Wanderer now has a FEVER (constrained qi turned to fire) — add the COOLING DUO: Mu Dan Pi (cools blood) and Zhi Zi (drains fire through urine). Also called Dan Zhi Xiao Yao San.
board_pearl: Jia Wei Xiao Yao San = Xiao Yao San + Mu Dan Pi + Zhi Zi — used when Liver qi stagnation has TRANSFORMED INTO FIRE with signs of heat (irritability, red eyes, early/heavy menses).
formula_math: Jia Wei Xiao Yao San = Xiao Yao San + Mu Dan Pi + Zhi Zi

key_modifications.modification: Add Sheng Di Huang, Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: More severe blood heat with heavy menstrual bleeding

key_modifications.modification: Add Long Gu, Mu Li
key_modifications.for: Restlessness, insomnia, anxiety

key_modifications.modification: Add Xia Ku Cao
key_modifications.for: Liver fire with headache and red eyes

clinical_pearls: Also known as Dan Zhi Xiao Yao San — named after the two added herbs.
The key differentiator from Xiao Yao San is the presence of HEAT signs: irritability, red tongue, rapid pulse.
Commonly used for menopausal symptoms (hot flashes, mood swings) in modern practice.
Mu Dan Pi cools blood without causing stasis; Zhi Zi drains fire downward through urination.
contraindications: Cold patterns from Spleen yang deficiency
No heat signs present (use plain Xiao Yao San)

english_name: Pinellia Decoction to Drain the Epigastrium
actions: Harmonizes the Stomach and directs rebellious qi downward
Disperses clumping and eliminates focal distention (pi)
indications: Epigastric focal distention (pi) with no palpable mass or pain on pressure, nausea, vomiting, borborygmus, diarrhea. The pattern is mixed cold-heat with Stomach qi disharmony, often from improper purgation damaging the middle jiao.
pulse: Wiry, rapid, or wiry, slippery (xian hua)
