mnemonic: BAN XIA drains the PI (focal distention): COLD team (Gan Jiang) vs. HEAT team (Huang Qin + Huang Lian) battle in the epigastrium while REN SHEN + GAN CAO + DA ZAO rebuild the damaged middle. Cold-Heat COMPLEX = pi pattern.
board_pearl: Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang is THE formula for epigastric focal distention (pi zheng) from MIXED COLD-HEAT in the middle jiao — the cold-heat complex is treated simultaneously with bitter-cold (Huang Qin/Lian) and acrid-warm (Ban Xia/Gan Jiang).
formula_math: Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang = Xiao Chai Hu Tang - Chai Hu - Sheng Jiang + Huang Lian + Gan Jiang (moves the battle from Shaoyang to the middle jiao)

key_modifications.modification: Replace Gan Jiang with Sheng Jiang
key_modifications.for: More nausea/vomiting with water sounds in stomach (becomes Sheng Jiang Xie Xin Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Replace Ren Shen with Zhi Gan Cao (increase dose)
key_modifications.for: Gastric reflux with belching and foul breath (becomes Gan Cao Xie Xin Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Increase Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: More heat signs predominating

clinical_pearls: The three Xie Xin Tang formulas (Ban Xia, Sheng Jiang, Gan Cao) all treat pi (focal distention) but differ in emphasis.
Pi means subjective focal distention WITHOUT a palpable mass — soft on palpation, not painful with pressure.
The combination of acrid-warm and bitter-cold herbs is the hallmark approach for cold-heat complex patterns.
Modern applications include chronic gastritis, peptic ulcer, and functional dyspepsia.
contraindications: Pure heat or pure cold patterns (not a mixed pattern)
Focal distention from food stagnation (use Bao He Wan)

pinyin: Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang
actions: Releases the exterior and clears interior heat
Lifts the yang and stops diarrhea
indications: Exterior pattern with interior heat causing diarrhea or dysenteric disorder. Fever, diarrhea with foul smell, burning anus, irritability, thirst, wheezing and sweating. From Shanghan Lun: an exterior condition improperly treated with purgation, causing hot diarrhea while exterior remains.
mnemonic: GE GEN goes UP (releases exterior, raises yang), while HUANG QIN + HUANG LIAN go DOWN (clear interior heat) — together they stop HOT diarrhea that started when someone wrongly purged an exterior condition. The 3 herbs spell 'GQQ' = 'Go Quit Quick' (stop the diarrhea quickly).
board_pearl: Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang treats HEAT diarrhea with exterior symptoms — Ge Gen simultaneously releases the exterior AND raises yang to stop diarrhea while the two Huangs clear interior heat.

key_modifications.modification: Add Mu Xiang, Huang Bai
key_modifications.for: Dysenteric disorder with tenesmus

key_modifications.modification: Add Yin Chen Hao
key_modifications.for: Damp-heat jaundice with diarrhea

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Tou Weng
key_modifications.for: More severe bloody dysentery

clinical_pearls: Ge Gen has a dual action here: releases the exterior AND lifts the clear yang to stop diarrhea.
Do not confuse with Ge Gen Tang (Ge Gen + Ma Huang etc.) which is purely exterior-releasing.
Huang Qin Tang (Shanghan Lun) treats Taiyang-Shaoyang diarrhea; this formula treats exterior with interior HEAT diarrhea.
Modern use includes acute bacterial enteritis and dysentery with fever.

actions: Improves the transportive function of the Spleen
indications: Dampness stagnating in the Spleen and Stomach. Distention and fullness in the epigastrium and abdomen, loss of appetite and taste, nausea and vomiting, heavy sensation in the limbs, loose stools, fatigue, increased desire to sleep.
tongue: Swollen with thick, white, greasy coating
mnemonic: PING WEI (calm the stomach) = the BASE dampness formula: CANG ZHU dries like a TOWEL, HOU PO squeezes out the dampness, CHEN PI moves the qi — 3 warriors + Gan Cao + Jiang/Zao support. 'A CHOU stomach needs calming.'
board_pearl: Ping Wei San is the foundational formula for DAMP STAGNATION in the middle jiao — Cang Zhu is the chief that dries dampness; thick greasy white tongue coating is the hallmark sign.
formula_math: Ping Wei San is a BASE formula combined with many others: + Wu Ling San = Wei Ling Tang; + Er Chen Tang direction; + Bu Huan Jin Zheng Qi San concept

key_modifications.modification: Add Huo Xiang, Ban Xia
key_modifications.for: More severe nausea and vomiting from dampness

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Wu Ling San
key_modifications.for: Dampness with edema and urinary difficulty (Wei Ling Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin, Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: Dampness transforming into damp-heat

clinical_pearls: This is the most basic and fundamental formula for dampness in the middle jiao.
Cang Zhu (not Bai Zhu) is used because it is more drying and aromatic — better for EXCESS dampness.
Frequently used as a base formula combined with other formulas for complex dampness presentations.
The thick, white, greasy tongue coating is often the single most important diagnostic indicator.

english_name: Agastache Powder to Rectify the Qi
indications: External wind-cold with internal dampness stagnation. Fever, chills, headache, fullness and distention in the chest and epigastrium, nausea, vomiting, borborygmus, diarrhea, loss of taste. Classic 'summer-damp' or 'stomach flu' presentation.
mnemonic: HUO XIANG ZHENG QI = the 'STOMACH FLU' formula with 13 herbs: HUO Xiang leads the aromatic army to RECTIFY the QI. Think 'SUMMER cold + upset stomach' = Huo Xiang Zheng Qi. It has EVERYTHING: exterior-releasers (Su Ye, Bai Zhi), dampness-dryers (Ban Xia, Hou Po, Chen Pi), Spleen-tonics (Bai Zhu, Fu Ling).
board_pearl: Huo Xiang Zheng Qi San is THE formula for EXTERNAL WIND-COLD + INTERNAL DAMPNESS — the classic 'stomach flu' or summer-damp pattern with vomiting, diarrhea, and chills/fever.
formula_math: Huo Xiang Zheng Qi San contains Ping Wei San base (Hou Po, Chen Pi + dampness-drying) + Er Chen Tang elements (Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Fu Ling) + exterior-releasing herbs

key_modifications.modification: Remove Zi Su Ye, Bai Zhi
key_modifications.for: No exterior symptoms present

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin, Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: Dampness transforming into heat

key_modifications.modification: Add Sha Ren, Cang Zhu
key_modifications.for: More severe dampness with bloating

clinical_pearls: One of the most popular OTC patent medicines in China — available as oral liquid (Huo Xiang Zheng Qi Shui).
The large number of herbs reflects its broad approach: exterior-releasing + dampness-transforming + qi-regulating + Spleen-tonifying.
Huo Xiang (Agastache/Patchouli) is the quintessential aromatic dampness-transforming herb.
Particularly useful for gastrointestinal illness during summer or humid seasons.
contraindications: Summer-heat patterns without dampness (use Qing Shu Yi Qi Tang)

actions: Clears damp-heat from the upper, middle, and lower jiao
Promotes the smooth flow of qi
Separates the turbid from the clear
indications: Early-stage damp-warmth (shi wen) or damp-heat with dampness predominating over heat. Headache, body heaviness, afternoon fever, aversion to cold, chest and epigastric distention, facial complexion like smoky dust, no thirst or no desire for drinks.
mnemonic: THREE NUTS open THREE JIAO: Xing Ren (apricot) ventilates the UPPER (Lung), Bai Dou Kou (cardamom) aromatically transforms the MIDDLE (Spleen), Yi Yi Ren (coix) drains dampness from the LOWER — the triple-level approach to DAMP-WARMTH.
board_pearl: San Ren Tang uses three seeds/nuts to address DAMP-WARMTH (shi wen) through all three jiao — Xing Ren (upper), Bai Dou Kou (middle), Yi Yi Ren (lower); key sign is dampness GREATER than heat.

key_modifications.modification: Add Cang Zhu, Huang Bai
key_modifications.for: More pronounced damp-heat in the lower jiao

key_modifications.modification: Add Lian Qiao, Chi Xiao Dou
key_modifications.for: Damp-heat with skin eruptions

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: If heat becomes more prominent

clinical_pearls: From Wu Jutong's Wen Bing Tiao Bian — a key formula in the Warm Disease (wen bing) school.
The principle is to OPEN the upper, TRANSFORM the middle, and DRAIN the lower — a comprehensive three-jiao strategy.
Use when dampness is GREATER than heat — the tongue coating is WHITE greasy, not yellow.
Compare with Gan Lu Xiao Du Dan when heat becomes equal to or greater than dampness.
contraindications: Damp-heat where heat predominates over dampness

actions: Warms yang and promotes qi transformation
indications: Water amassment pattern (xu shui zheng): headache, fever, irritability, strong thirst but vomiting immediately after drinking, difficulty urinating. Also for edema, diarrhea, short voiding of urine, and the sensation of heaviness and pulsation below the umbilicus (throbbing in the epigastrium), as well as thin mucus (tan yin) conditions.
mnemonic: FIVE LING (five ingredients with Poria): ZE XIE leads the DRAIN team (Ze Xie + Fu Ling + Zhu Ling = THREE water-drainers), BAI ZHU strengthens the Spleen dam, and GUI ZHI opens the yang gate for qi transformation. 'THIRSTY but VOMITS water' = Wu Ling San.
board_pearl: Wu Ling San is THE formula for WATER AMASSMENT (xu shui) — the pathognomonic sign is thirst with vomiting immediately after drinking water (water goes in, comes right back up because Bladder qi transformation is impaired).
formula_math: Wu Ling San = Ze Xie + Si Ling San (Fu Ling, Zhu Ling, Bai Zhu) + Gui Zhi; Wu Ling San + Ping Wei San = Wei Ling Tang

key_modifications.modification: Remove Gui Zhi, add Hua Shi, A Jiao
key_modifications.for: Water accumulation with heat and blood in urine (becomes Zhu Ling Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Ren Shen, Sheng Jiang
key_modifications.for: Spleen qi deficiency with more severe edema

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Ping Wei San
key_modifications.for: Dampness with Spleen distention (Wei Ling Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Yin Chen Hao
key_modifications.for: Jaundice with dampness (Yin Chen Wu Ling San)

clinical_pearls: Gui Zhi is critical — it warms yang to promote qi TRANSFORMATION (qi hua) in the Bladder, not just diuresis.
Shanghan Lun presents this for Taiyang water amassment: the exterior is not fully resolved while water accumulates internally.
One of the most versatile formulas in TCM — used for edema, UTI, diarrhea, ascites, and Meniere's-like dizziness.
The formula promotes urination to treat diarrhea — 'li xiao bian, shi da bian' (promote urination to solidify stool).
contraindications: Yin deficiency with scanty dark urine and heat signs (use Zhu Ling Tang instead)

indications: Water-heat mutual binding with yin deficiency. Urinary difficulty, fever, thirst with desire to drink, diarrhea, cough, nausea, insomnia and irritability. Distinguished from Wu Ling San pattern by the presence of heat signs and yin deficiency.
tongue: Red, white or slightly yellow coating
mnemonic: ZHU LING Tang is Wu Ling San's YIN-FRIENDLY cousin: replaces warming Gui Zhi and Bai Zhu with cooling Hua Shi and nourishing E Jiao. 'DRAIN water but DON'T damage yin' — E Jiao is the safety net.
board_pearl: Zhu Ling Tang = drains water + nourishes yin (E Jiao) + clears heat (Hua Shi) — used instead of Wu Ling San when there is HEAT and YIN DEFICIENCY with water accumulation.
formula_math: Zhu Ling Tang = Wu Ling San - Gui Zhi - Bai Zhu + Hua Shi + E Jiao

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Mao Gen, Xiao Ji
key_modifications.for: Hematuria with dampness

key_modifications.modification: Add Mu Tong, Che Qian Zi
key_modifications.for: More severe urinary difficulty

clinical_pearls: Key distinction from Wu Ling San: Zhu Ling Tang is for HEAT patterns (red tongue, rapid pulse) while Wu Ling San is for COLD-neutral patterns.
E Jiao is added dissolved at the end (yang hua) — it nourishes yin to prevent the diuretic herbs from damaging fluids.
Wu Ling San warms yang (Gui Zhi); Zhu Ling Tang clears heat (Hua Shi) — opposite thermal strategies for water accumulation.
Shanghan Lun specifies: if pulse is floating, use Wu Ling San; if pulse is thin and rapid, use Zhu Ling Tang.
contraindications: No heat signs present (use Wu Ling San)

actions: Regulates qi and strengthens the Spleen
indications: Skin edema (pi shui) — generalized edema that starts in the limbs and face, with a sensation of heaviness, distention in the epigastrium and abdomen, labored and heavy urination.
tongue: White, greasy coating, may be swollen
mnemonic: FIVE PEELS for puffy SKIN (pi): ALL five herbs are PI (peels/bark) — because edema is in the SKIN, use SKIN of herbs to treat it! Sheng Jiang PI, Sang Bai PI, Chen PI, Da Fu PI, Fu Ling PI. Like treats like — skin treats skin.
board_pearl: Wu Pi San uses five PEELS (pi) to treat SKIN edema (pi shui) — the 'like treats like' principle; all herbs are barks or peels because the edema is in the skin layer.

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi, Bai Zhu
key_modifications.for: Spleen qi deficiency with edema

key_modifications.modification: Add Ze Xie, Zhu Ling
key_modifications.for: More severe water retention

key_modifications.modification: Replace Sheng Jiang Pi with Wu Jia Pi
key_modifications.for: Alternative version (some editions list Wu Jia Pi)

clinical_pearls: The use of all peels/barks is based on the theory that peels work on the surface/skin level of the body.
Some editions substitute Wu Jia Pi for one of the peels — both versions are historically valid.
Milder than Wu Ling San and more appropriate for superficial edema without internal water amassment.
Sang Bai Pi specifically addresses Lung qi to regulate the water passages from above.
contraindications: Edema from heart or kidney failure (requires stronger formulas)

indications: Wind-edema or wind-dampness with qi deficiency. Heaviness of the body, edema, sweating with aversion to wind, difficulty urinating, swollen joints with pain. The patient tends to be overweight with a puffy appearance and superficial sweating.
mnemonic: FANG JI + HUANG QI = 'DEFENSE (fang) SHIELD (qi)' — Huang Qi BOOSTS the weak wei qi while Fang Ji DRAINS dampness from below. For the PUFFY, SWEATY, HEAVY patient who looks waterlogged. Think 'heavy person who sweats easily and is swollen.'
board_pearl: Fang Ji Huang Qi Tang is for WIND-EDEMA with QI DEFICIENCY — the patient is overweight, sweats easily, and has edema; Huang Qi tonifies qi while Fang Ji drains dampness.

key_modifications.modification: Add Fu Zi
key_modifications.for: Yang deficiency with more severe edema and cold (Fang Ji Fu Zi Tang direction)

key_modifications.modification: Increase Huang Qi to 30g+
key_modifications.for: More severe qi deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Yi Yi Ren, Cang Zhu
key_modifications.for: Damp bi syndrome with joint pain

clinical_pearls: From Jingui Yaolue — Zhang Zhongjing's formula for wind-water (feng shui) pattern.
The combination of Huang Qi with Fang Ji simultaneously TONIFIES qi and DRAINS dampness — a tonify-and-drain approach.
Modern applications include nephrotic syndrome, cardiac edema, and obesity with fluid retention.
Han Fang Ji (Stephania tetrandra) is used for edema; Mu Fang Ji (Cocculus) is used more for bi syndrome.

indications: Yang-type jaundice (yang huang) from damp-heat. Whole body is bright yellow/orange like a tangerine, especially the sclera. Slight abdominal distention, thirst, difficulty urinating with dark scanty urine, possible fever and sweating that does not resolve the condition.
pulse: Slippery, rapid (hua shu) or deep, rapid, forceful (chen shu you li)
mnemonic: YIN CHEN HAO Tang has only 3 herbs for BRIGHT YELLOW jaundice: Yin Chen Hao (THE jaundice herb) clears damp-heat from the Liver/GB, Zhi Zi drains heat through URINE, Da Huang purges heat through STOOL — two exits for damp-heat to leave.
board_pearl: Yin Chen Hao Tang is THE formula for YANG-type jaundice (bright orange-yellow) from DAMP-HEAT — only 3 herbs: Yin Chen Hao + Zhi Zi (drain via urine) + Da Huang (drain via stool).
formula_math: For yang-type (damp-heat) jaundice = Yin Chen Hao Tang; for yin-type (cold-damp) jaundice = Yin Chen Zhu Fu Tang (Yin Chen Hao + Bai Zhu + Fu Zi + Gan Jiang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Bai, Ze Xie
key_modifications.for: More severe damp-heat in the lower jiao

key_modifications.modification: Add Da Qing Ye, Ban Lan Gen
key_modifications.for: Toxic heat with jaundice (hepatitis)

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Wu Ling San
key_modifications.for: Jaundice with edema and urinary difficulty

clinical_pearls: Critical distinction: YANG jaundice (bright yellow, damp-heat) vs. YIN jaundice (dull, smoky yellow, cold-damp).
Yin Chen Hao has a unique specificity for treating jaundice — it is the single most important herb for this condition.
The two-pathway drainage strategy (urine via Zhi Zi + stool via Da Huang) is characteristic of this formula.
Modern applications include hepatitis, cholecystitis, and gallstones with jaundice.
contraindications: Yin-type (cold-damp) jaundice — dull/smoky yellow coloring (use Yin Chen Zhu Fu Tang)
Jaundice from blood stasis (use different approach)

actions: Promotes urination and unblocks painful urinary dysfunction (lin)
indications: Hot lin syndrome (re lin) — damp-heat in the Bladder. Frequent, urgent, painful urination with burning sensation, dark turbid or bloody urine, distention and fullness in the lower abdomen, dry mouth and throat, possible constipation.
mnemonic: EIGHT herbs RECTIFY the urinary tract: 'MU Tong + HUA Shi + CHE Qian + QU Mai + BIAN Xu' = FIVE urination promoters flooding the Bladder, plus ZHI ZI drains fire, DA HUANG purges heat from below, and DENG XIN CAO lights the way through the urethra. UTI = Ba Zheng San.
board_pearl: Ba Zheng San is THE formula for HOT LIN (re lin/damp-heat UTI) — eight herbs that clear heat and promote urination for painful, burning, frequent urination with dark urine.

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Mao Gen, Xiao Ji
key_modifications.for: Hematuria (blood lin)

key_modifications.modification: Add Hai Jin Sha, Ji Nei Jin
key_modifications.for: Stones (stone lin)

key_modifications.modification: Reduce Da Huang, add Sheng Di
key_modifications.for: Milder heat or slight yin deficiency

clinical_pearls: The most comprehensive and commonly used formula for acute urinary tract infections in TCM.
Note: Mu Tong — modern practice typically substitutes Guan Mu Tong (Aristolochia) with Chuan Mu Tong (Clematis) due to nephrotoxicity concerns with aristolochic acid.
Da Huang serves dual purpose: purges heat from the bowels AND provides an additional route to drain damp-heat.
Not for long-term use — this is a strong clearing formula that can damage Spleen qi and yin if used excessively.
contraindications: Lin syndrome from deficiency (qi or yin deficiency)
Elderly or debilitated patients without modification

indications: Damp-heat in the lower jiao. Red, hot, swollen joints of the lower extremities, atrophy (wei) of the lower limbs, foul-smelling vaginal discharge, damp-heat skin lesions of the lower body, foul-smelling foot sores.
mnemonic: TWO MARVELS: HUANG BAI (clears heat from the LOWER jiao like a cold river) + CANG ZHU (dries dampness like a hot sun) = the simplest damp-heat formula. Just TWO herbs, both MARVELOUS for lower body damp-heat.
board_pearl: Er Miao San = Huang Bai + Cang Zhu — the foundational TWO-herb pair for DAMP-HEAT in the LOWER JIAO; it is the BASE that builds into San Miao San and Si Miao San.
formula_math: Er Miao San + Niu Xi = San Miao San; Er Miao San + Niu Xi + Yi Yi Ren = Si Miao San

key_modifications.modification: Add Niu Xi
key_modifications.for: Lower limb weakness and pain (becomes San Miao San)

key_modifications.modification: Add Niu Xi + Yi Yi Ren
key_modifications.for: Lower limb damp-heat with heaviness (becomes Si Miao San)

key_modifications.modification: Add Bi Xie, Yi Yi Ren
key_modifications.for: More severe dampness with turbid urination

clinical_pearls: The most fundamental formula for lower jiao damp-heat — a building block for more complex formulas.
Huang Bai is bitter and cold (clears heat); Cang Zhu is aromatic and warm (dries dampness) — the pairing perfectly addresses both aspects of damp-heat.
Modern applications include gout, eczema of the lower extremities, vaginal infections, and arthritis of the knees.

actions: Strengthens the sinews and bones of the lower extremities
indications: Damp-heat in the lower jiao with weakness, atrophy, or pain of the lower limbs. Swollen, painful knees, lower extremity weakness or atrophy (wei zheng), difficulty walking, numbness of the feet.
mnemonic: THREE MARVELS = Er Miao San + the KNEE hero NIU XI — the OX KNEE (Niu Xi literally means ox knee) directs the formula to the LOWER LIMBS and strengthens sinews/bones. For damp-heat settling in the KNEES and LEGS.
board_pearl: San Miao San = Er Miao San + Niu Xi — the addition of Niu Xi specifically targets the LOWER LIMBS, making it the go-to formula for damp-heat causing knee pain, lower limb weakness, or wei (atrophy) syndrome.
formula_math: San Miao San = Er Miao San (Huang Bai + Cang Zhu) + Niu Xi; San Miao San + Yi Yi Ren = Si Miao San

key_modifications.modification: Add Yi Yi Ren
key_modifications.for: More dampness with heaviness (becomes Si Miao San)

key_modifications.modification: Add Mu Gua, Wei Ling Xian
key_modifications.for: Bi syndrome with more severe joint pain

key_modifications.modification: Add Du Huo, Sang Ji Sheng
key_modifications.for: Lower back and knee pain with Kidney deficiency

clinical_pearls: Niu Xi has dual function: it directs the formula downward to the lower limbs AND strengthens sinews and bones.
The progression Er Miao -> San Miao -> Si Miao represents increasing specificity for lower limb damp-heat conditions.
Wei zheng (atrophy syndrome) of the lower limbs from damp-heat is a classic indication — heat damages sinews and muscles.
Modern applications include gout, rheumatoid arthritis of the knees, and lower extremity neuropathy.
contraindications: Pregnancy (Niu Xi moves blood and directs downward)
Wei syndrome from pure yin/blood deficiency without damp-heat

indications: Middle Jiao deficiency cold with diarrhea with watery stool, nausea and vomiting, no appetite, abdominal pain that responds favorably to warmth and pressure, inability to taste food, cold extremities, lassitude, desire to sleep.
mnemonic: LI ZHONG regulates the middle: 'Gentle Brides Require Zest' — Gan Jiang, Bai Zhu, Ren Shen, Zhi Gan Cao warm and tonify a cold, weak Spleen.
board_pearl: Li Zhong Wan is the foundational formula for middle Jiao deficiency cold — all four herbs in equal doses warm and tonify the Spleen and Stomach.
formula_math: Li Zhong Wan + Fu Zi = Fu Zi Li Zhong Wan (adds Kidney Yang warming for more severe cold)

key_modifications.modification: Add Fu Zi
key_modifications.for: Severe cold with devastated Yang (Fu Zi Li Zhong Wan)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi and Dang Gui
key_modifications.for: Bleeding from middle Jiao deficiency cold

key_modifications.modification: Add Wu Mei and Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: Roundworm infestation with vomiting

clinical_pearls: All four herbs are in equal dosage — a hallmark of this formula.
This is the pill form of Ren Shen Tang (decoction); same ingredients, different preparation.
Distinguished from Si Ni Tang because it focuses on Spleen Yang deficiency rather than Kidney Yang collapse.

actions: Warms and tonifies the middle Jiao
indications: Consumptive deficiency with intermittent spasmodic abdominal pain that responds favorably to warmth and pressure, lassitude, reduced appetite, low-grade fever, palpitations with irritability, dry mouth and throat, sore and aching extremities.
mnemonic: XIAO JIAN ZHONG: 'Gui Zhi Tang with DOUBLE Bai Shao and MALTOSE' — the sweet maltose builds the middle while doubled peony stops the cramping.
board_pearl: Xiao Jian Zhong Tang is Gui Zhi Tang with doubled Bai Shao and added Yi Tang (maltose) — key formula for spasmodic abdominal pain from middle Jiao deficiency cold.
formula_math: Gui Zhi Tang + Yi Tang + extra Bai Shao (doubled) = Xiao Jian Zhong Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Dang Gui
key_modifications.for: Blood deficiency (becomes Dang Gui Jian Zhong Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi
key_modifications.for: Qi deficiency with spontaneous sweating (becomes Huang Qi Jian Zhong Tang)

clinical_pearls: Bai Shao is doubled from Gui Zhi Tang to emphasize the antispasmodic and pain-relieving action.
Yi Tang (maltose) is the chief herb — it nourishes the middle and relaxes spasm.
Distinguished from Da Jian Zhong Tang: Xiao is milder, for deficiency with spasm; Da is for severe cold with violent pain and vomiting.
contraindications: Vomiting or abdominal distension from excess
Caution with patients who have diabetes due to Yi Tang

actions: Warms and tonifies the middle Jiao deficiency
indications: Severe middle Jiao deficiency cold with epigastric and abdominal pain so severe the patient cannot tolerate being touched, strong sensation of cold in the epigastrium, borborygmus, vomiting to the point of being unable to eat, may see the movement of intestines through the abdominal wall.
tongue: Pale tongue with white slippery coating
mnemonic: DA JIAN ZHONG: 'Ginger, Pepper, Ginseng, Sugar' — when cold is so severe you can SEE the intestines writhing through the belly wall, bring the big guns: hot Shu Jiao pepper.
board_pearl: Da Jian Zhong Tang is for severe interior cold with visible intestinal writhing and violent abdominal pain — Shu Jiao (Sichuan pepper) is the unique chief herb.

key_modifications.modification: Add Fu Zi
key_modifications.for: Extremely severe cold with Yang collapse

key_modifications.modification: Add Ban Xia
key_modifications.for: Pronounced nausea and vomiting

clinical_pearls: The hallmark indication is visible intestinal peristalsis through the abdominal wall — a sign of extreme interior cold.
Shu Jiao (Sichuan pepper) is acrid and hot, uniquely dispersing cold and killing pain in the middle Jiao.
Da Jian Zhong is for severe cold with violent pain; Xiao Jian Zhong is for milder deficiency cold with cramping.

actions: Warms and tonifies the Liver and Stomach
indications: Liver and Stomach deficiency cold with dry retching or vomiting immediately after eating, indeterminate gnawing hunger, acid reflux, vertex headache with nausea and vomiting (Jue Yin headache), cold hands and feet. Also for vomiting and diarrhea with cold hands and feet and agitation.
tongue: Pale tongue with white slippery coating
mnemonic: WU ZHU YU TANG: 'Evodia with Ginseng dates and ginger' — VERTEX headache with VOMITING screams Jue Yin cold rising; Evodia is the only herb that goes to the vertex and warms the Liver.
board_pearl: Wu Zhu Yu Tang is THE formula for vertex headache with vomiting — the hallmark of Jue Yin (Liver channel) cold rising upward.

key_modifications.modification: Add Ban Xia
key_modifications.for: Severe vomiting with phlegm

key_modifications.modification: Add Dang Gui and Chuan Xiong
key_modifications.for: Vertex headache with blood deficiency

clinical_pearls: Three classic presentations in Shang Han Lun: (1) Yang Ming — dry retching with foamy saliva and headache; (2) Shao Yin — vomiting with cold hands/feet; (3) Jue Yin — vomiting and diarrhea with vertex headache.
Sheng Jiang dose is double Wu Zhu Yu — emphasizing the anti-emetic action.
Vertex headache = Jue Yin = Liver channel pathology = Wu Zhu Yu Tang — a board favorite.
contraindications: Vomiting from Stomach heat or Stomach Yin deficiency
Headache from Liver Yang rising with heat signs

indications: Kidney Yang devastation with extremely cold extremities (frigid past the elbows and knees), aversion to cold, lying curled up, lassitude with desire to sleep, vomiting, diarrhea with undigested food, abdominal pain and cold, no thirst or desire for hot drinks.
tongue: Pale tongue with white slippery coating
pulse: Deep, thin, faint pulse at the verge of expiring
mnemonic: SI NI TANG: 'FuZi Gan Jiang Gan Cao' — only THREE herbs to rescue FOUR frigid limbs. Fu Zi rescues Yang from the abyss, Gan Jiang warms the middle, Gan Cao harmonizes.
board_pearl: Si Ni Tang is THE emergency rescue formula for Yang collapse (devastated Kidney Yang) — frigid extremities past the elbows and knees with a faint, expiring pulse.
formula_math: Si Ni Tang + Ren Shen = Si Ni Jia Ren Shen Tang (adds Qi rescue to Yang rescue)

key_modifications.modification: Add Ren Shen
key_modifications.for: Severe Qi collapse alongside Yang collapse (Si Ni Jia Ren Shen Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Replace Gan Jiang with Sheng Jiang and add Cong Bai
key_modifications.for: Milder Yang deficiency with diarrhea (Tong Mai Si Ni Tang variation)

key_modifications.modification: Increase Fu Zi dose
key_modifications.for: More severe Yang devastation

clinical_pearls: Si Ni means 'four counterflows' referring to frigid extremities — all four limbs are cold past the elbows and knees.
Fu Zi is the critical herb: it enters all 12 channels and rescues devastated Yang — no substitute exists.
Distinguished from Li Zhong Wan: Si Ni treats Kidney Yang collapse (life-threatening); Li Zhong treats Spleen Yang deficiency (chronic).
Do NOT confuse with Si Ni San (Frigid Extremities Powder) which treats Liver Qi constraint — completely different mechanism.
contraindications: Caution with pregnancy due to Fu Zi

actions: Warms the channels and disperses cold
indications: Blood deficiency with cold in the channels causing cold hands and feet (not frigid past the elbows/knees like Si Ni Tang), pain and cold in the lower back and legs, cold lower abdomen, frostbite, Raynaud's phenomenon, dysmenorrhea from blood deficiency and cold.
pulse: Deep, thin pulse or pulse barely perceptible at the cun position
mnemonic: DANG GUI SI NI: 'Gui Zhi Tang swaps Sheng Jiang for Dang Gui + Xi Xin + Mu Tong' — when cold fingers aren't from Yang collapse but from BLOOD DEFICIENCY failing to warm the channels.
board_pearl: Dang Gui Si Ni Tang treats cold extremities from Blood deficiency and cold in the channels — NOT Yang collapse. Key differentiator from Si Ni Tang.
formula_math: Gui Zhi Tang - Sheng Jiang + Dang Gui + Xi Xin + Mu Tong = Dang Gui Si Ni Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Wu Zhu Yu and Sheng Jiang
key_modifications.for: Internal cold accumulation with chronic cold conditions

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi
key_modifications.for: Additional Qi deficiency

clinical_pearls: Key distinction: Si Ni Tang = Yang collapse (frigid past elbows/knees, faint pulse); Dang Gui Si Ni Tang = Blood deficiency cold (cold fingers/toes, thin pulse).
Xi Xin penetrates deeply to disperse cold lodged in the channels — only 3g needed.
Modern use includes Raynaud's disease, frostbite, and peripheral circulatory disorders.
Mu Tong unblocks the channels and vessels to allow warming herbs to penetrate.
contraindications: Yang collapse — use Si Ni Tang instead

pinyin: Huang Qi Gui Zhi Wu Wu Tang
english_name: Astragalus and Cinnamon Twig Five-Substance Decoction
indications: Blood Bi (painful obstruction) from Qi and Blood deficiency with numbness of the body and extremities, muscle weakness, difficulty with movement, superficial edema, may have a sensation of wind or crawling on the skin.
pulse: Thin, rough or choppy pulse; or tight, large pulse without strength
mnemonic: HUANG QI GUI ZHI WU WU: 'Gui Zhi Tang minus Gan Cao plus Huang Qi' — only FIVE substances for BLOOD BI numbness. Huang Qi pushes Qi to the surface to unblock numb limbs.
board_pearl: Huang Qi Gui Zhi Wu Wu Tang is THE formula for Blood Bi — numbness and muscle weakness from Qi/Blood deficiency failing to nourish the channels.
formula_math: Gui Zhi Tang - Zhi Gan Cao + Huang Qi = Huang Qi Gui Zhi Wu Wu Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Dang Gui and Chuan Xiong
key_modifications.for: More pronounced blood deficiency and stasis

key_modifications.modification: Add Fang Feng and Qiang Huo
key_modifications.for: Accompanying wind-damp painful obstruction

clinical_pearls: Wu Wu means 'five substances' — exactly five ingredients.
From the Jin Gui Yao Lue — originally for 'Blood Bi' presenting as numbness rather than pain.
Sheng Jiang is doubled to 18g to drive the formula to the exterior channels.
Modern applications include diabetic neuropathy, post-stroke numbness, and carpal tunnel syndrome.
contraindications: Bi syndrome from excess, heat, or dampness

indications: Spleen and Stomach Qi deficiency with pallid complexion, low and soft voice, reduced appetite, loose stools, weakness of the limbs.
mnemonic: SI JUN ZI: 'Four Gentlemen — Ren Shen, Bai Zhu, Fu Ling, Gan Cao' — the gentlest, most BASIC Qi tonic. Think of four well-mannered gentlemen politely strengthening your Spleen.
board_pearl: Si Jun Zi Tang is the BASE formula for all Qi tonification — the Qi counterpart to Si Wu Tang for Blood.
formula_math: Si Jun Zi Tang + Chen Pi + Ban Xia = Liu Jun Zi Tang; Si Jun Zi Tang + Si Wu Tang = Ba Zhen Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Chen Pi and Ban Xia
key_modifications.for: Phlegm and dampness with nausea (Liu Jun Zi Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi and Dang Gui
key_modifications.for: More severe Qi deficiency with blood deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Shan Yao and Lian Zi
key_modifications.for: Spleen deficiency with diarrhea

clinical_pearls: This is the most fundamental Qi-tonifying formula — the 'mother' of many derivative formulas.
Si Jun Zi Tang is to Qi what Si Wu Tang is to Blood — learn them as a pair.
All four herbs are gentle and balanced ('gentlemen') — no harsh or extreme actions.

indications: Spleen and Stomach Qi deficiency with phlegm and dampness causing reduced appetite, nausea or vomiting, focal distension in the chest and epigastrium, coughing with copious thin white sputum.
tongue: Pale tongue with white greasy coating
mnemonic: LIU JUN ZI: 'Four Gentlemen invite two more guests: Chen Pi and Ban Xia' — when Qi deficiency breeds PHLEGM, add the dynamic duo of phlegm transformation.
board_pearl: Liu Jun Zi Tang = Si Jun Zi Tang + Er Chen Tang base (Chen Pi + Ban Xia) — tonifies Qi AND resolves phlegm from Spleen deficiency.
formula_math: Si Jun Zi Tang + Chen Pi + Ban Xia = Liu Jun Zi Tang; Liu Jun Zi Tang + Mu Xiang + Sha Ren = Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Mu Xiang and Sha Ren
key_modifications.for: Qi stagnation with bloating (Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhu Ru
key_modifications.for: Nausea with heat signs

comparison_formulas: Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi Tang
clinical_pearls: When Spleen Qi deficiency generates phlegm and dampness, Si Jun Zi alone is insufficient — Liu Jun Zi addresses both root and branch.
Ban Xia and Chen Pi form the core of Er Chen Tang, making this formula a hybrid of tonification and phlegm resolution.
Commonly used as a base formula in modern oncology support for chemotherapy-related nausea and appetite loss.

pinyin: Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi Tang
indications: Spleen and Stomach Qi deficiency with phlegm-dampness AND Qi stagnation causing epigastric and abdominal distension and pain, belching, reduced appetite, nausea, vomiting, loose stools.
tongue: Pale tongue with white greasy coating
mnemonic: XIANG SHA LIU JUN ZI: 'Six Gentlemen plus AROMATIC movers Mu Xiang and Sha Ren' — when Qi is so weak it STAGNATES, add aromatic herbs to get things moving again.
board_pearl: Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi Tang adds Qi-moving aromatics (Mu Xiang + Sha Ren) to Liu Jun Zi Tang for Spleen Qi deficiency complicated by Qi stagnation and bloating.
formula_math: Liu Jun Zi Tang + Mu Xiang + Sha Ren = Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi Tang

key_modifications.modification: Increase Mu Xiang
key_modifications.for: More pronounced Qi stagnation with pain

key_modifications.modification: Add Huo Xiang
key_modifications.for: Dampness with nausea

clinical_pearls: The progression Si Jun Zi → Liu Jun Zi → Xiang Sha Liu Jun Zi represents increasing complexity: Qi deficiency → with phlegm → with phlegm AND stagnation.
Mu Xiang and Sha Ren are aromatic and warm, preventing the cloying nature of tonifying herbs from causing stagnation.
Sha Ren should be added near the end of decoction to preserve its volatile oils.

english_name: Tonify the Middle and Augment the Qi Decoction
indications: Spleen and Stomach Qi deficiency with sinking of middle Qi causing organ prolapse (uterus, rectum, stomach), chronic diarrhea, shortness of breath, laconic speech, limbs lacking strength, shiny pale complexion, spontaneous sweating, aversion to cold, desire for warm drinks. Also for Qi deficiency fever (intermittent or chronic low-grade fever that worsens with exertion).
tongue: Pale tongue with thin white coating
pulse: Flooding, large, forceless pulse at the proximal position; or deficient, soft pulse
mnemonic: BU ZHONG YI QI: 'Huang Qi leads the LIFTING team — Sheng Ma and Chai Hu are the ELEVATORS that raise sunken organs.' When your uterus/rectum falls, LIFT with Bu Zhong.
board_pearl: Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang is THE formula for sinking/prolapse from Qi deficiency — Sheng Ma and Chai Hu in small doses RAISE the Yang Qi. Also treats Qi deficiency fever.
formula_math: Si Jun Zi Tang + Huang Qi + Dang Gui + Chen Pi + Sheng Ma + Chai Hu = Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Ke (枳壳)
key_modifications.for: Severe prolapse — Zhi Ke pushes up from below while Sheng Ma/Chai Hu lift from above

key_modifications.modification: Add Ge Gen
key_modifications.for: Chronic diarrhea from Spleen Qi sinking

key_modifications.modification: Increase Huang Qi dose
key_modifications.for: More severe Qi deficiency or sweating

clinical_pearls: Li Dong-Yuan's masterpiece formula — embodies his theory that internal damage from Spleen/Stomach deficiency causes disease.
Sheng Ma and Chai Hu in SMALL doses (3-6g) raise Qi; in large doses they release the exterior — dosage determines function.
Qi deficiency fever is a classic indication often missed: intermittent low-grade fever that worsens with fatigue, improved by rest.
Huang Qi is the chief and in the highest dose — it is the primary Qi tonifier and Yang raiser.
contraindications: Yin deficiency with heat signs — raising Yang will worsen heat

indications: Qi and Yin deficiency of the Lung with chronic cough with sparse sputum that is difficult to expectorate, shortness of breath, spontaneous sweating, dry mouth and tongue. Also for summerheat injuring Qi and Yin with profuse sweating, lassitude, and a deficient rapid pulse.
tongue: Pale red, dry tongue with thin coating
pulse: Deficient, rapid pulse or thin, weak pulse; this formula 'generates' (revives) the pulse
mnemonic: SHENG MAI SAN: 'Only THREE herbs GENERATE the PULSE: Ren Shen pumps Qi, Mai Dong moistens Yin, Wu Wei Zi SEALS the leaking sweat.' Three herbs, one dying pulse, revival.
board_pearl: Sheng Mai San tonifies Qi AND Yin simultaneously with just three herbs — the go-to formula for Qi and Yin deficiency with spontaneous sweating. Its injectable form (Shen Mai) is used for cardiogenic shock.

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi
key_modifications.for: More severe Qi deficiency with sweating

key_modifications.modification: Add Di Huang and Dang Gui
key_modifications.for: Blood deficiency accompanying Qi and Yin deficiency

clinical_pearls: Only three herbs but profoundly effective — the minimalist masterpiece of Qi and Yin tonification.
Wu Wei Zi is sour and astringent — it 'locks the door' to prevent further leaking of Qi and fluids through sweating.
Modern injectable form (Shen Mai injection) is used in Chinese hospitals for cardiac emergencies.
The name 'Generate the Pulse' reflects its ability to revive a fading, weak pulse by restoring Qi and fluids.
contraindications: Early-stage febrile disease when pathogen has not been cleared

indications: Wei Qi deficiency with spontaneous sweating, aversion to drafts, recurrent colds and wind invasions, shiny pale complexion.
mnemonic: YU PING FENG: 'A JADE WINDSCREEN of just three: Huang Qi builds the wall, Bai Zhu cements it, Fang Feng patrols for Wind.' Three herbs form an impenetrable shield against recurrent colds.
board_pearl: Yu Ping Feng San has only THREE herbs that stabilize the Wei Qi — the immune-boosting formula for patients who catch every cold. Fang Feng is used paradoxically: a wind-expelling herb used to PREVENT wind invasion.

key_modifications.modification: Add Mu Li and Fu Xiao Mai
key_modifications.for: More severe spontaneous sweating

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Gui Zhi Tang
key_modifications.for: Wei Qi deficiency with active wind-cold invasion

clinical_pearls: Original powder proportions: Huang Qi 1 : Bai Zhu 2 : Fang Feng 2, but modern decoction doses vary.
The paradox of Fang Feng: it expels wind but is used here to PREVENT wind — in small doses within a tonifying formula, it scouts for and deflects pathogenic wind.
Modern research supports immunomodulatory effects — used for allergic rhinitis, recurrent URIs, and IgA nephropathy.
Distinguished from Gui Zhi Tang: Yu Ping Feng San is for PREVENTION (no active pathogen); Gui Zhi Tang is for TREATMENT (active wind-cold).
Also categorized under: Stabilize and Bind
Cross-reference: Also appears in Tonify Qi category — its primary mechanism is augmenting Wei Qi.
Fang Feng is the Zuo herb that both expels residual wind and prevents Huang Qi from being too cloying.
Classic ratio in the original text is 1:2:2 (Huang Qi : Bai Zhu : Fang Feng), though modern clinical use often increases Huang Qi.
Bensky emphasizes this formula's role in prevention rather than treatment of active invasion.
contraindications: Active exterior invasion — resolve the exterior first

indications: Spleen Qi deficiency with internal dampness causing loose stools or diarrhea, reduced appetite, chest and epigastric stifling sensation, pallid and wan complexion, weakness of the limbs, thin body, may have vomiting.
tongue: Pale tongue with white greasy coating
mnemonic: SHEN LING BAI ZHU: 'Si Jun Zi Tang goes to the FARM — adds Shan Yao yams, Lian Zi lotus, Bai Bian Dou beans, Yi Yi Ren barley, and Sha Ren spice.' Wholesome Spleen food for chronic diarrhea from dampness.
board_pearl: Shen Ling Bai Zhu San = Si Jun Zi Tang base + herbs that leach dampness and strengthen Spleen. The go-to formula for Spleen Qi deficiency with DIARRHEA from dampness. Jie Geng opens the Lung to help the Spleen spread Qi.
formula_math: Si Jun Zi Tang + Shan Yao + Bai Bian Dou + Lian Zi + Yi Yi Ren + Sha Ren + Jie Geng = Shen Ling Bai Zhu San

key_modifications.modification: Add Huo Xiang and Pei Lan
key_modifications.for: More pronounced dampness with nausea

key_modifications.modification: Add Cang Zhu
key_modifications.for: Heavy turbid dampness

clinical_pearls: Jie Geng is an unusual inclusion: it opens and raises Lung Qi, which facilitates the Spleen's dispersing function — the Lung and Spleen work together.
Distinguished from Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang: Shen Ling Bai Zhu San focuses on Spleen deficiency with DAMPNESS; Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang focuses on Spleen deficiency with SINKING.
Many of the added herbs (Shan Yao, Lian Zi, Bai Bian Dou, Yi Yi Ren) are food-grade — this formula is extremely gentle and suitable for long-term use.
The formula gently leaches dampness downward through bland herbs while tonifying the Spleen to address the root cause of dampness.

indications: Blood deficiency with generalized muscle tension, dizziness, blurred vision, lusterless complexion and nails, dry skin and hair. Also for any Blood deficiency menstrual disorders: irregular menstruation, dysmenorrhea, amenorrhea with a pale complexion.
tongue: Pale tongue with thin white coating or no coating
pulse: Thin, wiry or thin, choppy pulse
mnemonic: SI WU TANG: 'Four Women's Substances — Shu Di nourishes, Dang Gui moves, Bai Shao preserves, Chuan Xiong invigorates.' The QUEEN of Blood formulas, the feminine counterpart to Si Jun Zi's masculine Qi.
board_pearl: Si Wu Tang is the BASE formula for Blood tonification — the Blood counterpart to Si Jun Zi Tang for Qi. Chuan Xiong prevents blood stasis from all the rich tonics.
formula_math: Si Wu Tang + Si Jun Zi Tang = Ba Zhen Tang; Si Wu Tang + Tao Ren + Hong Hua = Tao Hong Si Wu Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Tao Ren and Hong Hua
key_modifications.for: Blood stasis (Tao Hong Si Wu Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Replace Bai Shao with Chi Shao and replace Shu Di with Sheng Di
key_modifications.for: Blood heat patterns

key_modifications.modification: Add Ai Ye and E Jiao
key_modifications.for: Uterine bleeding from deficiency cold (Jiao Ai Tang)

clinical_pearls: Si Wu Tang is to Blood what Si Jun Zi Tang is to Qi — the foundational pair for tonification.
Chuan Xiong is the 'blood within Qi' herb — it moves Qi to move Blood, preventing the heavy, cloying tonics from causing stagnation.
This formula primarily nourishes Liver Blood — the Liver stores Blood and governs menstruation.
Can be modified extensively: Tao Hong Si Wu Tang (+ blood stasis), Shao Yao Si Wu Tang (+ pain), etc.
contraindications: Yin deficiency with heat — Shu Di may be too cloying
Spleen deficiency with loose stools — the rich, greasy herbs may worsen digestion
Active bleeding from heat in the Blood

english_name: Tangkuei Decoction to Tonify the Blood
indications: Blood deficiency with Qi deficiency presenting as fever, hot sensation in the muscles, red face, irritability, thirst with a desire for warm drinks (key differentiator), headache. Also for sores or ulcers that do not heal, or heavy uterine bleeding from Qi deficiency failing to hold Blood.
pulse: Flooding, large, deficient pulse — feels excess but is actually deficient
mnemonic: DANG GUI BU XUE: 'Only TWO herbs in a 5:1 ratio — Huang Qi 30g DWARFS Dang Gui 6g.' The paradox: to TONIFY BLOOD, use 5x more QI tonic! Qi is the commander of Blood.
board_pearl: Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang uses a 5:1 ratio of Huang Qi to Dang Gui — the key exam point is that to generate Blood you must first tonify Qi, because 'Qi generates Blood.' The flooding-large-deficient pulse mimics excess heat but is actually severe deficiency.

key_modifications.modification: Add Shu Di Huang and Bai Shao
key_modifications.for: More severe Blood deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Ren Shen
key_modifications.for: Severe Qi collapse

clinical_pearls: The 5:1 ratio (Huang Qi : Dang Gui) embodies the principle 'Qi generates Blood' — without Qi, Blood cannot be produced.
The fever and flooding pulse mimic a heat pattern, but the desire for WARM drinks reveals it as deficiency — a classic 'true cold, false heat' presentation.
Li Dong-Yuan's creation — just two herbs, yet profoundly illustrates the Qi-Blood relationship.
Board trap: Do not confuse this false heat with true excess heat — the desire for warm drinks and deficient quality of the pulse are the clues.

indications: Heart and Spleen deficiency with both Qi and Blood deficiency causing palpitations, anxiety, forgetfulness, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep, reduced appetite, pallid wan complexion, lassitude. Also for Spleen not controlling Blood causing chronic bleeding: bloody stools, uterine bleeding, early periods with pale dilute blood, or subcutaneous purpura.
tongue: Pale tongue with thin white coating
mnemonic: GUI PI TANG: 'Restore the Spleen to nourish the Heart' — Si Jun Zi base + Heart-calming herbs (Suan Zao Ren, Yuan Zhi, Long Yan Rou). When overthinking DRAINS the Spleen and DISTURBS the Heart, causing insomnia, anxiety, AND bleeding.
board_pearl: Gui Pi Tang treats the Spleen-Heart axis: Spleen Qi deficiency causes Blood deficiency which fails to nourish the Heart (insomnia, palpitations, anxiety) AND fails to hold Blood in the vessels (chronic bleeding).
formula_math: Si Jun Zi Tang + Huang Qi + Dang Gui + Long Yan Rou + Suan Zao Ren + Yuan Zhi + Mu Xiang + Sheng Jiang + Da Zao = Gui Pi Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add E Jiao and Ai Ye
key_modifications.for: More severe uterine bleeding

key_modifications.modification: Add Chai Hu and Zhi Zi (becomes Jia Wei Gui Pi Tang)
key_modifications.for: Liver depression with heat transforming from constraint

key_modifications.modification: Add Dang Gui and more Huang Qi
key_modifications.for: More severe blood deficiency

clinical_pearls: Mu Xiang is included to prevent the many cloying tonics from causing Qi stagnation — it 'keeps things moving.'
Gui Pi Tang vs. Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan: Gui Pi nourishes Heart through Spleen (Qi and Blood deficiency); Tian Wang nourishes Heart through Kidney (Yin deficiency with heat).
Two main presentations: (1) Heart-Spleen deficiency with insomnia/anxiety; (2) Spleen not controlling Blood with chronic bleeding.
Yuan Zhi and Suan Zao Ren are the key Heart-calming pair — they nourish Heart Blood and calm the Shen.
Also categorized under: Calm Spirit
Cross-reference: Also appears in Tonify Blood category — its ability to treat bleeding from Spleen not controlling Blood places it in both categories.
Mu Xiang is included to prevent the heavy tonifying herbs from causing stagnation — the single Qi-regulating herb in the formula.
The hallmark distinction: if insomnia + poor appetite + possible bleeding → Gui Pi Tang. If insomnia + dry throat + night sweats → Suan Zao Ren Tang or Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan.
Long Yan Rou (longan fruit) nourishes Heart Blood and calms the spirit — it is the sweet, fruity herb that distinguishes this formula.
contraindications: Yin deficiency with heat signs — use Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan instead

indications: Dual Qi and Blood deficiency from chronic illness, excessive blood loss, or insufficient nourishment causing pallid or sallow complexion, dizziness, reduced appetite, shortness of breath, laconic speech, palpitations, lassitude.
tongue: Pale tongue with thin white coating
pulse: Thin, frail or thin, choppy pulse
mnemonic: BA ZHEN TANG: 'EIGHT treasures = FOUR Gentlemen + FOUR Substances.' Qi and Blood deficiency? Combine the two foundational formulas into one powerhouse.
board_pearl: Ba Zhen Tang = Si Jun Zi Tang + Si Wu Tang. The simplest and most elegant formula for dual Qi and Blood deficiency.
formula_math: Si Jun Zi Tang + Si Wu Tang = Ba Zhen Tang; Ba Zhen Tang + Huang Qi + Rou Gui = Shi Quan Da Bu Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qi and Rou Gui
key_modifications.for: More severe deficiency with cold (Shi Quan Da Bu Tang)

key_modifications.modification: Add Chen Pi
key_modifications.for: Qi stagnation with bloating from the tonics

clinical_pearls: The 'formula math' of Ba Zhen Tang (4+4=8) is a classic board question.
Ba Zhen Tang is the foundation upon which Shi Quan Da Bu Tang and Ren Shen Yang Ying Tang are built.
Often used postpartum or post-surgery when both Qi and Blood are depleted.
The elegance is in its simplicity: two perfectly balanced foundational formulas combined.

actions: Warms and tonifies Qi and Blood
indications: Qi and Blood deficiency with Yang deficiency presenting as chronic non-healing sores and ulcers, or deficiency following illness/surgery with fatigue, reduced appetite, persistent low-grade fever, or night sweats, cold extremities, a sallow complexion.
tongue: Pale tongue with thin white coating
mnemonic: SHI QUAN DA BU: 'TEN ingredients for TOTAL tonification = Ba Zhen (8) + Huang Qi + Rou Gui (2 warmers).' When eight treasures aren't enough, add warmth with astragalus and cinnamon bark.
board_pearl: Shi Quan Da Bu Tang = Ba Zhen Tang + Huang Qi + Rou Gui. It adds Yang-warming to the Qi and Blood tonification of Ba Zhen Tang — used for deficiency that includes cold signs.
formula_math: Ba Zhen Tang + Huang Qi + Rou Gui = Shi Quan Da Bu Tang (8+2=10, 'Shi Quan' means 'ten complete')

key_modifications.modification: Add Lu Rong
key_modifications.for: Severe Kidney Yang deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Chen Pi and Sha Ren
key_modifications.for: Qi stagnation from the heavy tonics

clinical_pearls: Shi Quan = 'ten complete' — all ten herbs work together for comprehensive tonification.
Rou Gui (cinnamon bark) is the key addition over Ba Zhen Tang: it warms the Kidney Yang and ensures the tonifying herbs are properly transformed.
Used in cancer supportive care in both Chinese and Japanese (Kampo: Juzentaihoto) medicine.
Distinguished from Ren Shen Yang Ying Tang: Shi Quan is broader Yang warming; Ren Shen Yang Ying Tang adds Heart-calming and Lung-nourishing.

english_name: Ginseng Decoction to Nourish the Nutritive Qi
indications: Qi and Blood deficiency with deficient Ying (nutritive) Qi causing lassitude, reduced appetite, emaciation, persistent cough, palpitations, insomnia, forgetfulness, night sweats, intermittent fever, or chronic non-healing sores.
mnemonic: REN SHEN YANG YING: 'Shi Quan Da Bu minus Chuan Xiong plus Wu Wei Zi, Yuan Zhi, Chen Pi' — swaps the blood-mover for Heart-calmers. When deficiency causes INSOMNIA and COUGH alongside fatigue.
board_pearl: Ren Shen Yang Ying Tang is Shi Quan Da Bu Tang minus Chuan Xiong, plus Wu Wei Zi, Yuan Zhi, and Chen Pi — it shifts focus toward nourishing the Heart Shen and containing Lung Qi leakage (cough, night sweats).
formula_math: Shi Quan Da Bu Tang - Chuan Xiong + Wu Wei Zi + Yuan Zhi + Chen Pi = Ren Shen Yang Ying Tang

key_modifications.modification: Add Suan Zao Ren
key_modifications.for: Severe insomnia

key_modifications.modification: Add Mai Men Dong
key_modifications.for: Yin deficiency with dry cough

clinical_pearls: Chuan Xiong is REMOVED from Shi Quan Da Bu Tang — its ascending, dispersing nature is counterproductive when trying to contain and nourish.
Wu Wei Zi astringes Lung Qi (stops cough and night sweats) while Yuan Zhi calms the Heart Shen (insomnia, palpitations).
Chen Pi prevents stagnation from the many rich, cloying tonics.
Use when Shi Quan Da Bu Tang is indicated but the patient also has insomnia, cough, and night sweats.
contraindications: Yin deficiency with pronounced heat signs

actions: Enriches and nourishes Kidney and Liver Yin
indications: Kidney and Liver Yin deficiency with soreness and weakness of the lower back and knees, dizziness, vertigo, tinnitus, diminished hearing, night sweats, spontaneous or nocturnal emissions, tidal fever, heat in the palms, soles, and chest (five-center heat), chronic dry mouth and throat, toothache from deficiency fire. In children: failure to thrive, delayed fontanelle closure.
mnemonic: LIU WEI DI HUANG: 'THREE tonifiers + THREE drainers in a 2:1 ratio.' Shu Di, Shan Zhu Yu, Shan Yao NOURISH; Ze Xie, Mu Dan Pi, Fu Ling DRAIN. Tonify without stagnating — the GENIUS of this formula.
board_pearl: Liu Wei Di Huang Wan is THE foundational Kidney Yin formula with the elegant 3-tonify/3-drain structure. The 8:4:4 (or 2:1:1) dosage ratio is classic. It is the base for many derivative formulas (Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan, Qi Ju Di Huang Wan, etc.).
formula_math: Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Zhi Mu + Huang Bai = Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan; Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Gou Qi Zi + Ju Hua = Qi Ju Di Huang Wan; Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Mai Men Dong + Wu Wei Zi = Mai Wei Di Huang Wan; Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Fu Zi + Rou Gui = Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Mu and Huang Bai
key_modifications.for: Yin deficiency with stronger fire (Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan)

key_modifications.modification: Add Gou Qi Zi and Ju Hua
key_modifications.for: Liver/Kidney Yin deficiency with eye problems (Qi Ju Di Huang Wan)

key_modifications.modification: Add Mai Men Dong and Wu Wei Zi
key_modifications.for: Lung and Kidney Yin deficiency with cough (Mai Wei Di Huang Wan)

key_modifications.modification: Add Fu Zi and Rou Gui
key_modifications.for: Kidney Yang deficiency (Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan)

clinical_pearls: Originally created by Qian Yi for pediatric use (five delays, five weaknesses) — later became the premier adult Kidney Yin formula.
The 3-tonify/3-drain structure prevents the cloying tonics from creating stagnation or dampness.
Ze Xie drains Kidney turbidity, Mu Dan Pi clears Liver fire, Fu Ling leaches Spleen dampness — each drainer pairs with a tonifier.
Distinguished from Zuo Gui Wan: Liu Wei uses the tonify-and-drain method; Zuo Gui uses pure tonification without draining.
contraindications: Spleen deficiency with loose stools — the rich Yin tonics can worsen dampness

indications: True Yin (Kidney Yin and essence) deficiency with dizziness, tinnitus, soreness and weakness of the lower back and knees, spontaneous and nocturnal emissions, spontaneous sweating and night sweats, dry mouth and throat, thirst, wasting and thirsting disorder (Xiao Ke), premature aging.
tongue: Red tongue with little or no coating, dry
mnemonic: ZUO GUI WAN: 'Restore the LEFT (Yin) Kidney.' Liu Wei Di Huang minus the 3 drainers, plus ESSENSE-fillers: deer antler jelly, tortoise shell jelly, Gou Qi Zi, Tu Si Zi, Niu Xi. PURE tonification, no drainage — for true Yin/essence depletion.
board_pearl: Zuo Gui Wan is PURE Yin and essence tonification without any draining herbs — it removes Liu Wei's 3 drainers (Ze Xie, Mu Dan Pi, Fu Ling) and adds essence-filling animal products and seeds. 'Left' = Yin = Kidney Water.
formula_math: Liu Wei Di Huang Wan - Ze Xie - Mu Dan Pi - Fu Ling + Gou Qi Zi + Lu Jiao Jiao + Gui Ban Jiao + Chuan Niu Xi + Tu Si Zi = Zuo Gui Wan

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Mu and Huang Bai
key_modifications.for: Yin deficiency with more prominent fire

key_modifications.modification: Add Bie Jia
key_modifications.for: Steaming bone disorder with severe night sweats

clinical_pearls: Zhang Jing-Yue created Zuo Gui and You Gui as a paired set: Zuo (left) restores Yin/Water; You (right) restores Yang/Fire.
No draining herbs means this is for TRUE severe Yin/essence depletion — when drainage would further deplete the patient.
Lu Jiao Jiao and Gui Ban Jiao are animal gelatins that powerfully fill essence and marrow — 'like fills like.'
Distinguished from Liu Wei Di Huang Wan: Liu Wei is milder with its tonify-and-drain approach; Zuo Gui is aggressive pure tonification for more severe deficiency.
contraindications: Spleen deficiency with loose stools or poor digestion — the heavy, cloying tonics will overwhelm the Spleen

indications: Yin deficiency with vigorous fire flaring upward: steaming bone disorder, tidal fever, night sweats, spontaneous emissions, irritability, coughing of blood, hemoptysis, sensation of heat and pain in the knees and legs (as if weakened).
pulse: Rapid, forceful pulse at the proximal position
mnemonic: DA BU YIN WAN: 'GREAT Yin tonic — Two HEAVY Yin anchors (Shu Di + Gui Ban) slam down two FIRE fighters (Huang Bai + Zhi Mu).' When deficiency fire rages upward, anchor Yin and DRAG fire down.
board_pearl: Da Bu Yin Wan pairs two Yin-enriching heavyweights (Shu Di + Gui Ban) with two fire-draining herbs (Huang Bai + Zhi Mu) — specifically for vigorous deficiency fire with steaming bone disorder.

key_modifications.modification: Add Di Gu Pi and Yin Chai Hu
key_modifications.for: More severe tidal fever and night sweats

key_modifications.modification: Add E Jiao
key_modifications.for: Hemoptysis from Yin deficiency fire

clinical_pearls: Traditionally prepared as pills with pig spinal cord (Zhu Ji Sui) to further enrich essence and marrow.
The combination of Huang Bai + Zhi Mu is a classic pair for clearing deficiency fire — they appear together in Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan as well.
Gui Ban is heavy and descending — it anchors floating Yang and pulls fire back down to the Kidney.
Distinguished from Liu Wei Di Huang Wan: Da Bu Yin Wan actively CLEARS deficiency fire; Liu Wei primarily NOURISHES Yin with gentle drainage.

indications: Liver and Kidney Yin deficiency with Liver Qi stagnation causing hypochondriac and chest pain, epigastric and abdominal distension, acid reflux, dry mouth and throat, bitter taste in the mouth, chronic hepatitis presentation.
tongue: Red, dry tongue with little coating
pulse: Thin, frail or thin, wiry pulse
mnemonic: YI GUAN JIAN: 'One LINKING decoction connects Liver Yin nourishment to Qi flow.' Five Yin nourishers + ONE Qi mover (Chuan Lian Zi). When the Liver is too DRY to flow, moisten it and it moves itself.
board_pearl: Yi Guan Jian is THE formula for Liver Yin deficiency with Qi stagnation — the key insight is that Liver Qi stagnates BECAUSE Yin is deficient (Liver lacks moisture to flow), so nourish Yin first, move Qi gently second. Only ONE Qi-moving herb (Chuan Lian Zi) is needed.

key_modifications.modification: Remove Dang Gui, add Bai Shao
key_modifications.for: Acid reflux is the dominant symptom

key_modifications.modification: Add Yu Jin and Bie Jia
key_modifications.for: Liver enlargement or masses from chronic hepatitis

key_modifications.modification: Add Wu Mei
key_modifications.for: Severe acid reflux

clinical_pearls: The philosophy: when Liver Yin is deficient, the Liver dries out and cannot spread Qi smoothly — the root is Yin deficiency, not Qi stagnation.
Chuan Lian Zi is cold and bitter — it moves Liver Qi while clearing heat, perfectly suited for Yin deficiency Liver stagnation.
Distinguished from Xiao Yao San: Yi Guan Jian is for Yin deficiency causing stagnation (nourish Yin approach); Xiao Yao San is for Qi stagnation with Spleen deficiency (move Qi approach).
Modern use in chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, and gastric/duodenal ulcers with Yin deficiency.
contraindications: Liver Qi stagnation from excess — use Chai Hu Shu Gan San or Xiao Yao San instead

actions: Enriches and tonifies the Liver and Kidney Yin
indications: Liver and Kidney Yin deficiency with dizziness, blurred vision, dry eyes, tinnitus, premature graying of hair, soreness and weakness of the lower back and knees, spontaneous and nocturnal emissions, menstrual irregularity or uterine bleeding from Yin deficiency.
mnemonic: ER ZHI WAN: 'TWO ULTIMATE herbs harvested at the Summer and Winter SOLSTICES' — Nu Zhen Zi (winter solstice) and Han Lian Cao (summer solstice). Two herbs, two seasons, one Yin-nourishing pill. Simple but gentle.
board_pearl: Er Zhi Wan has only TWO herbs that gently nourish Liver and Kidney Yin without cloying — safe for patients with Spleen deficiency who cannot tolerate heavy Yin tonics like Shu Di Huang.

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Liu Wei Di Huang Wan
key_modifications.for: More severe Kidney Yin deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Sang Shen Zi
key_modifications.for: Premature graying and hair loss

key_modifications.modification: Add Xian He Cao
key_modifications.for: Uterine bleeding

clinical_pearls: Er Zhi (Two Ultimates/Solstices): Nu Zhen Zi is traditionally harvested at winter solstice (peak Yin); Han Lian Cao at summer solstice (peak Yang containing Yin).
The gentlest of all Yin tonifying formulas — no cloying, greasy herbs, making it suitable for patients with weak digestion.
Often used as an adjunct formula combined with other prescriptions rather than as a standalone.
Nu Zhen Zi nourishes Liver and Kidney Yin while Han Lian Cao also cools Blood and stops bleeding.
contraindications: Spleen Yang deficiency with diarrhea from cold

english_name: Kidney Qi Pill from the Golden Cabinet
indications: Kidney Yang deficiency with soreness and weakness of the lower back, cold lower half of the body, tension in the lower abdomen, difficulty with urination or excessive urination (especially nocturia), edema of the lower extremities, diminished libido, impotence. Also for Xiao Ke (wasting and thirsting) with copious pale urination, and chronic cough or wheezing from Kidney not grasping Qi.
tongue: Pale, swollen tongue with thin white moist coating, possible teeth marks
pulse: Deep, thin, weak pulse, especially at the proximal (chi) position
mnemonic: JIN GUI SHEN QI WAN: 'Liu Wei Di Huang + a TINY spark of Yang (Fu Zi + Gui Zhi).' Just a SMALL flame of Yang (3g each!) ignites the sea of Yin — 'a little fire to warm a lot of water.'
board_pearl: Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan = Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Fu Zi + Gui Zhi in SMALL doses (3g each). The original uses Gui Zhi (not Rou Gui). The genius: a small amount of Yang fire within abundant Yin nourishment reflects 'seek Yang within Yin.'
formula_math: Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Fu Zi + Gui Zhi = Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan; Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan + Niu Xi + Che Qian Zi = Ji Sheng Shen Qi Wan (for edema)

key_modifications.modification: Add Niu Xi and Che Qian Zi
key_modifications.for: Edema and difficulty urinating (Ji Sheng Shen Qi Wan)

key_modifications.modification: Replace Gui Zhi with Rou Gui
key_modifications.for: Modern version with stronger Kidney-warming action

key_modifications.modification: Increase Fu Zi dose
key_modifications.for: More severe Yang deficiency with cold

clinical_pearls: The original Zhang Zhong-Jing formula uses Gui Zhi, not Rou Gui — many modern commercial versions substitute Rou Gui, which is a stronger Yang warmer.
Fu Zi and Gui Zhi are in SMALL doses (3g) relative to the Yin herbs — this embodies 'seeking Yang within Yin,' using a small flame to generate warmth within a foundation of Yin.
Distinguished from You Gui Wan: Jin Gui uses the tonify-and-drain method with small Yang additions; You Gui is pure aggressive Yang tonification.
Urinary symptoms are a key indication: either excessive urination (Yang not controlling water) or difficult urination (Yang not transforming water).
contraindications: Yin deficiency with heat signs — the Yang herbs will worsen fire
Dry mouth with true thirst for cold drinks

indications: Kidney Yang deficiency and waning of the Gate of Vitality (Ming Men) fire with exhaustion, aversion to cold, cold extremities and cold lower back, impotence, spermatorrhea, infertility (cold womb), loose stools or dawn diarrhea (wu geng xie), edema, profuse clear urination, soreness and weakness of lower back and knees.
tongue: Pale, swollen tongue with white coating
mnemonic: YOU GUI WAN: 'Restore the RIGHT (Yang) Kidney.' PURE Yang tonification — NO drainers. Liu Wei base minus the 3 drainers, plus Lu Jiao Jiao, Du Zhong, Tu Si Zi, Gou Qi Zi, Dang Gui, Rou Gui, Fu Zi. When the Ming Men fire is nearly OUT, go all-in on warming.
board_pearl: You Gui Wan is the Yang counterpart to Zuo Gui Wan — PURE tonification of Kidney Yang and essence with NO draining herbs. 'Right' = Yang = Kidney Fire/Ming Men. Fu Zi + Rou Gui doses are larger than Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan, reflecting more aggressive Yang warming.
formula_math: Liu Wei Di Huang Wan - Ze Xie - Mu Dan Pi - Fu Ling + Gou Qi Zi + Du Zhong + Tu Si Zi + Lu Jiao Jiao + Dang Gui + Rou Gui + Fu Zi = You Gui Wan

key_modifications.modification: Add Bu Gu Zhi and Yi Zhi Ren
key_modifications.for: Dawn diarrhea (Si Shen Wan herbs)

key_modifications.modification: Add Rou Cong Rong
key_modifications.for: Impotence and infertility

clinical_pearls: Zhang Jing-Yue's paired set: Zuo Gui Wan (Left/Yin) and You Gui Wan (Right/Yang) — Left=Water=Yin, Right=Fire=Yang.
No draining herbs — this is PURE tonification for severe Yang depletion when drainage would further weaken the patient.
Lu Jiao Jiao (deer antler gelatin) fills essence and strongly warms Yang — 'like fills like' with animal products.
Distinguished from Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan: Jin Gui uses the tonify-Yin-to-support-Yang method with small Yang doses; You Gui is aggressive pure Yang tonification for severe deficiency.
contraindications: Caution in pregnancy due to Fu Zi and Rou Gui

indications: Kidney Yang deficiency with essence insecurity causing frequent, cloudy urination, urinary incontinence, spermatorrhea, premature ejaculation, soreness and weakness of the lower back, dizziness and tinnitus, waning of libido, cold limbs.
mnemonic: TU SI ZI WAN: 'Cuscuta is a PARASITIC vine that CLINGS and holds on' — like this formula clings to and secures leaking Kidney essence. Tu Si Zi + Wu Wei Zi + Sang Piao Xiao LOCK the gate against spermatorrhea and urinary incontinence.
board_pearl: Tu Si Zi Wan warms Kidney Yang AND secures essence — combining warming herbs (Fu Zi, Rou Gui, Lu Rong) with astringent/securing herbs (Tu Si Zi, Wu Wei Zi, Sang Piao Xiao) for Yang deficiency with essence leakage.

clinical_pearls: Tu Si Zi is the chief because it simultaneously tonifies Kidney Yang, nourishes Yin, and secures essence — a balanced, non-harsh Yang tonic.
The formula balances warming (Fu Zi, Rou Gui, Lu Rong) with Yin nourishment (Shu Di, Shi Hu, Shan Yao) and astringent securing (Wu Wei Zi, Sang Piao Xiao).
Shi Hu (dendrobium) nourishes Kidney Yin — preventing the warming herbs from drying out fluids.
Distinguished from You Gui Wan: Tu Si Zi Wan emphasizes SECURING essence (astringent herbs); You Gui Wan emphasizes WARMING Yang (aggressive Yang tonification).
contraindications: Urinary tract infections with heat signs

actions: Resolves all six types of stagnation
indications: Qi, blood, phlegm, fire, dampness, and food stagnation manifesting as a stifling sensation in the chest and abdomen, fixed pain in the hypochondria, belching, vomiting, acid regurgitation, and indigestion.
mnemonic: ESCAPE from all SIX restraints: Cang Zhu ATTACKs dampness, Chuan Xiong MOVES blood, Xiang Fu FREES qi, Zhi Zi CLEARS fire, Shen Qu DIGESTS food — five herbs for six stagnations.
board_pearl: Yue Ju Wan is the only formula that treats all six types of stagnation (qi, blood, phlegm, fire, dampness, food) with equal-dose herbs, and Xiang Fu is the key herb addressing qi constraint which is considered the root of all six.
formula_math: Each herb targets one stagnation: Xiang Fu = qi, Chuan Xiong = blood, Cang Zhu = dampness, Zhi Zi = fire, Shen Qu = food; phlegm is resolved indirectly by treating the other five.

clinical_pearls: All five herbs are used in equal dosage — reflecting the idea that no single stagnation predominates in the base formula.
Xiang Fu is considered the 'commander' because qi stagnation is the root cause generating the other five.
This formula is a conceptual framework: in practice, the herb addressing the predominant stagnation is increased.
contraindications: Qi and blood deficiency without stagnation

indications: Plum-pit qi (mei he qi): a sensation of something stuck in the throat that can neither be swallowed nor ejected, a stifling sensation in the chest and hypochondria, coughing with copious sputum.
mnemonic: The PLUM PIT got stuck: BAN XIA and HOU PO dissolve the pit, FU LING drains the juice, SHENG JIANG warms the fruit, and ZI SU YE peels the skin away.
board_pearl: Ban Xia Hou Po Tang is THE formula for plum-pit qi (mei he qi), a globus sensation in the throat caused by phlegm and qi stagnation — classically described in the Jin Gui Yao Lue for women.
formula_math: Ban Xia Hou Po Tang = Er Chen Tang framework (Ban Xia + Fu Ling) minus Chen Pi, Zhi Gan Cao + Hou Po, Sheng Jiang, Zi Su Ye for stronger qi-moving and phlegm-resolving action in the throat.

clinical_pearls: This is the principal formula for globus hystericus in modern clinical practice.
Sheng Jiang is used in a large dose (15g) to enhance the phlegm-resolving effect of Ban Xia.
From the Jin Gui Yao Lue — originally described for women but used for all genders clinically.
Zi Su Ye is aromatic and moves qi in the exterior and chest, helping open the throat.
contraindications: True physical obstruction in the throat (must rule out organic pathology)

actions: Unblocks yang qi in the chest
Transforms turbid yin and phlegm accumulation
indications: Chest bi (xiong bi) with severe qi stagnation and phlegm obstruction: chest pain radiating to the back, fullness and distension in the chest, wheezing, difficult breathing, shortness of breath.
mnemonic: The CHEST is LOCKED: ZHI SHI breaks the lock, XIE BAI opens the CHEST yang, GUI ZHI warms the pathways, HOU PO expands the space, GUA LOU clears the phlegm clogging it.
board_pearl: Zhi Shi Xie Bai Gui Zhi Tang treats the most severe form of chest bi (xiong bi) — use it when Gua Lou Xie Bai Ban Xia Tang is not strong enough, as it adds Zhi Shi and Hou Po for stronger qi-breaking action.
formula_math: Gua Lou Xie Bai Ban Xia Tang (Gua Lou + Xie Bai + Ban Xia) − Ban Xia + Zhi Shi + Hou Po + Gui Zhi = stronger qi-moving, chest-opening formula for more severe chest bi.

Severity progression: Gua Lou Xie Bai Bai Jiu Tang (mild) → Gua Lou Xie Bai Ban Xia Tang (moderate) → Zhi Shi Xie Bai Gui Zhi Tang (severe).
Modern use includes coronary artery disease and angina pectoris with phlegm-turbidity obstruction.
Gui Zhi is used in a small dose (3g) — just enough to warm yang without being overly dispersing.
contraindications: Chest pain due to yin deficiency with internal heat
Pregnancy (Zhi Shi strongly moves qi downward)

indications: Shan qi (hernia disorder) due to cold stagnation in the Liver channel: lower abdominal pain radiating to the testicles, or bulging disorder of the lower abdomen. Pain is aggravated by cold and alleviated by warmth.
mnemonic: TIAN TAI delivers WU YAO to the TOP of hernia pain: MU XIANG and XIAO HUI XIANG warm the cold channel, QING PI squeezes the Liver qi, GAO LIANG JIANG fires the furnace, BING LANG drives it down, CHUAN LIAN ZI (fried with BA DOU then tossed) guides to the Liver.
board_pearl: Tian Tai Wu Yao San is the primary formula for cold-type shan qi (hernia) with pain radiating to the testicles — remember Ba Dou is used only to process Chuan Lian Zi and is then DISCARDED.

clinical_pearls: Ba Dou is the hottest herb in the materia medica — it is stir-fried with Chuan Lian Zi to make the cold Chuan Lian Zi warm, then discarded.
Chuan Lian Zi is normally bitter and cold, but after processing with Ba Dou it conducts the warm herbs to the Liver channel without introducing cold.
Modern applications include inguinal hernia, testicular pain, and epididymitis.
Originally ground into powder and taken in small doses with wine.

indications: Liver and Kidney deficiency cold with qi stagnation: hernia pain, lower abdominal pain, testicular pain and swelling that is aggravated by cold. A sensation of cold in the lower abdomen.
mnemonic: WARM the LIVER like a cozy CABIN: DANG GUI and GOU QI stock the pantry (blood/yin), ROU GUI and XIAO HUI XIANG light the fire, WU YAO fans the flames (moves qi), CHEN XIANG sinks the warmth down, FU LING keeps it dry, SHENG JIANG sparks it.
board_pearl: Nuan Gan Jian treats hernia from Liver/Kidney DEFICIENCY cold (tonifies + warms), whereas Tian Tai Wu Yao San treats hernia from EXCESS cold stagnation (moves qi + scatters cold) — this is the key differential on boards.
formula_math: Nuan Gan Jian = Tian Tai Wu Yao San concept + blood/yin tonification (Dang Gui, Gou Qi Zi) — for deficiency cold hernia vs. excess cold hernia.

clinical_pearls: Key distinction from Tian Tai Wu Yao San: Nuan Gan Jian includes blood and yin tonics (Dang Gui, Gou Qi Zi), indicating underlying deficiency.
Chen Xiang directs qi downward — it sinks the warming action to the lower jiao.
From Jing Yue Quan Shu (Zhang Jie-Bin) — reflects his emphasis on warming and tonifying.
Gou Qi Zi is unusual in a warming formula — it prevents the warm herbs from injuring Liver yin.
contraindications: Liver fire or Liver yang rising

indications: Hiccup or retching due to Stomach deficiency with heat: hiccup, dry retching, nausea after a prolonged illness or febrile disease. The patient is weak and has residual heat.
tongue: Tender, red tongue with little coating
mnemonic: After being sick, the STOMACH HICCUPs with residual heat: JU PI and ZHU RU are the hiccup-stopping DUO — JU PI descends qi, ZHU RU clears heat. REN SHEN, GAN CAO, DA ZAO rebuild the weak Stomach, SHENG JIANG stops vomiting.
board_pearl: Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang treats hiccup from Stomach DEFICIENCY with HEAT (post-illness), while Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang treats hiccup from Stomach DEFICIENCY with COLD — heat vs. cold is the board distinction.
formula_math: Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang (deficiency + heat hiccup) vs. Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang (deficiency + cold hiccup) — a classic paired comparison.

clinical_pearls: Classic pairing: Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang (hot hiccup) vs. Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang (cold hiccup).
Zhu Ru is the key differentiator — it clears Stomach heat without being too cold, making it safe for deficiency.
Commonly used after chemotherapy or post-surgical nausea with underlying deficiency.
The formula is from the Jin Gui Yao Lue, highlighting its classical pedigree.
contraindications: Hiccup from excess cold in the Stomach
Hiccup from Liver qi invading the Stomach (excess pattern)

indications: Hiccup due to Stomach deficiency cold: persistent hiccup with a low, weak sound, pale complexion, cold sensation in the epigastrium, reduced appetite, lack of thirst.
mnemonic: COLD hiccups? DING XIANG (clove) and SHI DI (persimmon cap) NAIL the hiccup shut — clove WARMS the cold Stomach, persimmon CAPS the rebellious qi. REN SHEN and SHENG JIANG support from below.
board_pearl: Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang is for COLD-type deficiency hiccup (pale tongue, slow pulse), while Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang is for HEAT-type deficiency hiccup (red tongue, rapid pulse).
formula_math: Ding Xiang Shi Di Tang (deficiency + cold hiccup) mirrors Ju Pi Zhu Ru Tang (deficiency + heat hiccup) — both tonify Stomach qi but warm vs. clear.

clinical_pearls: Only four herbs — one of the simplest formulas to remember.
Ding Xiang (clove) is one of the strongest herbs for warming the Stomach and stopping hiccup.
Shi Di (persimmon calyx) specifically descends Stomach qi — it is the go-to herb for hiccup regardless of pattern.
Clinically used for intractable hiccup in terminal or chronic illness with cold signs.
contraindications: Hiccup from Stomach heat or Stomach fire
Hiccup from Liver qi stagnation (excess type)

english_name: Perilla Fruit Decoction for Directing Qi Downward
indications: Excess above with deficiency below: wheezing and coughing with copious watery sputum, chest fullness, dyspnea worse on exhalation, low back pain, weakness and cold in the lower extremities. Upper excess (phlegm) with lower deficiency (Kidney yang).
pulse: Wiry, tight on the cun position
mnemonic: SU ZI sends qi DOWN from congested lungs to weak kidneys: BAN XIA dissolves phlegm, QIAN HU and HOU PO open the chest, ROU GUI warms the Kidney root below, DANG GUI nourishes blood to prevent drying. Top-heavy asthma grounded at the root.
board_pearl: Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang is for asthma/wheezing with EXCESS phlegm ABOVE and Kidney yang DEFICIENCY BELOW — the concept of 'upper excess, lower deficiency' is the board buzzword.
formula_math: Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang addresses upper excess + lower deficiency; compare with Ding Chuan Tang which addresses wind-cold constraining Lung with internal phlegm-heat (no Kidney deficiency component).
