clinical_pearls: Dang Gui is included to nourish blood and prevent the drying effects of the qi-moving and phlegm-resolving herbs — a unique feature.
Rou Gui warms Kidney yang to help the Kidneys grasp qi — addressing the root of 'lower deficiency.'
The 'upper excess, lower deficiency' concept is fundamental: phlegm fills the Lungs (excess above) while Kidneys fail to grasp qi (deficiency below).
Commonly modified for COPD in elderly patients with chronic phlegm and weak Kidneys.
contraindications: Lung heat with yellow, thick sputum
Yin deficiency cough with scanty sputum
Wheezing from Liver fire invading the Lungs

indications: Stomach deficiency with phlegm obstruction and qi rebellion: epigastric focal distension and hardness, frequent belching, retching or vomiting, hiccup. Often seen after a febrile illness when the Stomach is weakened.
mnemonic: The STOMACH REBELs upward: XUAN FU HUA (the flower) floats phlegm away, DAI ZHE SHI (the stone) SINKS rebellious qi like an anchor. REN SHEN and DA ZAO rebuild the Stomach. BAN XIA and SHENG JIANG stop the retching.
board_pearl: Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang treats epigastric hardness with belching from Stomach deficiency and phlegm — Xuan Fu Hua resolves phlegm while Dai Zhe Shi (heavy mineral) anchors rebellious Stomach qi downward.
formula_math: Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang builds on the framework of Xiao Ban Xia Tang (Ban Xia + Sheng Jiang for nausea) + Ren Shen for deficiency + Xuan Fu Hua/Dai Zhe Shi for directing qi downward and resolving phlegm.

clinical_pearls: From the Shang Han Lun — used after improper purging damages the Stomach qi.
Xuan Fu Hua must be wrapped in cloth (bao jian) when decocting to prevent its fine hairs from irritating the throat.
Dai Zhe Shi is a mineral (iron oxide) — its heaviness is what anchors rebellious qi. It must be pre-decocted.
The flower-and-stone pairing is elegant: the light flower (Xuan Fu Hua) resolves phlegm while the heavy stone (Dai Zhe Shi) descends qi.
contraindications: Excess patterns without underlying Stomach deficiency
Pregnancy (Dai Zhe Shi is heavy and descending)

english_name: Peach Pit Decoction to Order the Qi
indications: Blood stasis in the lower jiao with heat: lower abdominal fullness and pain that is worse with pressure, night fevers, irritability or even manic behavior. Dysmenorrhea or amenorrhea with palpable masses. Acute lower abdominal pain with blood stasis.
tongue: Dark red or purple, may have stasis spots
mnemonic: PEACH pit PURGES blood stasis with heat: TAO REN breaks the stasis, DA HUANG and MANG XIAO purge the heat downward (like Cheng Qi), GUI ZHI moves blood through the vessels, GAN CAO moderates.
board_pearl: Tao He Cheng Qi Tang = blood stasis + heat in the lower jiao purged via the bowels — it combines blood-breaking (Tao Ren) with the purgative power of Cheng Qi Tang (Da Huang + Mang Xiao), and the night fever/manic behavior are classic Shang Han Lun buzzwords.
formula_math: Tao He Cheng Qi Tang = Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang (Da Huang + Mang Xiao + Gan Cao) + Tao Ren + Gui Zhi. It adds blood-moving to the purgative base.

clinical_pearls: From the Shang Han Lun — originally for the 'heat binding with blood in the lower jiao' (re jie xia jiao xue) syndrome.
Night fevers that come and go are a hallmark indication — the blood stasis traps heat which manifests at night when yin predominates.
Gui Zhi here is not for releasing exterior — it warms and moves blood through the vessels to enhance Tao Ren's stasis-breaking action.
This is a milder blood stasis formula compared to Di Dang Tang, which uses Shui Zhi (leech) and Meng Chong for severe blood stasis.
contraindications: Qi and blood deficiency without excess

english_name: Drive Out Stasis in the Mansion of Blood Decoction
actions: Spreads the qi in the chest
indications: Blood stasis in the chest (xue fu): chest pain, headache, chronic stubborn hiccup, choking sensation when drinking, palpitations, insomnia, irritability, extreme mood swings, evening tidal fever, dark or purplish lips.
tongue: Dark red, may have petechiae or purple spots
mnemonic: BLOOD MANSION (xue fu = chest) needs EVICTION: Tao-Hong Si Wu Tang (Tao Ren, Hong Hua, Dang Gui, Sheng Di, Chuan Xiong, Chi Shao) EVICTS blood stasis, Si Ni San ingredients (Chai Hu, Zhi Ke) MOVE the qi, NIU XI directs downward, JIE GENG opens upward — creating a PUMP that clears the chest.
board_pearl: Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang is the #1 formula for blood stasis in the CHEST — it combines Tao Hong Si Wu Tang (blood stasis) with Si Ni San's qi-moving pair (Chai Hu + Zhi Ke) plus Jie Geng (opens upward) and Niu Xi (directs downward).
formula_math: Xue Fu Zhu Yu Tang = Tao Hong Si Wu Tang (Si Wu Tang + Tao Ren + Hong Hua) + Chai Hu + Zhi Ke (from Si Ni San) + Jie Geng + Niu Xi + Gan Cao.

clinical_pearls: Wang Qing-Ren's masterpiece from Yi Lin Gai Cuo — he created four Zhu Yu Tang formulas for blood stasis in different body regions.
The Jie Geng + Niu Xi pairing creates a 'pump' — Jie Geng lifts to the chest, Niu Xi descends, together circulating blood throughout.
The four Zhu Yu Tang formulas: Xue Fu (chest), Ge Xia (below diaphragm), Shao Fu (lower abdomen), Shen Tong (whole body/channels).
Widely used in modern practice for coronary artery disease, post-concussion syndrome, chronic headache, and depression with blood stasis.

english_name: Tonify the Yang to Restore Five-Tenths Decoction
indications: Sequelae of wind-stroke (zhong feng) due to qi deficiency with blood stasis in the channels: hemiplegia, deviation of the mouth and eyes, slurred speech, drooling, urinary frequency or incontinence, lower limb atrophy.
tongue: Dark, pale tongue with white coating
mnemonic: After a STROKE, only FIVE-TENTHS of the body works: a MASSIVE dose of HUANG QI (120g!) powers the qi to push blood through blocked channels. The blood movers (Dang Gui Wei, Chi Shao, Chuan Xiong, Tao Ren, Hong Hua) are in TINY doses — the qi does the heavy lifting. DI LONG (earthworm) opens the channels.
board_pearl: Bu Yang Huan Wu Tang uses an enormous dose of Huang Qi (120g) as the Jun herb for post-stroke hemiplegia — the massive qi tonification drives small amounts of blood-moving herbs through obstructed channels; the 10:1 ratio of Huang Qi to blood movers is the key concept.
formula_math: Bu Yang Huan Wu Tang = massive Huang Qi + small-dose Tao Hong Si Wu Tang (minus Sheng Di) + Di Long. The extreme dose ratio reflects qi deficiency as the root and blood stasis as the branch.

clinical_pearls: The 120g dose of Huang Qi is not a typo — it is Wang Qing-Ren's original dose, though modern practitioners often start at 30-60g and gradually increase.
Dang Gui Wei (tail) is specified because the tail of Dang Gui is the strongest blood-invigorating part.
Di Long (earthworm) unblocks channels and collaterals — it is a key herb for post-stroke channel obstruction.
The name means 'restore five-tenths' because each side of the body represents five-tenths — the formula restores the paralyzed half.
Used for ischemic stroke recovery, not during acute hemorrhagic stroke.
contraindications: Excess-type wind-stroke with Liver yang rising
Patients with uncontrolled hypertension (large-dose Huang Qi may raise blood pressure)

english_name: Drive Out Blood Stasis in the Lower Abdomen Decoction
indications: Blood stasis in the lower abdomen with cold: lower abdominal pain with or without palpable masses, menstrual pain with dark clotted blood, irregular menstruation, infertility from cold uterus with blood stasis.
tongue: Dark purple or pale with purple spots
mnemonic: The LOWER BELLY is COLD and STUCK: warming trio (Xiao Hui Xiang, Gan Jiang, Rou Gui) THAWS the cold, pain-stopping duo (Yan Hu Suo, Mo Yao) BREAKS the cramps, blood movers (Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Chi Shao) FREE the flow, Shi Xiao San pair (Pu Huang, Wu Ling Zhi) ZAPS the stasis.
board_pearl: Shao Fu Zhu Yu Tang targets blood stasis with COLD in the LOWER ABDOMEN — it is the only Zhu Yu Tang that includes warming herbs (Gan Jiang, Rou Gui, Xiao Hui Xiang), distinguishing it from the other three which address stasis without cold.
formula_math: Shao Fu Zhu Yu Tang = blood-moving core (Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Chi Shao) + Shi Xiao San (Pu Huang + Wu Ling Zhi) + warming herbs (Xiao Hui Xiang, Gan Jiang, Rou Gui) + pain relief (Yan Hu Suo, Mo Yao). It is the 'cold' version among Wang Qing-Ren's four Zhu Yu formulas.

clinical_pearls: Wang Qing-Ren's four Zhu Yu formulas by location: Xue Fu (chest), Ge Xia (below diaphragm/hypochondria), Shao Fu (lower abdomen), Shen Tong (whole body channels).
Contains Shi Xiao San (Pu Huang + Wu Ling Zhi) embedded within it — the famous stasis-breaking and pain-stopping pair.
Modern applications include endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and uterine fibroids with cold signs.
The warming herbs distinguish this from Ge Xia Zhu Yu Tang, which is for blood stasis with qi stagnation and mild heat below the diaphragm.

indications: Blood stasis and qi stagnation in the Heart and Stomach: epigastric and abdominal pain with a stabbing quality, a sensation of fullness and distension in the chest and epigastrium.
mnemonic: Only THREE herbs for HEART and STOMACH stasis pain: DAN SHEN does the work of Si Wu Tang alone (invigorates blood), TAN XIANG (sandalwood) opens the chest qi, SHA REN awakens the Stomach qi. Simple and elegant.
board_pearl: Dan Shen Yin is a three-herb formula for blood stasis and qi stagnation causing Heart/Stomach pain — Dan Shen is said to function like the entire Si Wu Tang by itself.
formula_math: Dan Shen Yin is one of the simplest blood-invigorating formulas. Often combined with Shi Xiao San for more severe stasis pain.

key_modifications.modification: Add Pu Huang, Wu Ling Zhi (Shi Xiao San)

clinical_pearls: There is a famous saying: 'One herb, Dan Shen, functions as Si Wu Tang' (yi wei Dan Shen gong tong Si Wu).
Only three herbs — extremely easy to remember and frequently tested.
Tan Xiang (sandalwood) moves qi in the chest, making it specific for Heart/chest pain.
Sha Ren aromatically transforms dampness and moves qi in the Stomach — it targets the Stomach component.
Widely used as an add-on for coronary artery disease and gastric pain with blood stasis.

indications: Blood stasis and masses in the lower abdomen: palpable but not hard immovable masses (zheng jia) in the lower abdomen, fixed abdominal pain, uterine bleeding with dark purple blood with clots, dysmenorrhea. Originally for blood stasis during pregnancy with restless fetus and vaginal bleeding.
tongue: Dark purple or with purple spots
mnemonic: Five-herb PILL for pelvic MASSES: GUI ZHI warms and moves, FU LING drains dampness from the mass, MU DAN PI and CHI SHAO cool and break stasis, TAO REN crushes the clots. Originally a PREGNANCY formula — gently breaks stasis to CALM a restless fetus.
board_pearl: Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan is the go-to formula for uterine fibroids (zheng jia) with blood stasis — and uniquely, it can be used during PREGNANCY when blood stasis causes restless fetus with abdominal pain and bleeding.
formula_math: Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan = Gui Zhi (warm, move) + Fu Ling (drain) + blood stasis trio (Mu Dan Pi, Chi Shao, Tao Ren). Compare with Tao He Cheng Qi Tang which purges stasis with heat via the bowels.

clinical_pearls: From the Jin Gui Yao Lue — originally for pregnant women with blood stasis causing restless fetus with bleeding.
Despite being a blood stasis formula, it is one of the few considered safe in pregnancy (in appropriate cases) because it is mild and in pill form.
Made as pills (wan) rather than decoction to ensure gradual, gentle action — important for pregnancy safety.
Modern applications: uterine fibroids, ovarian cysts, endometriosis, and chronic pelvic inflammatory disease.
All five herbs are used in equal dosage in the original formulation.
contraindications: Pregnancy without blood stasis signs (do not use preventively)
Severe blood or qi deficiency without stasis

indications: Blood stasis causing pain anywhere in the body: sharp, stabbing pain in the chest, epigastrium, or lower abdomen, irregular menstruation with dark clotted blood, postpartum abdominal pain with lochia retention, or sudden onset of abdominal pain.
tongue: Purple or dark, may have stasis spots
mnemonic: Only TWO herbs make you SUDDENLY SMILE (the pain vanishes!): WU LING ZHI (flying squirrel feces) and PU HUANG (cattail pollen) — the ultimate stasis-busting POWER COUPLE. Feces and pollen: an unlikely pair that works magic on blood stasis pain.
board_pearl: Shi Xiao San is a two-herb formula (Wu Ling Zhi + Pu Huang) for acute blood stasis pain — 'Sudden Smile' because the pain relief is rapid; it is frequently embedded within larger formulas like Shao Fu Zhu Yu Tang.
formula_math: Shi Xiao San (Wu Ling Zhi + Pu Huang) is a building block embedded in Shao Fu Zhu Yu Tang and often added to other formulas for acute blood stasis pain.

clinical_pearls: The name 'Sudden Smile' (Shi Xiao) refers to the rapid pain relief — the patient goes from grimacing to smiling.
Wu Ling Zhi is flying squirrel feces — it strongly breaks blood stasis.
Pu Huang raw invigorates blood; Pu Huang charred (Pu Huang Tan) stops bleeding — choosing the preparation changes the formula's emphasis.
Traditionally taken as a powder with vinegar (cu) to enhance the blood-moving effect.
Wu Ling Zhi and Ren Shen are classically considered incompatible (xiang wei) — avoid combining them.
contraindications: Do not combine with Ren Shen (classical incompatibility with Wu Ling Zhi)

indications: Chong and Ren deficiency cold with blood stasis and blood deficiency: irregular menstruation (early, late, or continuous), prolonged menstrual bleeding, bleeding between periods, infertility, cold lower abdomen, low-grade fever at dusk with warm palms, dry lips and mouth.
tongue: Pale, purple, or dark red with thin coating
mnemonic: WARM the MENSES with a 12-herb orchestra: Wu Zhu Yu and Gui Zhi WARM the cold uterus, Si Wu Tang base (Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Bai Shao) + E Jiao NOURISH blood, Mu Dan Pi clears deficiency heat without being too cold, Mai Men Dong moistens the dryness, Ren Shen boosts qi, Ban Xia + Sheng Jiang harmonize the Stomach. Warm + nourish + clear = complex gynecology.
board_pearl: Wen Jing Tang addresses the complex pattern of Chong/Ren deficiency COLD with BLOOD STASIS and concurrent DEFICIENCY HEAT (warm palms, dry lips) — the simultaneous cold and heat signs are the board buzzwords.
formula_math: Wen Jing Tang = warming pair (Wu Zhu Yu + Gui Zhi) + modified Si Wu Tang (Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Bai Shao, minus Sheng Di + E Jiao) + Mu Dan Pi + Mai Men Dong (for deficiency heat) + Ren Shen + Ban Xia + Sheng Jiang + Gan Cao.

clinical_pearls: From the Jin Gui Yao Lue — one of the most complex gynecological formulas.
The simultaneous presence of cold (cold lower abdomen) and heat (warm palms, dry lips) is the hallmark — the cold is in the uterus while the heat is deficiency heat from damaged yin.
Mu Dan Pi and Mai Men Dong address the deficiency heat component without introducing true cold that would worsen the uterine cold.
Ban Xia is unusual in a gynecological formula — it is included to direct rebellious qi downward and harmonize the Stomach.
E Jiao nourishes blood and stops bleeding — it is dissolved separately and added to the strained decoction.
12 herbs — the largest composition among the blood-regulating formulas listed here.

indications: Postpartum blood stasis with cold: postpartum retention of lochia, cold and pain in the lower abdomen, dark lochia with clots. The formula both expels old blood (hua) and generates new blood (sheng).
mnemonic: After birth, GENERATE new blood and TRANSFORM the old: DANG GUI (massive 24g dose!) generates new blood, CHUAN XIONG moves blood in the vessels, TAO REN breaks the retained lochia clots, PAO JIANG (blast-fried ginger) warms the cold womb, GAN CAO harmonizes. Five herbs: out with the old, in with the new.
board_pearl: Sheng Hua Tang is THE postpartum formula — 'sheng' (generate new blood) and 'hua' (transform old stasis) captures its dual action; the large dose of Dang Gui (24g) simultaneously nourishes and invigorates blood.
formula_math: Sheng Hua Tang = large-dose Dang Gui + Chuan Xiong + Tao Ren (blood stasis movers) + Pao Jiang (warming) + Gan Cao. A focused five-herb postpartum formula.

clinical_pearls: Pao Jiang (blast-fried ginger) is warmer than Gan Jiang and specifically enters the blood level to warm the uterus and stop bleeding while warming.
The name perfectly captures the formula's dual nature: generate (sheng) new blood while transforming (hua) old stasis.
Traditionally taken postpartum for the first few days, then stopped once lochia flows normally.
Dang Gui at 24g is one of the highest single-herb doses in the formulary — emphasizing the critical need to nourish blood after childbirth.
Originally from Fu Qing-Zhu's Nu Ke (Fu Qing-Zhu's Gynecology).
contraindications: Postpartum hemorrhage with profuse active bleeding (too much blood moving action)
Postpartum blood stasis with heat signs
Before delivery (may prematurely expel the fetus)

indications: Bleeding due to blood heat: hematemesis, hemoptysis, epistaxis, or any bleeding from the upper body due to blood moving recklessly from heat. Acute, sudden onset of bleeding with bright red blood.
mnemonic: TEN CHARRED herbs for HOT BLEEDING: Da Ji and Xiao Ji (big and little thistle) COOL blood, He Ye (lotus leaf) LIFTS the clear, Ce Bai Ye and Bai Mao Gen stop upper body bleeding, Qian Cao Gen invigorates to prevent stasis from charring, Zhi Zi and Da Huang DRAIN heat downward, Mu Dan Pi COOLS blood stasis, Zong Lu Pi (palm fiber) astringes. ALL CHARRED — char stops bleeding.
board_pearl: Shi Hui San charcoals ALL TEN herbs to enhance hemostatic action — it is for acute bleeding from blood heat (hematemesis, hemoptysis, epistaxis); the original instruction is to grind, mix with radish juice or lotus root juice, and take cold.

clinical_pearls: All ten herbs are charred (shao hui/tan) — charring is a traditional method to enhance hemostatic properties.
Originally taken with fresh radish juice (lai fu zhi) or lotus root juice (ou jie zhi) served cold to further cool the blood.
The inclusion of Da Huang (even charred) ensures heat is drained downward, redirecting the reckless blood.
Zong Lu Pi (trachycarpus/palm fiber) is strongly astringent and specifically stops bleeding.
This formula addresses emergent, acute bleeding — it is not for chronic, slow bleeding.
contraindications: Bleeding from deficiency cold (yang deficiency failing to hold blood)
Chronic slow bleeding from Spleen qi deficiency

indications: Intestinal wind and damp-heat causing bloody stools: bleeding from hemorrhoids or bloody stools, blood that is bright red (intestinal wind) or dark and turbid (zang du), preceding or following the stool.
mnemonic: SOPHORA FLOWER stops RECTAL BLEEDING: HUAI HUA cools the Large Intestine and stops blood, CE BAI YE cools blood and stops bleeding, JING JIE SUI (charred) dispels WIND from the intestines (intestinal wind = hemorrhoidal bleeding), ZHI KE moves qi in the intestines to reduce bearing down.
board_pearl: Huai Hua San specifically treats bleeding from the Large Intestine (hemorrhoids, bloody stools from intestinal wind or zang du) — it is the targeted rectal bleeding formula, while Shi Hui San is for generalized upper body blood-heat bleeding.
formula_math: Huai Hua San is a focused four-herb intestinal bleeding formula. Compare with Shi Hui San (ten herbs, generalized bleeding from heat) and Xiao Ji Yin Zi (urinary bleeding).

clinical_pearls: Huai Hua (Sophora japonica flower) has a specific affinity for the Large Intestine and cools intestinal heat.
Jing Jie Sui is charred to transform it from a wind-dispersing herb into a hemostatic herb — charred Jing Jie is one of the best herbs for stopping bleeding.
Intestinal wind (chang feng) = bright red blood before the stool; zang du = dark turbid blood after the stool.
Zhi Ke moves qi downward in the intestines — it prevents qi stagnation from the astringent hemostatic herbs.
Commonly used for internal hemorrhoids and ulcerative colitis with bleeding.
contraindications: Rectal bleeding from Spleen yang deficiency (cold type)

indications: Blood lin (xue lin) — hematuria from heat in the lower jiao: blood in the urine, painful urination, urinary urgency and frequency, possible lower abdominal distension, blood that may be fresh red.
mnemonic: BLOODY URINE (xue lin) gets the SMALL THISTLE treatment: XIAO JI cools blood and stops urinary bleeding, SHENG DI HUANG (big 30g dose) cools blood heat, OU JIE and PU HUANG stop bleeding, Ba Zheng San echoes (HUA SHI, MU TONG, DAN ZHU YE, ZHI ZI) clear heat and promote urination, DANG GUI prevents blood stasis, GAN CAO harmonizes.
board_pearl: Xiao Ji Yin Zi is THE formula for blood lin (hematuria from heat) — it combines hemostatic herbs (Xiao Ji, Ou Jie, Pu Huang) with heat-clearing diuretics (similar to Ba Zheng San ingredients) to simultaneously stop bleeding and clear lower jiao heat through urination.
formula_math: Xiao Ji Yin Zi = hemostatic herbs (Xiao Ji, Ou Jie, Pu Huang) + blood-cooling (Sheng Di Huang) + Ba Zheng San-like diuretics (Hua Shi, Mu Tong, Dan Zhu Ye, Zhi Zi) + Dang Gui + Gan Cao.

clinical_pearls: Addresses bleeding in the urinary system specifically — compare Shi Hui San (upper body bleeding) and Huai Hua San (rectal bleeding).
Sheng Di Huang at 30g is a large dose — it strongly cools blood heat, the root cause of the bleeding.
Dang Gui is included to nourish and invigorate blood, preventing the hemostatic herbs from causing stasis.
Ou Jie (lotus root node) specifically stops bleeding while also promoting urination — perfectly suited for urinary bleeding.
Modern applications include urinary tract infections with hematuria, kidney stones with bleeding, and hemorrhagic cystitis.
contraindications: Cloudy urinary dribbling (gao lin) from dampness without heat

indications: Chong and Ren deficiency with bleeding: excessive menstruation, prolonged uterine bleeding, bleeding during pregnancy (threatened miscarriage), postpartum uterine bleeding, abdominal pain during pregnancy. The bleeding is due to deficiency and cold, not heat.
mnemonic: JIAO (gelatin) and AI (mugwort) — the PREGNANCY PROTECTORS: E JIAO nourishes blood and stops bleeding, AI YE warms the uterus and calms the fetus. Si Wu Tang (Dang Gui, Sheng Di, Bai Shao, Chuan Xiong) rebuilds the blood supply. GAN CAO ties it together. WARM + NOURISH + STOP bleeding = safe pregnancy formula.
board_pearl: Jiao Ai Tang is the primary formula for uterine bleeding from Chong/Ren DEFICIENCY — it is essentially Si Wu Tang + E Jiao + Ai Ye + Gan Cao; it nourishes blood and WARMS the uterus to stop bleeding and calm the fetus (threatened miscarriage).
formula_math: Jiao Ai Tang = Si Wu Tang (Dang Gui, Sheng Di Huang, Bai Shao, Chuan Xiong) + E Jiao + Ai Ye + Gan Cao. The Si Wu Tang base nourishes blood; E Jiao and Ai Ye add hemostatic and uterus-warming actions.

clinical_pearls: From the Jin Gui Yao Lue — one of the most important gynecological formulas in Chinese medicine.
E Jiao must be dissolved separately (yang hua) in the strained decoction — it should not be boiled with the other herbs.
Ai Ye (mugwort) warms the uterus and is specific for cold-type uterine bleeding — it is the herb most associated with warming the Chong and Ren.
The formula is essentially Si Wu Tang fortified with E Jiao and Ai Ye — if you know Si Wu Tang, you know most of this formula.
Unlike blood-heat bleeding formulas, this treats DEFICIENCY bleeding — the blood is pale, watery, and the patient is weak.
Commonly used for threatened miscarriage, functional uterine bleeding, and menorrhagia from deficiency.
contraindications: Uterine bleeding from blood heat (excess type)
Bleeding with blood stasis requiring strong invigoration

indications: Spontaneous sweating or night sweats due to deficiency, with palpitations, easily startled, irritability, and shortness of breath.
mnemonic: The OYSTER closes its SHELL (Mu Li) when the YELLOW WIND (Huang Qi + Ma Huang Gen) WAVES WHEAT (Fu Xiao Mai) — an oyster that shuts tight to stop leaking sweat.
board_pearl: Mu Li San is the go-to astringent formula for spontaneous sweating — pairs the astringent Mu Li with the sweat-stopping specialist Ma Huang Gen.
formula_math: Yu Ping Feng San tonifies Qi to stop sweating; Mu Li San astringes to stop sweating — they can be combined.

clinical_pearls: Ma Huang Gen (ephedra root) stops sweating — the opposite of Ma Huang (ephedra stem) which promotes sweating. A classic board distinction.
Fu Xiao Mai (light/floating wheat) inhibits sweating and nourishes the Heart — different from Xiao Mai (wheat) in Gan Mai Da Zao Tang.
Traditionally taken as a powder mixed with warm water.
contraindications: Sweating as a therapeutic strategy during active pathogenic invasion

indications: Daybreak diarrhea (fifth-watch diarrhea / cock-crow diarrhea) due to Kidney and Spleen Yang deficiency, with lack of interest in food, borborygmus, abdominal pain, cold limbs, soreness of the low back.
mnemonic: 4 GODS cure your 5AM runs: BU GU ZHI bones up the Kidneys, NUTMEG (Rou Dou Kou) plugs the gut, EVODIA (Wu Zhu Yu) warms, and SCHISANDRA (Wu Wei Zi) astringes — the cock-crow diarrhea formula.
board_pearl: Si Shen Wan is THE formula for cock-crow (fifth-watch/daybreak) diarrhea — early morning diarrhea from Kidney Yang deficiency failing to warm the Spleen.

clinical_pearls: The hallmark symptom is diarrhea that occurs specifically at daybreak (around 5 AM), when Yang Qi should be rising but Kidney Yang is too weak.
Bu Gu Zhi is the key Jun herb — it tonifies Kidney Yang and binds the Intestines.
Rou Dou Kou (nutmeg) is a key astringent for the Intestines that also warms the Middle Jiao.
Sheng Jiang and Da Zao are used to form pills — the fresh ginger is cooked with the dates.

indications: Spermatorrhea or nocturnal emissions due to Kidney deficiency with an unstable Gate of Essence, with dizziness, tinnitus, fatigue, sore lower back.
mnemonic: LOCK the GOLDEN GATE with a METAL KEY: DRAGON BONES & OYSTER SHELLS (Long Gu, Mu Li) are the lock, LOTUS parts (Lian Xu, Lian Zi) and ASTRAGALUS SEED (Sha Yuan Zi) with FOXNUT (Qian Shi) seal the essence.
board_pearl: Jin Suo Gu Jing Wan is the primary formula for spermatorrhea/nocturnal emissions from Kidney deficiency — the 'Metal Lock' astringes the Gate of Essence.

clinical_pearls: Contains two lotus products: Lian Xu (lotus stamen) and Lian Zi (lotus seed) — both are astringent for the Kidneys.
The heavy minerals Long Gu and Mu Li anchor and astringe — they appear together in many stabilizing formulas.
Sha Yuan Zi (flattened milkvetch seed/astragalus seed) tonifies Kidney Yang and secures essence — not to be confused with Huang Qi.

Emissions due to ministerial fire blazing

actions: Regulates and tonifies the Heart and Kidneys
indications: Heart-Kidney disharmony with urinary frequency, cloudy urine, or enuresis, spermatorrhea, poor memory, absentmindedness, palpitations, and a dull sensation in the head.
mnemonic: The MANTIS EGG-CASE guards against leaking: PRAYING MANTIS (Sang Piao Xiao) holds it together while DRAGON BONES anchor the Heart, YUAN ZHI + CHANG PU open Heart-Kidney communication, and TURTLE SHELL (Gui Ban) nourishes below.
board_pearl: Sang Piao Xiao San treats urinary frequency, enuresis, and cloudy urine from Heart-Kidney disharmony — the mantis egg-case (Sang Piao Xiao) is the signature astringent for Kidney leakage.

Gui Ban nourishes Kidney Yin and anchors Yang — pairs with Long Gu to anchor and stabilize.
This formula uniquely addresses both the Heart (memory, palpitations) and Kidney (urinary, essence) aspects of disharmony.
contraindications: Urinary frequency due to Damp-Heat in the Bladder

indications: Continuous, profuse, thin, white or pale yellow vaginal discharge without odor, due to Spleen deficiency with Dampness, accompanied by fatigue, sallow complexion, loose stools.
mnemonic: To END the (vaginal) DISCHARGE, build a DAM with TWO WHITES (Bai Zhu + Bai Shao) and a MOUNTAIN (Shan Yao), while GINSENG powers the Spleen pump and PLANTAIN SEED (Che Qian Zi) drains the Dampness out the back door.
board_pearl: Wan Dai Tang is THE formula for chronic profuse white vaginal discharge (Dai Xia) from Spleen Qi deficiency with Dampness — heavy Spleen tonification with mild astringency.

clinical_pearls: The large dose of Bai Zhu (30g) is key — it is the primary Spleen Qi tonifier and Dampness dryer in this formula.
Chai Hu and charred Jing Jie are used in tiny amounts to raise the Spleen Qi and hold the discharge up — not to release the exterior.
Cang Zhu pairs with Bai Zhu: one dries Dampness strongly, the other tonifies the Spleen to prevent Dampness from forming.
This is a Fu Qing-Zhu formula from Fu Qing Zhu's Gynecology.
contraindications: Vaginal discharge from Damp-Heat (yellow, thick, odorous)
Vaginal discharge from Kidney deficiency without Spleen involvement

indications: Insomnia and irritability due to Liver Blood deficiency with deficiency heat, with palpitations, night sweats, dizziness, and a dry throat.
mnemonic: SOUR DATE SEED (Suan Zao Ren) puts you to SLEEP — with a PORIA pillow, ANEMARRHENA (Zhi Mu) cools the heat, LIGUSTICUM (Chuan Xiong) moves stagnant blood, and LICORICE sweetens the dream. Insomnia from Liver Blood deficiency.
board_pearl: Suan Zao Ren Tang is the #1 formula for insomnia from Liver Blood deficiency — Suan Zao Ren in large dose nourishes Liver Blood and calms the Hun.
formula_math: Only 5 herbs. Compare with Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan (insomnia from Heart/Kidney Yin deficiency) — different mechanism.

Zhi Mu clears deficiency heat that disturbs sleep — the irritability and night sweats component.
The Liver stores Blood and houses the Hun — when Liver Blood is deficient, the Hun wanders and sleep is disturbed.
Zhang Zhong-Jing formula from the Jin Gui Yao Lue.

english_name: Emperor of Heaven's Special Pill to Tonify the Heart
indications: Yin deficiency of the Heart and Kidneys with deficiency fire flaring upward, causing irritability, insomnia, palpitations with anxiety, fatigue, poor memory, nocturnal emissions, dry stools, mouth and tongue sores, low-grade fever.
mnemonic: The HEAVENLY EMPEROR needs 13 ministers to calm his HEART: 3 YIN nourishers (Sheng Di, Tian Men Dong, Mai Men Dong), 3 SPIRIT calmers (Suan Zao Ren, Bai Zi Ren, Yuan Zhi), 2 BLOOD movers (Dang Gui, Dan Shen), and GINSENG for Qi — all for Heart-Kidney Yin deficiency insomnia.
board_pearl: Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan treats insomnia from Heart AND Kidney Yin deficiency with deficiency fire — large-dose Sheng Di Huang is the Jun herb (not a Heart herb, but a Kidney Yin herb highlighting the Heart-Kidney axis).

comparison_formulas: Suan Zao Ren Tang — Liver Blood deficiency insomnia; Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan for Heart-Kidney Yin deficiency insomnia
Liu Wei Di Huang Wan — nourishes Kidney Yin broadly; Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan specifically targets Heart-Kidney axis
Zhu Sha An Shen Wan — sedates Heart fire directly; Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan nourishes Yin to address root
clinical_pearls: Sheng Di Huang as Jun in the largest dose emphasizes that this is fundamentally a Kidney Yin deficiency pattern affecting the Heart.
Jie Geng as Shi directs herbs upward to the Heart (acting as a guide/envoy).
Contains 13 herbs — one of the larger classical formulas — reflecting the complexity of Heart-Kidney disharmony.
Traditionally formed into pills using honey.
contraindications: Insomnia from phlegm, food stagnation, or Liver Qi stagnation
Spleen deficiency with loose stools (heavy Yin-nourishing herbs may worsen)

indications: Zang Zao (Visceral Agitation / hysteria): disorientation, frequent attacks of crying spells, inability to control oneself, frequent yawning, restless sleep — often in women. The spirit is unsettled by Heart Yin and Qi deficiency.
mnemonic: A SWEET CALM TEA of just 3 pantry items — LICORICE, WHEAT, and DATES — soothes the hysterical woman who can't stop crying. The sweetest, simplest formula for Zang Zao.
board_pearl: Gan Mai Da Zao Tang is THE formula for Zang Zao (visceral agitation / hysteria) — uncontrollable crying and emotional lability. Only 3 sweet herbs.
formula_math: Standalone 3-herb formula. The simplest Calm Spirit formula.

key_modifications.modification: Add Suan Zao Ren and Bai Zi Ren
key_modifications.for: Concurrent insomnia

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai He and Sheng Di Huang
key_modifications.for: Bai He Bing (lily disease) overlap with emotional restlessness

key_modifications.modification: Add Long Gu and Mu Li
key_modifications.for: Pronounced anxiety and palpitations

comparison_formulas: Suan Zao Ren Tang — insomnia from Liver Blood deficiency; Gan Mai Da Zao Tang for emotional lability/crying from Heart Qi and Yin deficiency
Ban Xia Hou Po Tang — plum-pit Qi from Liver Qi stagnation; Gan Mai Da Zao Tang for Zang Zao from deficiency
clinical_pearls: Zang Zao literally means 'visceral agitation' — classically described as a condition in women with emotional instability.
Note: Xiao Mai (wheat grain) is used here — NOT Fu Xiao Mai (light wheat, which is for sweating). A key distinction for exams.
All three herbs are sweet and nourishing — the formula works entirely through gentle tonification.
Zhang Zhong-Jing formula from Jin Gui Yao Lue.
contraindications: Emotional disturbance from excess patterns (Liver fire, phlegm-fire)
Use cautiously in Dampness or phlegm conditions due to sweet, cloying nature

indications: Liver Yang rising with internal wind generating headache, dizziness, vertigo, blurred vision, tinnitus, insomnia, tremors, possible hypertension.
mnemonic: HEAVEN'S HEMP (Tian Ma) and HOOK VINE (Gou Teng) are the dynamic duo that CALM LIVER WIND — backed by ABALONE SHELL (Shi Jue Ming) to anchor Yang, gardenia + scutellaria to clear heat, and Niu Xi to drag it all downward. The hypertension formula.
board_pearl: Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin is the primary formula for Liver Yang rising with early-stage internal wind — think hypertension with headache, dizziness, and a wiry rapid pulse.
formula_math: Compared to Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang: Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin is for Liver Yang rising (earlier/milder stage); Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang is for extreme heat generating severe wind (later/more critical).

key_modifications.modification: Add Long Gu and Mu Li
key_modifications.for: More severe Yang rising with strong pulsation

key_modifications.modification: Add Xia Ku Cao and Ju Hua
key_modifications.for: Pronounced headache and eye symptoms

key_modifications.for: Tremors and spasms from wind

comparison_formulas: Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang — more severe, extreme heat with high fever and convulsions; Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin for Liver Yang rising without extreme heat
Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang — anchors Liver Yang and extinguishes wind with heavy minerals; more for Liver-Kidney Yin deficiency with Yang rising
Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang — Tian Ma for wind-phlegm dizziness (deficiency); Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin for Liver Yang rising dizziness (excess)
clinical_pearls: Gou Teng (Uncaria hooks) should be added near the end of decoction (last 5 minutes) — prolonged boiling destroys its active components.
Chuan Niu Xi directs Blood and herbs downward — crucial for bringing down rising Liver Yang.
Du Zhong and Sang Ji Sheng tonify Liver and Kidney to address the root Yin deficiency beneath the Yang rising.
Ye Jiao Teng (caulis of He Shou Wu) calms the spirit and nourishes the Heart — for the insomnia component.
contraindications: Wind-stroke from Qi and Blood deficiency (Bu Yang Huan Wu Tang territory)

indications: Extreme heat generating internal Liver wind — high fever, irritability, restlessness, dizziness, convulsions, tremors of the extremities, possibly loss of consciousness. Seen in warm-febrile disease when heat enters the Jue Yin (Pericardium/Liver) level.
mnemonic: The ANTELOPE (Ling Yang Jiao) runs with HOOKS (Gou Teng) to catch the raging LIVER WIND FIRE: MULBERRY LEAF + CHRYSANTHEMUM cool it, REHMANNIA + WHITE PEONY nourish Yin, and FRITILLARIA + BAMBOO SHAVINGS clear phlegm-heat. Emergency convulsion formula.
board_pearl: Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang is for extreme heat generating Liver wind with convulsions — the hot, critical stage where Ling Yang Jiao's powerful cooling and wind-extinguishing action is required.
formula_math: More severe than Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin. Contains Sang Ju Yin elements (Sang Ye, Ju Hua) + wind-extinguishing + Yin-nourishing herbs.

key_modifications.modification: Add Xi Jiao (Shui Niu Jiao) and Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: Blood-level heat with delirium

key_modifications.modification: Add Shi Gao and Zhi Mu
key_modifications.for: Pronounced Qi-level heat

key_modifications.modification: Add E Jiao and Ji Zi Huang (egg yolk)
key_modifications.for: Severe Yin deficiency with wind

comparison_formulas: Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin — Liver Yang rising without extreme heat; Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang for extreme heat with convulsions
Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang — chronic Liver Yang rising from Yin deficiency; Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang for acute febrile convulsions
Da Ding Feng Zhu — late-stage wind from Yin/fluid exhaustion; Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang for heat-driven wind
clinical_pearls: Ling Yang Jiao (antelope horn) is decocted separately for 2 hours due to its hardness — or ground into powder and taken directly (1-1.5g).
Due to endangered species concerns, Shan Yang Jiao (goat horn) is commonly substituted at higher doses.
The combination of Sang Ye + Ju Hua comes from Sang Ju Yin — here they cool the Liver and clear wind-heat.
Chuan Bei Mu + Zhu Ru clear phlegm-heat, preventing heat from congealing fluids into phlegm that clouds the orifices.
contraindications: Internal wind from Blood deficiency (use Da Ding Feng Zhu)
Chronic wind from Liver-Kidney Yin deficiency without acute heat

english_name: Sedate the Liver and Extinguish Wind Decoction
indications: Liver and Kidney Yin deficiency with Liver Yang rising and stirring of internal wind — headache, dizziness, vertigo, tinnitus, distended eyes, flushed face, a feeling of Qi rushing upward to the head, progressive motor difficulties, possible wind-stroke (pre-stroke signs).
mnemonic: SEDATE the LIVER with HEAVY ANCHORS: HEMATITE (Dai Zhe Shi) and DRAGON/OYSTER (Long Gu, Mu Li) weigh it down, NIU XI drags everything downward, TURTLE SHELL (Gui Ban) nourishes Yin from below — Zhang Xi-Chun's anti-stroke formula.
board_pearl: Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang uses heavy minerals and shells to anchor Liver Yang — Niu Xi in large dose (30g) as Jun to direct everything downward. Think pre-stroke presentation.
formula_math: Heavy anchoring (Long Gu, Mu Li, Dai Zhe Shi, Gui Ban) + Yin nourishment (Xuan Shen, Tian Men Dong, Bai Shao) + downward directing (Niu Xi).

key_modifications.modification: Add Ling Yang Jiao
key_modifications.for: More severe wind with convulsions

key_modifications.modification: Add Gou Teng and Tian Ma
key_modifications.for: Pronounced dizziness and tremor

key_modifications.for: More severe Yin deficiency

comparison_formulas: Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin — Liver Yang rising with heat; Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang uses heavy anchoring minerals for more severe Yang rising
Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang — acute febrile convulsions; Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang for chronic Yin-deficient Yang rising
Da Ding Feng Zhu — wind from critical Yin/fluid exhaustion; Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang for pre-stroke Yang rising
clinical_pearls: Zhang Xi-Chun (Yi Xue Zhong Zhong Can Xi Lu) formula — his signature approach of using heavy minerals to anchor rising Yang.
Mai Ya (barley sprout) is included to harmonize the Stomach and prevent the heavy minerals from damaging digestion.
Yin Chen Hao clears Liver heat — an unusual inclusion that helps vent constrained Liver Qi.
Chuan Lian Zi drains Liver fire and moves Liver Qi — the bitter, cold Qi-moving component.
The wiry, long, forceful pulse is key — it indicates Liver Yang excess pushing upward.
contraindications: Wind from Blood deficiency without Yang rising
Spleen-Stomach deficiency (heavy minerals can damage digestion)

indications: Critical-stage wind due to collapse of true Yin — aftermath of warm-febrile disease or prolonged illness with severe Yin and fluid exhaustion. Tremors, spasms, twitching, a worn-out appearance, emaciation, a weak pulse that is about to expire.
tongue: Deep red or crimson, dry, shriveled
mnemonic: To ARREST the WIND PEARL of a dying flame: EGG YOLK + DONKEY GLUE (E Jiao) are the emergency Yin rescue, THREE SHELLS (Gui Ban, Bie Jia, Mu Li) anchor the vanishing Yin, REHMANNIA + OPHIOPOGON + PEONY flood Yin back in. Last-resort wind-from-Yin-collapse formula.
board_pearl: Da Ding Feng Zhu treats internal wind from critical Yin/fluid exhaustion — Egg Yolk (Ji Zi Huang) as Jun is the unique identifier of this formula, providing emergency Yin rescue.
formula_math: Contains elements of Fu Mai Tang (Zhi Gan Cao, E Jiao, Mai Men Dong, Ma Ren, Sheng Di) + shell-based anchoring. Essentially a maximum Yin-rescue formula.

key_modifications.modification: Add Ren Shen and Long Gu
key_modifications.for: Qi collapse accompanying Yin collapse

key_modifications.modification: Add Xi Jiao (Shui Niu Jiao)
key_modifications.for: Persistent heat in the Blood level

key_modifications.modification: Add Ling Yang Jiao
key_modifications.for: More severe wind with convulsions

comparison_formulas: Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang — wind from extreme heat (still has heat driving it); Da Ding Feng Zhu for wind from Yin exhaustion (heat may have subsided)
Zhen Gan Xi Feng Tang — Yang rising from Yin deficiency (chronic); Da Ding Feng Zhu for acute Yin collapse crisis
San Jia Fu Mai Tang — milder Yin-nourishing wind formula; Da Ding Feng Zhu is the maximum-strength version
clinical_pearls: This is a Wu Ju-Tong (Wen Bing) formula for the most critical stage of warm-febrile disease.
Ji Zi Huang (egg yolk) is a unique Jun herb — it is thick, rich, and nourishing, representing 'flesh and blood' Yin rescue.
Three shells (Gui Ban, Bie Jia, Mu Li) represent the heaviest Yin-anchoring strategy in TCM formulary.
E Jiao provides Blood-Yin nourishment and is rich and cloying — appropriate for emergency Yin rescue.
The formula name 'Pearl' refers to the egg yolk's pearl-like appearance.
contraindications: Wind from Liver Yang excess with strong pulse
Any pattern where Yin collapse is not the primary mechanism

indications: Wind rash (urticaria), eczema, or other weeping, itchy skin lesions due to wind-heat or wind-dampness invading the skin. Red, itchy eruptions that may weep fluid when scratched.
tongue: Red or pale red, thin yellow or greasy coating
mnemonic: ELIMINATE WIND from itchy skin with 13 herbs: SCHIZONEPETA + SILER (Jing Jie, Fang Feng) chase wind, CICADA SHELL (Chan Tui) vents rash, GYPSUM + ANEMARRHENA cool heat, REHMANNIA + SESAME nourish Blood, and BITTER SOPHORA (Ku Shen) dries the weeping Dampness.
board_pearl: Xiao Feng San is THE formula for wind-rash with itchy, weeping skin lesions (eczema/urticaria) — it simultaneously disperses wind, clears heat, dries Dampness, AND nourishes Blood.
formula_math: Wind-dispersing (Jing Jie, Fang Feng, Niu Bang Zi, Chan Tui) + Heat-clearing (Shi Gao, Zhi Mu, Ku Shen) + Dampness-drying (Cang Zhu, Mu Tong) + Blood-nourishing (Sheng Di, Dang Gui, Hei Zhi Ma).

comparison_formulas: Dang Gui Yin Zi — itchy skin from Blood deficiency and wind-dryness (no weeping); Xiao Feng San for weeping, Damp-type lesions
Xiao Du Yin — skin lesions from toxic heat; Xiao Feng San for wind-predominant itchy lesions
clinical_pearls: Though categorized under Extinguish Wind, this is for external wind affecting the skin — distinct from the internal Liver wind formulas above.
The Blood-nourishing herbs (Sheng Di, Dang Gui, Hei Zhi Ma) treat the saying 'to treat wind, first treat the Blood; when Blood moves, wind is naturally extinguished.'
Chan Tui (cicada moulting) is a key herb for skin eruptions — it vents rash and stops itching.
Ku Shen is intensely bitter and cold — specifically clears Damp-Heat from the skin.
contraindications: Skin conditions from pure Yin deficiency/Blood dryness without Dampness
Use cautiously in Spleen deficiency due to cold, bitter herbs

indications: Damp-phlegm due to Spleen deficiency — cough with copious white sputum that is easy to expectorate, fullness and distension in the chest and diaphragm, nausea, dizziness, palpitations.
mnemonic: The TWO CURED are BAN XIA and CHEN PI — both are 'aged/cured' (Chen) for better effect. Add PORIA to drain and LICORICE to harmonize. The BASE formula for all phlegm.
board_pearl: Er Chen Tang is the foundational base formula for Damp-phlegm — 'Two Cured' refers to Ban Xia and Chen Pi which improve with age. It is the building block for Wen Dan Tang, Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan, and many other phlegm formulas.
formula_math: Er Chen Tang is the BASE. Wen Dan Tang = Er Chen Tang + Zhu Ru + Zhi Shi + (Da Zao). Multiple phlegm formulas are built upon it.

key_modifications.modification: Add Cang Zhu and Hou Po
key_modifications.for: Pronounced Dampness with epigastric fullness (creates Ping Wei San combination)

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhu Ru and Zhi Shi
key_modifications.for: Phlegm-heat with insomnia (creates Wen Dan Tang)

comparison_formulas: Wen Dan Tang — Er Chen Tang + Zhu Ru, Zhi Shi for Gallbladder/Stomach phlegm-heat
Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan — transforms hot phlegm; Er Chen Tang transforms cold/damp phlegm
Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang — thin fluids/phlegm from Yang deficiency; Er Chen Tang for thicker Damp-phlegm
clinical_pearls: The name 'Two Cured' refers to Ban Xia and Chen Pi — both are more effective when aged/cured.
Wu Mei (1 piece) is often overlooked — its sour flavor prevents the drying herbs from scattering Qi too much and helps expectorate phlegm.
Sheng Jiang assists Ban Xia in transforming phlegm and also reduces Ban Xia's toxicity.
This formula is so foundational that understanding it unlocks understanding of most phlegm formulas in TCM.
contraindications: Dry cough from Yin deficiency (Ban Xia and Chen Pi are drying)
Cough with blood-streaked sputum from Lung Yin deficiency

indications: Gallbladder and Stomach disharmony with phlegm-heat — insomnia, nausea, dizziness, anxiety, palpitations, irritability, bitter taste in the mouth, possible seizures. Also for timidity, being easily startled, and strange dreams.
mnemonic: WARM the (timid) GALLBLADDER by clearing its phlegm-heat: take ER CHEN TANG (Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Fu Ling, Gan Cao), add BAMBOO SHAVINGS (Zhu Ru) to cool and BITTER ORANGE (Zhi Shi) to move — for the anxious, nauseous insomniac with a bitter taste.
board_pearl: Wen Dan Tang = Er Chen Tang + Zhu Ru + Zhi Shi — treats Gallbladder/Stomach phlegm-heat causing insomnia, anxiety, bitter taste, and nausea. Despite its name 'Warm,' it actually clears phlegm-heat.
formula_math: Wen Dan Tang = Er Chen Tang (Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Fu Ling, Gan Cao) + Zhu Ru + Zhi Shi + Da Zao. Add Huang Lian = Huang Lian Wen Dan Tang.

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: Creates Huang Lian Wen Dan Tang for more pronounced heat with bitter taste and irritability

key_modifications.modification: Add Suan Zao Ren, Yuan Zhi, Shu Di Huang, Ren Shen, Wu Wei Zi
key_modifications.for: Creates Shi Wei Wen Dan Tang for severe insomnia with Qi/Blood deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Dan Nan Xing and Shi Chang Pu
key_modifications.for: Phlegm misting the orifices with mental confusion

comparison_formulas: Er Chen Tang — base phlegm formula without heat-clearing; Wen Dan Tang adds Zhu Ru for heat and Zhi Shi for Qi movement
Ban Xia Hou Po Tang — phlegm with Qi stagnation (plum-pit Qi); Wen Dan Tang for phlegm-heat with Gallbladder/Stomach symptoms
Suan Zao Ren Tang — insomnia from Liver Blood deficiency; Wen Dan Tang insomnia from phlegm-heat
clinical_pearls: Despite being named 'Warm the Gallbladder,' this formula actually clears phlegm-heat — the name refers to restoring normal Gallbladder function (courage/decision-making).
The Gallbladder governs decision-making in TCM — phlegm-heat in the Gallbladder causes timidity, indecisiveness, and being easily startled.
Zhu Ru is the key addition over Er Chen Tang — it clears heat and stops vomiting, making the formula appropriate for phlegm-heat.
Commonly modified for psychiatric presentations: anxiety disorders, insomnia, bipolar disorder with phlegm-heat pattern.
contraindications: Insomnia from Yin or Blood deficiency without phlegm

english_name: Clear the Qi and Transform Phlegm Pill

composition.herb: Dan Nan Xing (Arisaematis Rhizoma praeparatum cum Felle Bovis)

indications: Phlegm-heat in the Lungs — cough with thick, yellow, difficult-to-expectorate sputum, chest fullness, nausea, focal distension in the chest.
mnemonic: CLEAR the HOT PHLEGM with a double punch: BILE-PROCESSED ARISAEMA (Dan Nan Xing) + BAN XIA transform phlegm, SCUTELLARIA (Huang Qin) + TRICHOSANTHES SEED (Gua Lou Ren) clear heat, BITTER ORANGE (Zhi Shi) + APRICOT SEED (Xing Ren) direct Qi downward. Yellow sputum formula.
board_pearl: Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan is for phlegm-HEAT with thick yellow sputum — Dan Nan Xing (bile-processed arisaema) is the signature herb that clears heat and transforms phlegm simultaneously.
formula_math: Contains Er Chen Tang elements (Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Fu Ling) + heat-clearing phlegm herbs. Complements Er Chen Tang (cold/damp phlegm) as the hot phlegm equivalent.

key_modifications.modification: Add Sang Bai Pi and Di Gu Pi
key_modifications.for: Lung heat with cough and wheezing

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Mu and Shi Gao
key_modifications.for: More pronounced heat signs

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhu Li (bamboo juice) and Jiang Zhi (ginger juice)
key_modifications.for: Phlegm-heat with loss of consciousness

comparison_formulas: Er Chen Tang — cold/damp phlegm; Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan for hot phlegm
Xiao Xian Xiong Tang — phlegm-heat in the chest (focal distension); Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan for phlegm-heat in the Lungs (cough)
Bei Mu Gua Lou San — dry phlegm from Yin deficiency; Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan for substantial hot phlegm
clinical_pearls: Dan Nan Xing is Tian Nan Xing processed with ox bile (Niu Dan) — this transforms its warm nature to cool, making it ideal for phlegm-heat.
The key diagnostic indicator is thick, yellow, sticky sputum that is difficult to expectorate.
Xing Ren directs Lung Qi downward — important because cough results from rebellious Lung Qi.
Formed into pills with ginger juice — the ginger helps the phlegm-transforming action.
contraindications: Cough from cold-phlegm or damp-phlegm (use Er Chen Tang)
Dry cough from Lung Yin deficiency

pinyin: Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang
indications: Wind-phlegm due to Spleen deficiency — dizziness, vertigo, headache with a heavy sensation, chest and epigastric fullness, nausea, vomiting, copious white sputum.
mnemonic: When PHLEGM creates WIND that makes you DIZZY: BAN XIA dries the phlegm, TIAN MA calms the wind, BAI ZHU + FU LING fix the Spleen that made the phlegm. The 'dizziness from phlegm' formula — no excess heat, no Liver Yang.
board_pearl: Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang treats dizziness from wind-phlegm due to Spleen deficiency — contrast with Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin (dizziness from Liver Yang rising). The white greasy tongue coating is the key differentiator.
formula_math: Er Chen Tang (Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Fu Ling, Gan Cao) + Tian Ma + Bai Zhu + Sheng Jiang/Da Zao. The phlegm base meets the wind-extinguishing herb.

key_modifications.modification: Add Dan Nan Xing and Zhu Ru
key_modifications.for: Phlegm-heat with dizziness

key_modifications.modification: Add Dang Shen and Huang Qi
key_modifications.for: Severe Spleen Qi deficiency

key_modifications.modification: Add Ze Xie and Che Qian Zi
key_modifications.for: Concurrent fluid retention and edema

comparison_formulas: Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin — dizziness from Liver Yang rising (red tongue, wiry rapid pulse); Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang from wind-phlegm (white greasy tongue, slippery pulse)
Er Chen Tang — base phlegm formula; Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang adds wind-calming and Spleen-strengthening
Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang — thin fluid retention with dizziness; Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang for thicker phlegm with wind
clinical_pearls: Exam favorite: dizziness + white greasy tongue + slippery pulse = Ban Xia Bai Zhu Tian Ma Tang. Dizziness + red tongue + wiry rapid pulse = Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin.
Tian Ma is the key wind-extinguishing herb — but here wind is generated by phlegm, not by Liver Yang rising.
Li Dong-Yuan formula from Pi Wei Lun — reflects his emphasis on the Spleen as the root cause.
Bai Zhu in larger dose (15g) emphasizes Spleen tonification to address the root cause of phlegm production.
contraindications: Dizziness from Liver Yang rising with heat signs

english_name: Poria, Cinnamon Twig, Atractylodes, and Licorice Decoction
indications: Phlegm/thin mucus (Tan Yin) due to Spleen Yang deficiency — fullness in the chest and hypochondrium, dizziness, palpitations, shortness of breath, cough with thin watery sputum.
mnemonic: 4 herbs to WARM AWAY WATER: PORIA (Fu Ling) drains it, CINNAMON TWIG (Gui Zhi) warms the Yang to move it, WHITE ATRACTYLODES (Bai Zhu) dries it, LICORICE harmonizes. The simplest cold-phlegm/thin-mucus formula.
board_pearl: Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang treats Tan Yin (thin mucus/fluid retention) from Spleen Yang deficiency — only 4 herbs. Gui Zhi warms Yang to transform the pathological fluids.
formula_math: Standalone 4-herb formula. Compare: Er Chen Tang for thicker Damp-phlegm; Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang for thinner fluid/mucus retention (Tan Yin).

key_modifications.modification: Add Ze Xie and Zhu Ling
key_modifications.for: More severe fluid retention with edema

key_modifications.modification: Add Ban Xia and Chen Pi
key_modifications.for: More substantial phlegm (approaching Er Chen Tang combination)

key_modifications.modification: Add Gan Jiang and Xi Xin
key_modifications.for: Cold fluid retention in the Lungs (Ling Gan Wu Wei Jiang Xin Tang direction)

comparison_formulas: Er Chen Tang — thicker Damp-phlegm (greasy coating); Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang for thin fluid retention (slippery coating)
Wu Ling San — fluid retention with urinary difficulty; Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang for fluid retention with dizziness/palpitations
Zhen Wu Tang — Kidney Yang deficiency fluid retention (more severe); Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang for Spleen Yang deficiency fluid retention (milder)
clinical_pearls: Zhang Zhong-Jing formula from Jin Gui Yao Lue — his approach to Tan Yin (thin mucus) disease.
The principle: 'For Tan Yin disease, use warm herbs to harmonize it' (Bing Tan Yin Zhe, Dang Yi Wen Yao He Zhi).
Gui Zhi here is not for releasing the exterior — it warms the Yang of the chest (Heart/Lung) to transform accumulated fluids.
Palpitations in this formula are from fluid retention pressing on the Heart, not from Blood deficiency.

english_name: Three-Seed Decoction to Nourish One's Parents
indications: Phlegm obstruction with food stagnation in elderly patients — cough, wheezing, copious sputum, chest fullness, poor appetite, indigestion.
mnemonic: 3 SEEDS to NOURISH your PARENTS (elderly): PERILLA SEED (Zi Su Zi) directs Qi down, MUSTARD SEED (Bai Jie Zi) expels phlegm from the flanks, RADISH SEED (Lai Fu Zi) reduces food stagnation. Three little seeds for old folks with phlegm and poor digestion.
board_pearl: San Zi Yang Qin Tang — 3 seeds for elderly patients with phlegm + food stagnation + wheezing. The name 'Nourish One's Parents' tells you the target demographic: the elderly.
formula_math: Only 3 herbs (all seeds). Simple, gentle formula for elderly patients who cannot tolerate strong medicines.

key_modifications.modification: Increase Zi Su Zi
key_modifications.for: Pronounced cough and wheezing

key_modifications.modification: Increase Bai Jie Zi
key_modifications.for: More phlegm, especially in the flanks/subcutaneous

key_modifications.modification: Increase Lai Fu Zi
key_modifications.for: More food stagnation with belching and distension

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Er Chen Tang
key_modifications.for: More substantial Damp-phlegm

comparison_formulas: Er Chen Tang — base Damp-phlegm formula; San Zi Yang Qin Tang is gentler and adds food stagnation treatment
Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang — Qi and phlegm rebellious with Kidney deficiency; San Zi Yang Qin Tang is simpler for elderly phlegm + food stagnation
Bao He Wan — primarily food stagnation; San Zi Yang Qin Tang for phlegm with secondary food stagnation
clinical_pearls: All three herbs are seeds — seeds are heavy, descending, and gentle. Perfect for elderly patients.
The formula name tells a story: an elderly couple's filial children created this gentle formula to help their aging parents.
Bai Jie Zi (white mustard seed) has a unique ability to search out phlegm in the channels and subcutaneous tissue.
Lai Fu Zi (radish seed) is said to counteract the tonifying effect of Ren Shen — avoid combining them.
contraindications: Wheezing from Kidney not grasping Qi (without phlegm)
Not appropriate as a long-term formula due to its descending, dispersing nature

indications: Cough from external wind that has not fully resolved — persistent cough after a cold, itchy throat, slight chills, thin white sputum. The external pathogen is mostly gone but the cough lingers.
mnemonic: STOP that lingering COUGH after a cold: PLATYCODON (Jie Geng) opens the Lungs up, ASTER (Zi Wan) + STEMONA (Bai Bu) + CYNANCHUM (Bai Qian) are the cough-stopping trio, SCHIZONEPETA (Jing Jie) sweeps out residual wind. The post-cold cough formula.
board_pearl: Zhi Sou San is for a lingering cough after an external wind invasion has mostly resolved — not for acute invasion (use Sang Ju Yin) and not for chronic internal cough.
formula_math: Standalone formula. A transitional formula between exterior-releasing and cough-stopping categories.

key_modifications.modification: Add Qian Hu and Xing Ren
key_modifications.for: More phlegm with wheezing

key_modifications.modification: Add Su Ye and Sheng Jiang
key_modifications.for: More wind-cold signs with chills

key_modifications.modification: Add Sang Ye and Ju Hua
key_modifications.for: Wind-heat residual with sore throat

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Qin and Zhi Mu
key_modifications.for: Cough with heat signs and yellow sputum

comparison_formulas: Sang Ju Yin — acute wind-heat cough (early stage); Zhi Sou San for post-cold lingering cough
Xing Su San — wind-cool-dryness cough; Zhi Sou San for residual wind with cough
Er Chen Tang — Damp-phlegm cough; Zhi Sou San for cough from residual exterior pathogen
clinical_pearls: Cheng Zhong-Ling formula from Yi Xue Xin Wu — designed specifically for the common clinical scenario of persistent cough after a cold.
The four cough-stopping herbs (Jie Geng, Zi Wan, Bai Bu, Bai Qian) are balanced between ascending (Jie Geng) and descending (Bai Qian) actions.
Jing Jie dispels residual wind gently — it is not as strong as Ma Huang, making it safe for a partially-resolved exterior.
Very commonly used in modern clinical practice for post-infectious cough.
contraindications: Acute exterior invasion with strong chills and fever (use release exterior formulas)
Cough from Liver fire invading the Lungs

english_name: Minor Sinking into the Chest Decoction
indications: Phlegm-heat clumping in the chest — focal distension and fullness in the epigastrium and chest that is painful on pressure, constipation, or cough with yellow thick sputum. A 'Minor' chest bind pattern (Xiao Jie Xiong).
mnemonic: 3 herbs SINK INTO the CHEST to break up the phlegm-heat knot: COPTIS (Huang Lian) clears the heat, BAN XIA dissolves the phlegm, TRICHOSANTHES (Gua Lou) opens the chest. Focal distension that hurts on pressure.
board_pearl: Xiao Xian Xiong Tang treats the Minor Chest Bind pattern (Xiao Jie Xiong) — phlegm-heat focal distension in the epigastrium that is painful on palpation. Only 3 herbs: Huang Lian + Ban Xia + Gua Lou.
formula_math: Only 3 herbs. From the Shang Han Lun. Compare with Da Xian Xiong Tang (major chest bind with Da Huang, Mang Xiao, Gan Sui — much more severe).

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Shi and Hou Po
key_modifications.for: More pronounced Qi stagnation with fullness

key_modifications.modification: Add Dan Nan Xing
key_modifications.for: More stubborn phlegm

key_modifications.modification: Add Chai Hu and Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: Concurrent Shao Yang symptoms

comparison_formulas: Da Xian Xiong Tang — Major Chest Bind with severe pain, hard distension, constipation (much more severe pattern)
Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan — phlegm-heat in the Lungs (cough focus); Xiao Xian Xiong Tang for phlegm-heat clumped in the chest/epigastrium
Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang — epigastric focal distension from mixed cold-heat; Xiao Xian Xiong Tang from phlegm-heat
clinical_pearls: Zhang Zhong-Jing formula from Shang Han Lun — the Minor Chest Bind pattern.
Key diagnostic point: the epigastric area is distended and painful ON PRESSURE (An Zhi Ze Tong). This distinguishes it from the Major Chest Bind (painful without touching).
Huang Lian + Ban Xia is a classic pair — cold + warm, clear heat + transform phlegm simultaneously.
Gua Lou (whole fruit) opens the chest and transforms phlegm — it includes both the peel (Gua Lou Pi) and seed (Gua Lou Ren) actions.
contraindications: Major Chest Bind pattern (Da Jie Xiong) — too mild for that
Epigastric fullness from Qi stagnation without phlegm-heat

indications: Dry-phlegm from Yin deficiency or dryness damaging the Lungs — cough with scant sputum that is difficult to expectorate, dry throat, wheezing. The phlegm is sticky and hard to bring up.
tongue: Red, dry, thin or scanty coating
mnemonic: FRITILLARIA (Bei Mu) meets TRICHOSANTHES (Gua Lou) — two moistening phlegm transformers for DRY LUNGS: TIAN HUA FEN generates fluids, PORIA prevents Dampness, JU HONG moves Qi, PLATYCODON opens the Lungs. The 'dry cough with sticky phlegm' formula.
board_pearl: Bei Mu Gua Lou San treats dry-phlegm — scant, sticky sputum that is hard to expectorate due to Lung dryness. It moistens rather than dries, making it opposite to Er Chen Tang in approach.
formula_math: Moistening approach to phlegm. Contrast with Er Chen Tang (drying approach). Bei Mu + Gua Lou is the core pair for dry-phlegm.

key_modifications.modification: Add Mai Men Dong and Sha Shen
key_modifications.for: More pronounced Yin deficiency with dry throat

key_modifications.modification: Add Xing Ren and Su Zi
key_modifications.for: More cough with wheezing

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Mu and Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: Concurrent heat signs

comparison_formulas: Er Chen Tang — dries Damp-phlegm (copious white sputum); Bei Mu Gua Lou San moistens dry-phlegm (scant sticky sputum)
Qing Qi Hua Tan Wan — clears hot thick phlegm (yellow); Bei Mu Gua Lou San for dry phlegm (sticky, hard to expectorate)
Sha Shen Mai Dong Tang — nourishes Lung Yin broadly; Bei Mu Gua Lou San specifically addresses dry-phlegm
clinical_pearls: Bei Mu here is typically Chuan Bei Mu (Sichuan fritillaria) which is cooler and more moistening than Zhe Bei Mu (Zhejiang fritillaria).
Tian Hua Fen (trichosanthes root) generates fluids — note it comes from the same plant as Gua Lou (trichosanthes fruit).
Ju Hong (red tangerine peel) is the inner red part of Chen Pi — it moves Qi without being as drying.
Key distinction: if sputum is copious and easy to spit → Er Chen Tang. If sputum is scant and hard to bring up → Bei Mu Gua Lou San.
contraindications: Cough with copious white watery sputum (Damp-phlegm — use Er Chen Tang)

indications: Food stagnation — epigastric and abdominal distension and fullness, acid regurgitation, belching, nausea, vomiting, aversion to food, possible diarrhea with foul smell.
mnemonic: PRESERVE HARMONY after overeating: HAWTHORN (Shan Zha) digests meat, SHEN QU digests grains/alcohol, RADISH SEED (Lai Fu Zi) digests starch — plus BAN XIA + CHEN PI for the nausea and FORSYTHIA (Lian Qiao) for the heat that food stagnation generates.
board_pearl: Bao He Wan is THE food stagnation formula — Shan Zha (hawthorn) digests meat/greasy food, Shen Qu digests grain/alcohol, Lai Fu Zi digests starchy food. Lian Qiao is the surprising addition that clears heat generated by stagnation.
formula_math: Bao He Wan is for mild-moderate food stagnation. Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan = Bao He Wan concept + purgation for more severe food stagnation with constipation.

key_modifications.modification: Add Huang Lian and Huang Qin
key_modifications.for: More pronounced heat from food stagnation

key_modifications.modification: Add Zhi Shi and Bing Lang
key_modifications.for: Severe distension and constipation

key_modifications.for: Qi stagnation with bloating

key_modifications.modification: Add Mai Ya and Gu Ya
key_modifications.for: Starchy food stagnation

comparison_formulas: Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan — food stagnation with Damp-Heat and constipation (adds purgation); Bao He Wan for simpler food stagnation
Jian Pi Wan — food stagnation with Spleen deficiency (adds tonification); Bao He Wan for pure excess food stagnation
Mu Xiang Bing Lang Wan — severe food stagnation with Qi/Blood stagnation
clinical_pearls: Each of the three digestive herbs has a specialty: Shan Zha for meat/fat, Shen Qu for grain/alcohol, Lai Fu Zi for starch/root vegetables.
Lian Qiao is the unexpected herb — it clears heat that food stagnation generates (food fermenting creates heat). This is a frequently tested point.
Ban Xia + Chen Pi together direct rebellious Stomach Qi downward, addressing nausea and vomiting.
Zhu Dan-Xi formula — very commonly used in modern clinical practice for indigestion.
contraindications: Spleen Qi deficiency without food stagnation
Chronic poor appetite from Qi deficiency (needs tonification, not reduction)

english_name: Unripe Bitter Orange Pill to Guide Out Stagnation
indications: Food stagnation with Damp-Heat — epigastric and abdominal distension and fullness, constipation or diarrhea with foul smell, tenesmus, dark scanty urine, abdominal pain that resists pressure.
mnemonic: BITTER ORANGE (Zhi Shi) GUIDES OUT the stuck food: DA HUANG purges it down, SHEN QU digests it, COPTIS + SCUTELLARIA clear the Damp-Heat, BAI ZHU + FU LING + ZE XIE drain the Dampness. When Bao He Wan isn't strong enough.
board_pearl: Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan is for food stagnation with Damp-Heat and constipation — it adds Da Huang (purgation) and Huang Lian/Huang Qin (heat-clearing) to the food stagnation approach. Stronger than Bao He Wan.
formula_math: Bao He Wan approach + Da Huang (purgation) + Huang Lian/Huang Qin (Damp-Heat clearing) + diuretics (Fu Ling, Ze Xie). Eliminates from both below (purge) and through urination.

key_modifications.modification: Add Mu Xiang and Bing Lang
key_modifications.for: More Qi stagnation with severe distension

key_modifications.modification: Add Shan Zha
key_modifications.for: Meat and greasy food stagnation

key_modifications.for: Milder stagnation or weaker patients

comparison_formulas: Bao He Wan — mild food stagnation without purgation needed; Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan for food stagnation with Damp-Heat needing purgation
Da Cheng Qi Tang — purges heat accumulation in the Yang Ming; Zhi Shi Dao Zhi Wan purges food stagnation with Damp-Heat
Mu Xiang Bing Lang Wan — most severe food stagnation with Qi/Blood stagnation
clinical_pearls: Li Dong-Yuan formula — he emphasized that food stagnation can generate Damp-Heat that must be cleared simultaneously.
Da Huang in large dose (30g) is the Jun purging herb — it is the primary difference from Bao He Wan's gentler approach.
Bai Zhu protects the Spleen during the purgation — preventing damage to the Zheng Qi.
Ze Xie + Fu Ling provide a urinary route for eliminating Damp-Heat — the formula attacks from two elimination routes.
contraindications: Spleen Qi deficiency without food stagnation
Weak, elderly patients (modify dose of Da Huang)

english_name: Calm the Palace Pill with Cattle Gallstone

composition.herb: Xi Jiao (Rhinocerotis Cornu) [substituted with Shui Niu Jiao]

indications: Heat entering the Pericardium with loss of consciousness — high fever, irritability progressing to stupor/coma, delirium, convulsions in warm-febrile disease. Hot-type closed disorder (Re Bi). Also for wind-stroke from phlegm-heat.
tongue: Red or crimson, dry, thick yellow or burnt coating
mnemonic: The EMPEROR's PALACE (Pericardium) is on FIRE — send in the OX GALLSTONE (Niu Huang) with MUSK (She Xiang) to blast open the doors, RHINO HORN (Xi Jiao) to cool the Blood, triple HUANG (Lian, Qin, Zhi Zi) to clear the flames, and CINNABAR + PEARL to settle the spirit. The HOT coma formula.
board_pearl: An Gong Niu Huang Wan is the #1 formula for HOT-type closed disorder (Re Bi) — high fever with loss of consciousness. It is the COLD formula among the three Open Orifices treasures (An Gong = cold, Zi Xue = cold, Su He Xiang = warm).
formula_math: Three Orifice-Opening Treasures: An Gong Niu Huang Wan (strongest for heat), Zi Xue Dan (heat with convulsions/extinguish wind), Su He Xiang Wan (COLD-type closed disorder). An Gong and Zi Xue are both cold; Su He Xiang is warm.

key_modifications.modification: Dissolve in Zhi Bao Dan liquid
key_modifications.for: Concurrent phlegm obstruction

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Zi Xue Dan
key_modifications.for: High fever with pronounced convulsions

key_modifications.modification: Administer via nasogastric tube
key_modifications.for: Complete coma where patient cannot swallow

comparison_formulas: Zi Xue Dan — also clears heat and opens orifices but better for high fever with convulsions (stronger wind-extinguishing); An Gong is broader for heat-coma
Su He Xiang Wan — opens orifices for COLD-type closed disorder (pale face, cold limbs); An Gong for HOT-type
Zhi Bao Dan — opens orifices and transforms phlegm; considered milder than An Gong
clinical_pearls: One of the 'Three Treasures' (San Bao) of emergency medicine in TCM — An Gong Niu Huang Wan, Zi Xue Dan, and Su He Xiang Wan.
She Xiang (musk) is the premier orifice-opening substance — its powerful aromatic nature penetrates to the Heart/Pericardium.
Xi Jiao (rhinoceros horn) is banned — Shui Niu Jiao (water buffalo horn) is the standard substitute at 10x the dose.
This is strictly an emergency formula — discontinue once consciousness returns. Prolonged use of aromatic orifice-openers disperses Zheng Qi.
Wu Ju-Tong formula from Wen Bing Tiao Bian.
contraindications: Cold-type closed disorder (Han Bi) — use Su He Xiang Wan instead
Collapsed/abandoned disorder (Tuo Zheng) — orifice-openers will further scatter Qi

composition.herb: Xi Jiao (Rhinocerotis Cornu) [substituted with Shui Niu Jiao]

indications: Heat entering the Pericardium with convulsions — high fever, loss of consciousness, convulsions, delirium, dark urine, irritability, restlessness. Similar to An Gong Niu Huang Wan but with more emphasis on convulsions and wind.
mnemonic: PURPLE SNOW falls to cool the BURNING PALACE: massive mineral cooling (GYPSUM, TALC, CALCITE), ANTELOPE HORN (Ling Yang Jiao) stops convulsions, RHINO HORN cools Blood, MUSK opens orifices, and MIRABILITE purges heat. The convulsion-focused HOT coma formula.
board_pearl: Zi Xue Dan is for HOT closed disorder with pronounced CONVULSIONS — compared to An Gong Niu Huang Wan, Zi Xue Dan has stronger wind-extinguishing (Ling Yang Jiao) and convulsion-stopping action.
formula_math: Among the Three Treasures: An Gong = best for clearing heat and opening orifices broadly; Zi Xue Dan = best when convulsions are prominent; Su He Xiang Wan = for COLD closed disorder.

key_modifications.modification: Combine with An Gong Niu Huang Wan
key_modifications.for: Severe heat with both delirium and convulsions

key_modifications.modification: Dissolve in warm water for nasogastric administration
key_modifications.for: Complete loss of consciousness

comparison_formulas: An Gong Niu Huang Wan — HOT closed disorder, strongest heat-clearing; Zi Xue Dan adds wind-extinguishing for convulsions
Su He Xiang Wan — COLD closed disorder (opposite!); Zi Xue Dan for HOT closed disorder
Ling Jiao Gou Teng Tang — also extinguishes wind from heat but for conscious patients; Zi Xue Dan for comatose patients
clinical_pearls: The 'Purple Snow' name comes from the crystallized appearance of the final product after the mineral decoction is refined.
This formula is unique among the Three Treasures for its emphasis on mineral cooling — massive doses of Shi Gao, Han Shui Shi, and Hua Shi.
Ling Yang Jiao makes this formula superior to An Gong for convulsions — its cool, salty nature extinguishes Liver wind.
Gold (Huang Jin) is decocted with the formula and removed — it is believed to anchor the spirit.
Historically a palace medicine — complex and expensive to produce.

composition.herb: Xi Jiao (Rhinocerotis Cornu) [substituted with Shui Niu Jiao]

indications: Cold-type closed disorder (Han Bi) — sudden loss of consciousness with clenched jaw, cold limbs, pale face, copious phlegm. Also for cold-type stroke, abdominal pain from cold, and sudden collapse from cold turbidity obstructing the orifices.
mnemonic: LIQUID STYRAX warms the FROZEN PALACE: a symphony of WARM AROMATICS (Styrax, Musk, Benzoin, Borneol, Frankincense, Sandalwood, Agarwood, Costus, Clove) blast open orifices sealed by COLD — the WARM coma formula for cold, pale, clenched patients.
board_pearl: Su He Xiang Wan is the ONLY warm Open Orifices formula among the Three Treasures — for COLD-type closed disorder (Han Bi) with cold limbs, pale face, clenched jaw. An Gong and Zi Xue are for HOT-type.
formula_math: Three Treasures comparison: An Gong Niu Huang Wan (cold formula for hot Bi), Zi Xue Dan (cold formula for hot Bi + convulsions), Su He Xiang Wan (WARM formula for cold Bi). Su He Xiang Wan is the odd one out.

key_modifications.modification: Combine with Si Ni Tang
key_modifications.for: Yang collapse with extreme cold limbs

key_modifications.modification: Administer with warm ginger decoction
key_modifications.for: Enhanced warming and orifice-opening

comparison_formulas: An Gong Niu Huang Wan — HOT closed disorder; Su He Xiang Wan for COLD closed disorder
Zi Xue Dan — HOT closed disorder with convulsions; Su He Xiang Wan for COLD closed disorder
Si Ni Tang — warms Yang for collapse but does not open orifices; Su He Xiang Wan opens orifices AND warms
clinical_pearls: The critical exam distinction: COLD limbs + PALE face + clenched jaw + loss of consciousness = Su He Xiang Wan. HIGH FEVER + RED face + loss of consciousness = An Gong Niu Huang Wan.
The formula is dominated by warm, aromatic, Qi-moving substances — nearly every herb is aromatic.
Su He Xiang (liquid styrax) is a warm, aromatic resin that opens orifices — it is the signature ingredient differentiating this from the cold formulas.
Emergency formula only — aromatic orifice-openers scatter Qi and should be discontinued once consciousness returns.
Must distinguish closed disorder (Bi Zheng) from collapsed disorder (Tuo Zheng) — orifice-openers are CONTRAINDICATED in collapse.
contraindications: Hot-type closed disorder (Re Bi) — use An Gong Niu Huang Wan
Collapsed/abandoned disorder (Tuo Zheng) — aromatic dispersal will worsen Qi collapse

indications: Roundworm (Hui Jue) syndrome — intermittent abdominal pain and cramps, vomiting (possibly vomiting roundworms), cold hands and feet, irritability. The classic presentation is alternating heat above and cold below. Also used for chronic diarrhea/dysentery with mixed cold-heat patterns.
tongue: May show a yellow coating at root and white coating at tip
mnemonic: The DARK PLUM (Wu Mei) makes worms pucker in disgust: sour Wu Mei stuns them, HOT herbs (Fu Zi, Gui Zhi, Gan Jiang, Xi Xin, Chuan Jiao) smoke them out, COLD herbs (Huang Lian, Huang Bai) cool the heat above, GINSENG + DANG GUI sustain the patient. The Jue Yin formula for worms AND cold-heat complex.
board_pearl: Wu Mei Wan is THE formula for roundworm (Hui) syndrome with the classic presentation of cold extremities + vomiting + intermittent abdominal pain. It uniquely combines hot AND cold herbs to address the Jue Yin mixed cold-heat pattern.
formula_math: Contains both hot herbs (Fu Zi, Gui Zhi, Gan Jiang, Xi Xin, Chuan Jiao — 5 hot) and cold herbs (Huang Lian, Huang Bai — 2 cold) = Jue Yin mixed cold-heat formula. Not purely antiparasitic.

key_modifications.modification: Add Bing Lang and Shi Jun Zi
key_modifications.for: Stronger antiparasitic action

key_modifications.modification: Reduce hot herbs, increase Huang Lian
key_modifications.for: More heat signs than cold

key_modifications.modification: Add Bai Shao and Mu Xiang
key_modifications.for: More pronounced abdominal pain and cramping

key_modifications.modification: Used without modification for chronic diarrhea/dysentery
key_modifications.for: Mixed cold-heat pattern chronic diarrhea (Jue Yin level)

comparison_formulas: Hua Chong Wan — purely antiparasitic formula; Wu Mei Wan treats the whole Jue Yin cold-heat pattern
Wu Zhu Yu Tang — Jue Yin headache with vomiting from cold; Wu Mei Wan for Jue Yin with mixed cold-heat and worms
Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang — also treats mixed cold-heat but in the Stomach/Intestines; Wu Mei Wan is specifically Jue Yin level
clinical_pearls: Zhang Zhong-Jing formula from the Shang Han Lun — the representative formula of the Jue Yin chapter.
Wu Mei (mume/dark plum) is extremely sour — roundworms are said to be 'calmed' by sour flavor, 'driven out' by bitter and acrid, and 'paralyzed' by pungent. This formula has all three flavors.
The 5 hot + 2 cold herb structure reflects the Jue Yin pattern: Yang deficiency below (cold limbs, diarrhea) with heat harassing above (irritability, vomiting).
Modern clinical use extends well beyond parasites — it is widely used for chronic colitis, IBS, and other conditions with mixed cold-heat presentation.
Chuan Jiao (Sichuan pepper) is both acrid/hot AND specifically toxic to parasites.
The pills are traditionally formed using rice and honey.
contraindications: Acute febrile disease with purely excess heat
Pure cold pattern without mixed heat component
Acute parasitic obstruction of the bile duct (surgical emergency)
